Phase 3: Enhancement (Week 7-8) 9. Add progress tracking 10. Integrate Discord community 11. Create student submission gallery 12. Add "Knowledge Check" questions to all lessons

Phase 4: Polish (Week 9-10) 13. Add time estimates 14. Create prerequisite links 15. Test mobile responsiveness 16. Get feedback from 3-5 test students

docs.title: Competitive Advantages for Robotics
docs.text:
TOP's approach works for web dev. For robotics, we can go further:

  1. Physical Builds
  2. Show build photos alongside CAD
  3. Include "build logs" from real teams

  4. Video of mechanisms in action

  5. Competition Context

  6. Game-specific examples
  7. Strategy integration

  8. Tournament preparation

  9. Hardware Access

  10. Parts inventory tracking
  11. "Can build with basic kit" labels

  12. Alternative designs for limited budgets

  13. Team Dynamics

  14. Collaboration lessons (not solo)
  15. Role-based tracks (builder, programmer, strategist)

  16. Mentor-specific content

  17. Failure Documentation

  18. "What Went Wrong" case studies
  19. Common mistakes gallery
  20. Debugging checklists

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
The Odin Project's success comes from: - Clarity: Never confusing, always clear next steps - Consistency: Same structure every time - Community: You're not alone - Realism: Honest about difficulty and timelines - Respect: Treats learners as capable humans, not children

For robotics education, these patterns are directly transferable. The key is maintaining the same philosophy: hands-on, project-based, community-supported, and brutally honest about the learning curve.

Screenshots captured: - /Users/malharsoni/CTRC-Learning/docs/foundation/unit1/odin-real-homepage-2026-03-10T22-27-00-632Z.png - /Users/malharsoni/CTRC-Learning/docs/foundation/unit1/odin-paths-page-2026-03-10T22-27-41-640Z.png - /Users/malharsoni/CTRC-Learning/docs/foundation/unit1/odin-foundations-path-2026-03-10T22-27-49-353Z.png - /Users/malharsoni/CTRC-Learning/docs/foundation/unit1/odin-lesson-page-2026-03-10T22-28-00-557Z.png - /Users/malharsoni/CTRC-Learning/docs/foundation/unit1/odin-project-page-2026-03-10T22-28-25-337Z.png - /Users/malharsoni/CTRC-Learning/docs/foundation/unit1/odin-course-structure-2026-03-10T22-28-17-910Z.png - /Users/malharsoni/CTRC-Learning/docs/foundation/unit1/odin-progress-tracking-2026-03-10T22-28-36-484Z.png

Analysis complete. All patterns documented with robotics applications.

docs.title: Challenge: Free Spin Challenge
docs.text: The ultimate test of friction management. This challenge measures one thing: how long your drivetrain wheels spin freely after a single push. Every second of spin time reflects the quality of your bearings, spacing, screw joints, and alignment.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Achieve the longest wheel free-spin time on your completed drivetrain.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
Rule Details Robot Must be your completed drivetrain from Module 1.4 Motors Must be disengaged (remove cartridges or disconnect shafts from wheels) The Push One firm push of the robot across a flat surface (or one firm hand-spin of a wheel) Timing Stopwatch starts when you release the robot/wheel. Stops when all wheels stop spinning. Attempts Best of 3 attempts counts as your score Modifications You may make adjustments between attempts (re-spacing, tightening, etc.)

No External Lubrication

You may not apply oil, grease, WD-40, or any lubricant to your robot. Friction reduction must come from proper building technique only.

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text: Time Rating What It Means 0–2 seconds Needs Work Significant friction problems — likely missing bearings or over-constrained 2–4 seconds Acceptable Basic friction management but room for major improvement 4–6 seconds Good Solid building technique, properly spaced and bearing-supported 6–8 seconds Excellent High-quality assembly with attention to every friction source 8–10 seconds Outstanding Competition-quality friction management 10+ seconds Elite You understand friction at an advanced level

docs.title: How to Win
docs.text:
Friction Reduction Strategies

Bearings everywhere: Every axle must pass through bearings, not bare metal holes. This alone can double your spin time.

Screw joints where possible: If a wheel or roller doesn't need to be on a shared axle, consider mounting it on a shoulder screw (screw joint) for lower friction than an axle-in-bearing setup.

Spacer every gap: Any rotating component touching a stationary surface creates axial friction. Space everything apart. Even a 1/16" spacer between a gear face and a C-Channel wall makes a measurable difference.

Controlled play, not tight packing: Don't crank shaft collars tight against bearings. Leave ~1mm of play so nothing binds.

Check alignment: A bent axle or misaligned bearings create friction that no amount of spacing will fix. Sight down each axle — it should spin true without wobble.

Gear mesh quality: If your gear train is part of the spinning system, gear mesh distance matters enormously. Too tight and teeth bind. Find the sweet spot where gears turn smoothly with minimal backlash.

docs.title: Before Your Attempts
docs.text:
1. Disengage all motors from the drivetrain
  2. Place robot on a clean, flat, hard surface (tile or smooth table)
  3. Spin each wheel individually by hand — identify any that feel rough or sticky
  4. Fix any issues found

📷 Photo Needed

Robot on flat surface, motors disengaged, ready for free spin test

docs.title: During Each Attempt
docs.text:
1. Place robot on the surface
  2. Give one firm, even push (not a slam — consistent force)
  3. Release and start the stopwatch
  4. Watch all wheels — time stops when the last wheel stops
  5. Record the time

🎬 GIF/Video Needed

Proper push technique: one firm push, release, wheels continue spinning

docs.title: Between Attempts
docs.text:
You have 3 minutes between attempts to make adjustments:

  • Re-space components
  • Adjust shaft collar positions
  • Check bearing seating
  • Realign axles
  • Adjust gear mesh distance

Diagnosis Guide

If one wheel stops much earlier than the others: That wheel's axle has a friction problem. Check its bearings, spacing, and any components on that specific axle.

If all wheels stop at roughly the same time (but too quickly): You likely have a systemic issue — bearings missing throughout, or all spacing is too tight.

If wheels spin well individually but stop fast when the robot rolls: Your gear train may be binding, or the frame is flexing under the weight and causing misalignment. Check structural rigidity.

📷 Photo Needed

Common friction sources annotated: gear rubbing, missing bearing, tight shaft collar

docs.title: Bracket Format
docs.text:
After all students complete their 3 attempts, the top scores are ranked:

  1. 1st Place — Longest spin time
  2. 2nd Place — Second longest
  3. 3rd Place — Third longest

Ties are broken by second-best attempt time.

This Challenge Repeats

You can re-attempt this challenge at any point during the Foundation Program as you improve your building skills. Many students achieve dramatically better times after completing Unit 3 (CAD) and Unit 4 (Mechanisms) because they better understand precision assembly.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] Friction management is a measurable, testable skill — not guesswork
  • [x] Bearings, spacers, and screw joints are your primary friction-reduction tools
  • [x] The difference between "acceptable" and "elite" is attention to detail
  • [x] Iteration between attempts teaches you more than any lecture
  • [x] This skill directly translates to competition performance — low-friction robots are faster and more power-efficient

docs.title: Challenge: Obstacle Course Time Trial
docs.text: Drive your creation through fire. This challenge tests everything from Unit 1 — drivetrain quality, gear ratio selection, structural integrity, and driving skill — in a timed obstacle course with head-to-head elimination brackets.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Navigate the obstacle course fastest without assistance. Your robot must complete the full course under driver control. Fastest time wins.

docs.title: Course Layout
docs.text:
The course is designed by your mentor and may be customized, but the standard layout includes these elements:

[START]
  │
  ├── Straight Sprint (6 ft)
  │
  ├── Slalom (weave through 4 cones, spaced 18" apart)
  │
  ├── Tight U-Turn (around a single cone)
  │
  ├── Ramp Up & Down (plywood ramp, ~15° incline)
  │
  ├── Narrow Corridor (robot width + 2" on each side)
  │
  ├── 90° Turns (two sharp right-angle turns)
  │
  └── Sprint to Finish (4 ft straight)
[FINISH]

Course Customization

Your mentor may modify the course based on available space and materials. The elements above are guidelines — the actual course will be announced before the challenge begins. Walk the course before your first attempt.

📐 Diagram Needed

Top-down obstacle course: start line → sprint → slalom cones → U-turn → ramp → corridor → finish

📷 Photo Needed

Full obstacle course set up on floor showing all elements

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
Rule Details Robot Must be your completed drivetrain from Module 1.4 (with electronics and motors) Control Driver control only — one driver, one V5 controller Assistance No human help once the run starts. No pushing, repositioning, or touching the robot. Cone contact Touching a cone adds +2 seconds penalty per cone Knocked-over cone Knocking a cone over adds +5 seconds penalty Ramp failure If the robot cannot climb the ramp, it may bypass with a +10 second penalty

📷 Photo Needed

Plywood ramp obstacle (~15° incline) with robot approaching

📷 Photo Needed

Robot climbing the ramp, testing structural integrity and torque

| Course departure | If the robot leaves the course boundary, the driver must drive it back to where it left — time continues running | | Timing | Starts when the robot crosses the start line. Stops when any part of the robot crosses the finish line. |

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text:
Final Time = Raw Time + Penalties

Penalty Time Added Cone touched (still standing) +2 seconds Cone knocked over +5 seconds Ramp bypassed +10 seconds Did not finish (DNF) No score for that attempt

docs.title: Example Scoring
docs.text:
Driver Raw Time Cones Touched Cones Knocked Ramp Bypass Final Time Team A 28.4s 1 0 No 30.4s Team B 24.1s 0 2 No 34.1s Team C 32.7s 0 0 Yes 42.7s

Clean Is Often Faster Than Fast

A clean run with no penalties frequently beats a reckless fast run. The penalty for a knocked cone (5s) is usually more than the time saved by cutting that corner.

docs.title: Qualifying Round
docs.text: Every team gets 2 timed runs through the course. Your best time (including penalties) is your qualifying score.

docs.title: Bracket Tournament
docs.text:
The top qualifiers are seeded into a single-elimination bracket:

Quarterfinals (if 8+ teams):
  Seed 1 vs Seed 8
  Seed 2 vs Seed 7
  Seed 3 vs Seed 6
  Seed 4 vs Seed 5

Semifinals:
  Winner QF1 vs Winner QF4
  Winner QF2 vs Winner QF3

Finals:
  Winner SF1 vs Winner SF2

In bracket matches, both robots run the course simultaneously (if space allows two parallel courses) or back-to-back. Lower final time advances.

Bracket Sizing

Bracket size depends on the number of teams. With fewer than 8 teams, the bracket starts at semifinals or finals. With 4 or fewer teams, it may be a round-robin format.

docs.title: What This Tests
docs.text: Skill (from Unit 1) How It's Tested Parts knowledge (1.1) Did you select the right wheel type for the course? Structural integrity (1.2) Does the robot hold together through impacts and ramp stress? Gear ratio (1.3) Is your ratio appropriate? Too slow = bad times. Too fast = loss of control. Friction management (1.3) Low-friction drivetrains accelerate faster and maintain speed Build quality (1.4) Straight-rolling robots are easier to drive than crooked ones Electronics mounting (1.4) Loose batteries or brains will shift during ramp climbs

docs.title: Strategy Tips
docs.text:
Gear Ratio Choice Matters Here

  • Speed ratio (3:1 or faster): Great for straights, but hard to control in tight turns and slaloms. Risk of overshooting.
  • Torque ratio (1:3 or lower): Easy to control, but slow on straights. Reliable on the ramp.
  • Balanced ratio (around 1:1.5): Usually the winning choice — fast enough on straights, controllable in turns.

Drive Practice

Before the challenge, spend at least 15 minutes just driving. Practice:

  • Driving in a straight line
  • Making smooth turns (not jerky corrections)
  • Stopping precisely where you want
  • Navigating tight spaces at controlled speed

Most time-trial losses come from poor driving, not poor hardware.

Walk the Course

Before your first run, walk the entire course on foot. Note:

  • Where are the tight spots?
  • Where can you go full speed?
  • Where do you need to slow down?
  • What's the best approach angle for the ramp?

docs.title: Post-Challenge Reflection
docs.text:
After the challenge, answer these questions in your engineering notebook:

  1. What was your qualifying time? Break it down — where did you gain or lose time?
  2. Did your gear ratio help or hurt you? Would you change it for a re-run?
  3. Did anything break or come loose? What does that tell you about your build quality?
  4. What would you do differently if you could rebuild from scratch?
  5. What did the winning robot do differently than yours? What can you learn from it?

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] An obstacle course tests the complete drivetrain system, not just one skill
  • [x] Gear ratio selection is a strategic decision with tradeoffs
  • [x] Driving skill matters as much as build quality — practice driving
  • [x] Clean runs beat fast-but-sloppy runs due to penalty structure
  • [x] Post-challenge reflection is where the real learning happens
  • [x] Head-to-head competition teaches you to perform under pressure

Congratulations on completing Unit 1! You now have a fully built, geared, electronics-mounted drivetrain. Next up: Unit 2: Programming — time to make it move on its own.

docs.title: Module 1.4: Drivetrain Build
docs.text: This is where everything comes together. In this module, you'll design and build a complete competition drivetrain from scratch, applying every skill from Modules 1.1–1.3. By the end, you'll have a rolling, geared, electronics-mounted robot chassis ready for programming.

docs.title: Build Requirements
docs.text:
Your drivetrain must meet all of the following requirements:

Requirement Details Motors Exactly 2x 11W V5 Smart Motors driving the wheels Frame Structurally rigid — no wobble when tested Bracing At least one diagonal brace or boxed rail Wheels 4 or 6 wheels, appropriate type for your design Gear Ratio Must include an external gear ratio (not just motor cartridge) Roll Straight Robot rolls in a straight line when pushed with motors disengaged Electronics V5 Brain, Battery, Radio, and Inertial Sensor mounted and wired Documentation EDP (Engineering Design Process) writeup

No Shortcuts

"Just motor cartridge" does not satisfy the gear ratio requirement. You must have gears meshing externally on the robot. This proves you understand gear ratios in practice, not just in theory.

docs.title: Planning Your Drivetrain
docs.text: Before you pick up a single part, answer these design questions:

docs.title: 1. What wheel type and size?
docs.text: Choice Tradeoff 4" wheels Faster (larger wheel = more distance per rotation), less torque at ground 3.25" wheels Balanced speed and torque 2.75" wheels More torque at ground, slower top speed, lower center of gravity Omni wheels Easy turning, can be pushed sideways Traction wheels Maximum grip, harder to turn (use with omni wheels for hybrid setups)

docs.title: 2. What gear ratio?
docs.text:
Think about your goal:

Goal Ratio Direction Example Fast robot (for racing/time trials) Speed up Green cart + 60t:36t external = 333 RPM Balanced robot (general competition) Slight speed Green cart + 48t:36t external = 267 RPM Pushing robot (defense/heavy loads) Torque up Green cart + 36t:60t external = 120 RPM

docs.title: 3. How many wheels?
docs.text: Configuration Pros Cons 4-wheel Simple, fewer parts, lighter Less traction, can high-center 6-wheel More traction, stable, won't high-center More parts, more friction sources

docs.title: 4. What's your frame geometry?
docs.text:
Sketch your drivetrain on paper before building. Include:

  • Top-down view with dimensions (in holes)
  • Wheel positions
  • Motor positions
  • Gear train layout
  • Brain/Battery mounting location

Sketch First

Spending 15 minutes sketching saves hours of rebuilding. Decide your dimensions, gear placement, and electronics locations before you cut or assemble anything.

📷 Photo Needed

Hand-drawn drivetrain sketch: top-down view with dimensions, wheel/motor/gear positions, electronics location

docs.title: Step 1: Frame Rails
docs.text:
Build two parallel rails that will support the wheels and motors.

  1. Select your C-Channels for the rails (typically 25- or 30-hole long)
  2. Box them if using 2-wide C-Channels (see Module 1.2 boxing technique)
  3. Verify both rails are identical in length and hole pattern

Symmetry Is Everything

Your two rails must be mirror images of each other. If the left rail has a motor at hole 5 and a wheel at hole 20, the right rail must match. Asymmetry causes the robot to drive crooked.

📷 Photo Needed

Two completed parallel boxed C-Channel rails, symmetrical and identical

docs.title: Step 2: Cross Bracing
docs.text:
Connect the two rails with cross members to create a rigid rectangular frame.

  1. Cut cross C-Channels to the desired drivetrain width
  2. Attach at front, rear, and at least one middle position
  3. Use 2+ screws at every joint, spaced apart
  4. Test for wobble — hold one rail and push the other

📷 Photo Needed

Cross members attached between rails at front, rear, and middle with multiple screws per joint

docs.title: Step 3: Wheels and Axles
docs.text:
Mount your wheels on axles supported by bearings in the rails.

  1. Press bearings into every hole where an axle passes through
  2. Slide axles through bearings
  3. Mount wheels with proper spacing (controlled play — ~1mm gap from rail)
  4. Lock axle position with shaft collars

📷 Photo Needed

Wheel mounting: bearings in holes, axle through, wheel with spacers, shaft collars locking

docs.title: Step 4: Gear Train
docs.text:
Install your external gear ratio.

  1. Mount the motor with the driving gear on the motor output shaft
  2. Mount the driven gear on the wheel axle
  3. Ensure gears mesh properly — teeth should interlock smoothly
  4. Test: spin the motor shaft by hand — the wheel should turn with the correct ratio
  5. Check for gear skipping — if teeth skip, adjust the center-to-center distance

📷 Photo Needed

Gear ratio installed: motor driving gear meshing with wheel axle driven gear

Gear Mesh Distance

Gears must be at the correct center-to-center distance. Too close = binding and excessive friction. Too far = teeth skipping under load. The correct distance is when teeth interlock with slight play — you can feel a tiny bit of wiggle but teeth don't disengage.

📐 Diagram Needed

Gear mesh: too close (binding) vs correct (slight play) vs too far (skipping)

docs.title: Step 5: The Roll Test
docs.text:
Before mounting electronics, verify your mechanical build:

  1. Disengage motors (remove cartridges or disconnect motor shafts)
  2. Place robot on a flat surface
  3. Push it forward firmly
  4. It should roll straight for several feet without veering left or right
  5. Wheels should spin freely for 3+ seconds after the push stops

🎬 GIF/Video Needed

Roll test: robot pushed on flat surface, rolls straight with wheels spinning freely

Result Diagnosis Rolls straight, spins long You're good — proceed to electronics Veers left or right One side has more friction — check bearings, spacing, gear mesh Stops quickly High friction everywhere — check all rotating assemblies Makes grinding noise Something is rubbing — find it by spinning each axle individually

docs.title: Step 6: Electronics Mounting
docs.text: Mount all V5 electronics securely to your chassis.

docs.title: V5 Brain
docs.text:
• Mount centrally on top of the chassis for easy access
  • Use the included mounting holes or zip-tie to cross members
  • Screen should face up or toward the driver for visibility
  • Ensure USB port is accessible for programming

📷 Photo Needed

V5 Brain mounted centrally on chassis, screen up, USB accessible

docs.title: V5 Battery
docs.text:
• Mount low and centered for best weight distribution
  • Secure with the battery strap or zip ties — it must not shift during driving
  • Position so the cable reaches the Brain easily
  • Battery should be removable for charging without disassembling the robot

📷 Photo Needed

V5 Battery mounted low and centered, secured with strap, cable to Brain

docs.title: V5 Radio
docs.text:
• Mount on top of the Brain or on a high point of the chassis
  • The radio communicates with the controller — higher is generally better
  • Don't bury it inside the chassis where metal blocks the signal

docs.title: Inertial Sensor (IMU)
docs.text:
• Mount as close to the center of the robot as possible
  • Mount flat (parallel to the ground)
  • Secure rigidly — any vibration or wobble causes drift in readings
  • This sensor is critical for autonomous driving (Unit 2)

📷 Photo Needed

Inertial sensor mounted flat at robot center, rigid and level

Wire Management

Route motor wires and sensor cables neatly. Use zip ties to secure cables to the frame. Loose wires get caught in gears, pinched by moving parts, and disconnected during matches. Clean wiring is not cosmetic — it's functional.

📷 Photo Needed

Good vs bad wiring: messy loose wires vs neatly zip-tied and routed

docs.title: Step 7: Wiring
docs.text:
1. Connect both drive motors to the V5 Brain using smart cables
  2. Connect the Inertial Sensor
  3. Connect the Radio
  4. Plug in the Battery
  5. Power on — verify the Brain detects all connected devices

📐 Diagram Needed

V5 Brain connections: 2 drive motors, inertial sensor, radio, battery — labeled ports

docs.title: Engineering Design Process (EDP) Documentation
docs.text:
Why Documentation?

In VEX competition, the Engineering Notebook is judged. Teams that document their design process can win Design Awards and qualify for championships even without winning matches. Start building this habit now.

docs.title: What to Document
docs.text:
For your drivetrain build, write up the following:

Section Content Problem Statement What are the requirements for the drivetrain? Brainstorm What design options did you consider? (wheel type, ratio, frame) Decision Matrix Why did you choose your final design? What tradeoffs did you make? Build Log Step-by-step notes on what you built, in what order Testing Roll test results, free-spin times, issues discovered Iteration What did you change after testing? What improved?

docs.title: Decision Matrix Example
docs.text:
Criteria (weight) 4-wheel Omni 6-wheel Mixed 4-wheel Traction Turning ease (3) 9 7 4 Pushing power (2) 4 7 8 Build simplicity (2) 8 5 7 Speed (1) 7 6 6 Weighted Total 56 50 47

Take Photos

Photograph your drivetrain at each stage of construction. Before and after fixing problems. Close-ups of gear trains and wiring. These photos are invaluable for your engineering notebook.

docs.title: Final Checklist
docs.text:
Before declaring your drivetrain complete, verify every item:

  • [ ] Frame is rigid — no wobble in any direction
  • [ ] Bracing is present — at least one diagonal or boxed section
  • [ ] All rotating axles use bearings
  • [ ] Controlled play on all wheels and gears (~1mm gap)
  • [ ] External gear ratio is installed and functioning
  • [ ] Robot rolls straight when pushed (motors disengaged)
  • [ ] All wheels spin freely for 3+ seconds
  • [ ] V5 Brain is mounted and accessible
  • [ ] Battery is mounted low, centered, and removable
  • [ ] Radio is mounted and unobstructed
  • [ ] Inertial sensor is centered, flat, and rigid
  • [ ] All wires are routed and zip-tied
  • [ ] Brain powers on and detects all devices
  • [ ] EDP documentation is complete with sketches and photos

📷 Photo Needed

Completed drivetrain from 4 angles: front, side, top, and 3/4 view

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] Plan before you build — sketch dimensions and layouts on paper
  • [x] Symmetry between left and right sides is critical for driving straight
  • [x] The roll test reveals friction problems before you add motors
  • [x] Electronics placement affects balance, signal quality, and sensor accuracy
  • [x] Wire management prevents mechanical failures during competition
  • [x] Document everything — it's a competition requirement, not busywork

Next up: Time to prove your skills. Free Spin Challenge and Obstacle Course Time Trial await.

docs.title: Module 1.3: Gear Ratios & Friction
docs.text: Gear ratios let you customize your robot's speed and torque without changing motors. Friction management determines how much of your motor's power actually reaches the wheels. Master both, and your drivetrain will outperform robots with the same hardware.

docs.title: The Basics
docs.text:
A gear ratio describes the relationship between two meshed gears:

  • Driving gear (input): The gear connected to the motor
  • Driven gear (output): The gear connected to the wheel or mechanism

The ratio is expressed as driving teeth : driven teeth.

Motor → [Driving Gear] → [Driven Gear] → Wheel
         (input)          (output)

📐 Diagram Needed

Driving gear (small, motor) meshing with driven gear (large, wheel) — rotation arrows and labels

docs.title: Calculating the Ratio
docs.text:
$$\text{Gear Ratio} = \frac{\text{Driven Gear Teeth}}{\text{Driving Gear Teeth}}$$

Driving Gear Driven Gear Ratio Effect 12 teeth 36 teeth 1:3 3x torque, 1/3 speed

📷 Photo Needed

12t gear meshing with 36t gear on VEX frame, labeled '1:3 Torque'

| 36 teeth | 12 teeth | 3:1 | 3x speed, 1/3 torque |

📷 Photo Needed

36t gear meshing with 12t gear on VEX frame, labeled '3:1 Speed'

| 24 teeth | 24 teeth | 1:1 | No change — direct drive | | 12 teeth | 60 teeth | 1:5 | 5x torque, 1/5 speed | | 60 teeth | 12 teeth | 5:1 | 5x speed, 1/5 torque |

docs.title: Speed vs. Torque Tradeoff
docs.text:
The Fundamental Rule

You cannot increase both speed and torque. Gears trade one for the other. Increasing torque always decreases speed by the same factor, and vice versa.

You Want You Need Gear Setup Real-World Example More torque (pushing power) Smaller driving → larger driven 12t → 36t (1:3) Climbing a hill in low gear More speed (faster movement) Larger driving → smaller driven 36t → 12t (3:1) Highway cruising in high gear Balanced Equal gears 24t → 24t (1:1) Flat road in mid gear

docs.title: Compound Gear Ratios
docs.text:
You can chain multiple gear pairs to achieve ratios that a single pair can't. The total ratio is the product of each stage:

Stage 1: 12t → 36t = 1:3
Stage 2: 12t → 36t = 1:3

Total: 1:3 × 1:3 = 1:9 (9x torque, 1/9 speed)

📐 Diagram Needed

Two-stage compound gear train: Stage 1 (12t→36t) + Stage 2 (12t→36t) = 1:9 total

Stacking with Motor Cartridges

Remember, the motor cartridge already sets an internal ratio. Your external gear ratio multiplies on top of it.

Example: Green cartridge (200 RPM) + external 1:2 torque ratio = 100 RPM output with 2x torque.

This gives you the same RPM as a red cartridge but with different motor characteristics.

Activity: Build Three Gear Ratios

Time: 25 minutes | Format: Individual

Build three separate gear assemblies (not on a drivetrain — just on a test frame):

  1. 1:1 ratio — same size gears. Spin the input and observe the output speed.
  2. 1:3 ratio — small driving, large driven. Feel the increased torque (resistance to stopping the output).
  3. 5:1 ratio — large driving, small driven. Notice the speed increase but easy-to-stop output.

For each ratio, answer:

  • How fast does the output spin relative to the input?
  • How hard is it to stop the output gear with your finger?
  • When would this ratio be useful on a robot?

docs.title: Friction
docs.text: Friction is the invisible enemy. Every point of contact between moving parts steals energy from your motors. A robot with poor friction management can lose 30–50% of its motor power to heat and wasted energy.

docs.title: Axial Friction
docs.text:
Axial friction occurs when a rotating component is in constant contact with a stationary surface. This is the most common and most damaging type.

Common sources:

Source What's Happening Fix Gear pressed against C-Channel Gear face rubbing on metal wall Add spacer between gear and wall Wheel rubbing on frame Wheel face contacting drivetrain rail Add spacer to create gap Shaft collar grinding on bearing Collar face pressing into bearing Loosen collar, add tiny spacer Stacked gears rubbing each other Adjacent gears' faces in contact Space them apart on the axle

📷 Photo Needed

Gear face pressed against C-Channel wall — the friction problem

📷 Photo Needed

Spacer added between gear face and C-Channel wall — the fix

docs.title: Radial Friction
docs.text: Radial friction occurs when an axle rubs against its mounting hole. This is solved by using bearings (covered in Module 1.1).

docs.title: Controlled Play
docs.text:
What Is Controlled Play?

Controlled play is a small, intentional gap (~1mm) between a rotating component and the surfaces next to it. This gap prevents binding while keeping the component positioned correctly.

The spectrum:

Condition Gap Result Too tight 0mm Binding, friction, motor strain, overheating Controlled play ~1mm Free rotation, minimal wobble, low friction Too loose 3mm+ Excessive wobble, gears skip teeth, misalignment

📐 Diagram Needed

Too tight (binding) vs controlled play (~1mm, correct) vs too loose (wobble)

docs.title: How to Achieve Controlled Play
docs.text:
1. Stack your components on the axle: bearing → spacer → gear → spacer → bearing
  2. Slide the assembly until there's a tiny gap on each side
  3. Lock position with shaft collars on the outside of the bearings
  4. Test: spin the axle — it should rotate freely with no grinding sounds
  5. Check: push the gear side-to-side — it should have barely perceptible movement

📷 Photo Needed

Properly spaced axle: bearing → spacer → gear → spacer → bearing, shaft collars outside

The Tight = Good Myth

Beginners often crank everything down as tight as possible, thinking tight = secure. For structural joints, tight is correct. For rotating assemblies, tight = friction = wasted power. Learn to feel the difference.

docs.title: Screw Joints
docs.text: A screw joint is a technique where a shoulder screw acts as an axle for a rotating component. Instead of using a VEX axle through bearings, you use a shoulder screw through the component's bore.

docs.title: Why Screw Joints?
docs.text: Property Axle in Bearings Screw Joint Friction Low (with bearings) Very low Precision Good Excellent Compactness Requires bearing on both sides Single-sided mounting Best for Drive axles (multiple wheels/gears) Individual pivots, non-motor rotation

docs.title: Building a Screw Joint
docs.text:
1. Take a shoulder screw (the smooth shoulder is the key)
  2. Pass it through the C-Channel hole from outside
  3. Slide your rotating component onto the smooth shoulder
  4. Add spacers as needed for positioning
  5. Thread a nylock nut on the end — tighten until the component spins freely but doesn't wobble excessively

📷 Photo Needed

Screw joint close-up: shoulder screw, spacers, wheel/gear on smooth shoulder, nylock nut — all labeled

📐 Diagram Needed

Cross-section of screw joint showing shoulder section where component rotates freely

The Nylock Sweet Spot

Tighten the nylock nut until it just touches the spacer stack, then back off about 1/8 turn. The component should spin freely. The nylock's nylon insert will hold the nut at this position permanently.

Activity: Screw Joint Free Spin Challenge

Time: 15 minutes | Format: Individual

  1. Build a small frame from C-Channel.
  2. Mount a wheel on a screw joint (shoulder screw through the hub).
  3. Add spacers so the wheel doesn't rub the frame.
  4. Flick the wheel and time how long it spins.
  5. Target: 5+ seconds of free spin.

Compare to Module 1.1 results: Is the screw joint assembly better or worse than your axle-and-bearing assembly? Why?

Typical results:

  • Poorly built axle assembly: 1–3 seconds
  • Well-built axle assembly: 4–6 seconds
  • Well-built screw joint: 6–10+ seconds

docs.title: Applying It All: Drivetrain Friction
docs.text: Your drivetrain is the most friction-critical assembly on the robot. Every wheel, every gear, every axle must spin freely.

docs.title: Drivetrain Friction Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Every axle passes through bearings (not bare metal holes)
  • [ ] Every gear has spacers preventing contact with C-Channel walls
  • [ ] Every wheel has controlled play (~1mm gap, not binding)
  • [ ] Shaft collars are positioned, not over-tightened against bearings
  • [ ] Gears mesh properly — teeth interlock without jamming or skipping
  • [ ] With motors disengaged, wheels spin freely for 3+ seconds from a push

docs.title: The Disengage Test
docs.text:
Disengage Test

Remove the motor cartridge or disconnect the motor from the drivetrain. Push the robot across the floor. It should roll freely for several feet without veering. If it stops quickly, drags to one side, or makes grinding noises, you have a friction problem. Find it and fix it before moving on.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] Gear ratios trade speed for torque — you can't increase both
  • [x] Small driving → large driven = more torque, less speed
  • [x] Large driving → small driven = more speed, less torque
  • [x] Axial friction from components rubbing surfaces is the biggest energy thief
  • [x] Controlled play (~1mm gap) prevents binding without causing wobble
  • [x] Screw joints using shoulder screws provide ultra-low-friction rotation
  • [x] Always test with the disengage test — if it doesn't roll freely, fix it

Next up: Module 1.4: Drivetrain Build — put it all together and build your competition drivetrain.

docs.title: Module 1.1: Safety & Common Parts
docs.text: Before you touch a single piece of metal, you need to know two things: how to stay safe, and what you're working with. This module covers workshop safety, every common VEX V5 part, and the tools you'll use to assemble them.

docs.title: Safety First
docs.text:
Non-Negotiable Safety Rules

These rules are not suggestions. Violating them will get you removed from the workshop.

  1. Common sense is your best tool. If something feels dangerous, stop and ask.
  2. Clean up after yourself. Every session ends with a clean workspace. No exceptions.
  3. Use tools properly. Screwdrivers are not pry bars. Wrenches are not hammers.
  4. Safety glasses are required in designated zones (grinding, cutting, drilling).
  5. No running in the workshop area.
  6. No loose clothing or dangling jewelry near rotating machinery.
  7. Report injuries immediately — even small cuts or pinches.

docs.title: Shop Tour
docs.text:
Before any building begins, your mentor will walk you through the workspace. Pay attention to:

  • Tool stations — where each tool lives and where it goes back
  • Parts bins — organized by type, keep them organized
  • Safety equipment locations — first aid kit, safety glasses, fire extinguisher
  • Restricted zones — areas requiring safety glasses or mentor supervision
  • Charging station — V5 Battery charging protocols

📷 Photo Needed

Workshop layout with labeled stations: tool area, parts bins, safety equipment, charging station

docs.title: V5 Common Tools and Uses
docs.text:
Tool Used For Tips Star Drive Screwdriver (T15) Driving all VEX star-drive screws Don't strip the head — press firmly while turning Combination Wrench (11/32") Tightening keps nuts and nylock nuts Hold the nut, turn the screw (or vice versa) Allen Keys (various) Set screws, shaft collars Keep the full set together — you'll need multiple sizes Needle-Nose Pliers Holding small nuts in tight spaces Not a substitute for a wrench Wire Cutters/Strippers Cutting zip ties, trimming wires Cut away from yourself Ruler / Calipers Measuring spacing, alignment Holes are 0.5" apart — use the hole pattern as your ruler

The Holes = Inches Trick

On VEX metal, every 2 holes = 1 inch. A 25-hole C-Channel is 12.5 inches long. Count holes instead of reaching for a ruler — it's faster and more accurate for VEX builds.

📷 Photo Needed

VEX tools laid out and labeled: T15 star driver, 11/32 wrench, allen keys, pliers, wire cutters

docs.title: Structural Metal: Channels and Beams
docs.text: VEX structural metal comes in several profiles. Each has a specific use case.

docs.title: C-Channels
docs.text:
C-Channels are the backbone of every robot. They are C-shaped in cross-section and come in various widths.

Type Cross Section Width Common Uses 2x C-Channel 2 holes wide Drivetrain rails, mechanism arms 3x C-Channel 3 holes wide Wider structural members, motor mounts 5x C-Channel 5 holes wide Chassis base plates, large structural elements

📷 Photo Needed

2-wide, 3-wide, and 5-wide C-Channels side by side showing cross-section profiles

docs.title: L-Channels
docs.text:
L-shaped metal used for bracing and mounting.

Type Description Common Uses 1x L-Channel Single-row angle bracket Light bracing, small brackets 2x L-Channel Two-row angle bracket Structural bracing, motor mounting

docs.title: U-Channels
docs.text: Type Description Common Uses 2x U-Channel U-shaped, 2 holes wide Enclosing axles, protective housings

docs.title: Flat Beams
docs.text:
Flat strips of metal with mounting holes.

Type Description Common Uses 1x Flat Beam Single-row flat strip Light cross-bracing, linkages 5x Flat Beam Five-row flat plate Base plates, large mounting surfaces

Activity: Part Identification Challenge

Time: 10 minutes | Format: Pairs

  1. One partner closes their eyes (or turns around).
  2. The other partner selects a random piece of structural metal from the parts bin.
  3. The blindfolded partner must identify the part by touch alone — name the type and count the holes to determine its length.
  4. Switch roles after 5 pieces each.

Bonus: Use the holes = inches trick to state the length in inches.

docs.title: Screws
docs.text: VEX uses star drive screws (T15 head). There are two main types, and understanding the difference is critical.

docs.title: Shoulder Screws vs. Standard Screws
docs.text:
Feature Shoulder Screw Standard Screw Shaft Smooth unthreaded shoulder below the head Fully threaded from head to tip Use case When parts need to rotate on the screw (pivot points, screw joints) When parts need to be locked together rigidly Friction Low — smooth shoulder allows rotation High — threads grip the metal Identification Smooth section visible below head Threads run all the way up

📷 Photo Needed

Shoulder screw vs standard screw side by side — arrow showing smooth shoulder vs full threading

Don't Mix Them Up

Using a standard screw where you need rotation will create binding and friction. Using a shoulder screw where you need rigidity will create a wobbly joint. Know which one you're grabbing.

docs.title: Screw Lengths
docs.text:
Screws come in various lengths. The most common:

  • 1/4" — single layer attachments
  • 3/8" — two layers of metal
  • 1/2" — two layers with spacer or standoff
  • 3/4" and 1" — thick stacks, motor mounts, multi-layer assemblies
  • 1.5" and 2" — boxing long assemblies, standoff stacks

docs.title: Nuts
docs.text:
Type Description When to Use Keps Nut Star-washer built into the base — grips and resists loosening General-purpose attachment, most connections Nylock Lock Nut Nylon insert creates friction lock — very secure High-vibration areas, joints that must never loosen

📷 Photo Needed

Keps nut and nylock nut side by side showing star washer vs nylon insert

Wrench Technique

Hold the nut with your wrench and turn the screw with your star driver. For tight spaces, flip it — hold the screw head and turn the nut. Find what works for the situation.

Activity: Screw Comparison Build

Time: 10 minutes | Format: Individual

  1. Take two short C-Channels and attach them at a single point using a standard screw + keps nut. Tighten fully. Try to wiggle the joint.
  2. Now do the same with a shoulder screw + keps nut. Tighten fully. Try to wiggle the joint.
  3. Notice the difference: the shoulder screw joint allows rotation even when tight. The standard screw joint is locked rigid.
  4. Try replacing the keps nut with a nylock nut on each. Feel the extra resistance.

docs.title: Spacers and Washers
docs.text: Spacers and washers are small but critical. They control the position, friction, and stability of every rotating and structural component.

docs.title: Spacers
docs.text:
Spacers are plastic cylinders that slide onto screws or axles to create precise gaps between components.

Size Color (typically) Common Use 1/16" Thin black Fine adjustment 1/8" Black Standard spacing between components 1/4" White/Black Larger gaps, bearing positioning 3/8" White Wide spacing

📷 Photo Needed

All spacer sizes (1/16, 1/8, 1/4, 3/8 inch) lined up with labels

docs.title: Washers
docs.text: Thin metal discs that distribute load and reduce friction between a screw head and the metal surface.

docs.title: Why They Matter
docs.text:
Problem Solution Two rotating parts rubbing together Add a spacer between them A screw head digging into soft material Add a washer under the head An axle wobbling side-to-side Fill the gap with spacers A gear grinding against a C-Channel Space the gear away from the rail

The #1 Beginner Mistake

Beginners skip spacers to "save time." This causes friction, binding, misalignment, and parts wearing out. Every gap needs to be filled intentionally — either with a spacer or with a deliberate clearance.

docs.title: Standoffs
docs.text: Standoffs are threaded aluminum cylinders that connect two parallel surfaces with precise spacing. They screw onto screws from both ends.

docs.title: Common Sizes
docs.text: Length Common Use 1/2" Short connections, bracing 1" Medium structural connections 2" Tall structural supports Custom (7/8") Boxing C-Channels (see below)

docs.title: Boxing with Standoffs
docs.text:
Boxing is the technique of connecting two parallel C-Channels face-to-face with standoffs between them, creating a rigid box beam structure.

The magic number for boxing two 2-wide C-Channels is 7/8" standoffs — this is the exact interior spacing that creates a tight, rigid box.

📷 Photo Needed

Two C-Channels boxed together with standoffs, viewed from end showing box-beam cross-section

📐 Diagram Needed

Cross-section of boxed C-Channel: two channels face-to-face with standoff between, screws labeled

Activity: Build a Boxed C-Channel

Time: 15 minutes | Format: Individual

  1. Take two matching C-Channels (same length).
  2. Using 7/8" standoffs (or combine sizes to get close), connect the two C-Channels face-to-face at both ends and at least one middle point.
  3. Use screws through both C-Channel faces, threading into the standoff.
  4. Test rigidity: try to twist and flex the assembly. Compare to a single C-Channel.

Result: The boxed assembly should be dramatically stiffer than a single C-Channel.

docs.title: Why Boxing Matters
docs.text:
Single C-Channel Boxed C-Channel Flexes under load Resists bending and twisting Can wobble side-to-side Rigid in all directions Lightweight Slightly heavier but much stronger Fine for light mechanisms Required for drivetrain rails and loaded arms

One Long Screw vs. Two Short Screws

When boxing, you can either use one long screw that passes through both C-Channels and the standoff, or two short screws that each thread into opposite ends of the standoff. Two short screws are generally preferred — they're easier to assemble and disassemble.

Activity: Build Your Initials

Time: 20 minutes | Format: Individual

Using C-Channels, standoffs, screws, and nuts, build a 3D structure that forms your initials (first and last name). The structure must:

  • Stand upright on its own
  • Use at least 3 standoffs
  • Include at least one boxed section
  • Be recognizable from 5 feet away

This is a creativity challenge — there's no single right answer. Show off your building skills.

docs.title: Steel vs. Aluminum
docs.text:
VEX structural metal comes in both steel and aluminum.

Property Steel Aluminum Weight Heavy ~1/3 the weight Strength Very strong Strong enough for most applications Flexibility Stiff Slightly more flex Cost Same Same Best for High-load areas (drivetrain rails) Everything else (superstructure, mechanisms)

Competition Strategy

Most competitive robots use steel for the drivetrain (where rigidity matters most) and aluminum for everything above (to save weight). Weight affects acceleration, turning speed, and battery life.

docs.title: Bearings
docs.text: Bearings press into the square holes of C-Channels and provide a smooth, centered bore for axles to spin through.

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
Type Bore Size Used With Low Strength Bearing 1/8" square bore Low-strength (1/8") axles High Strength Bearing 1/4" square bore High-strength (1/4") axles

📷 Photo Needed

Bearing pressed into C-Channel square hole with axle passing through

docs.title: Why Bearings Matter
docs.text:
Without a bearing, an axle spinning inside a square hole has:

  • Poor centering — the round axle rattles in the square hole
  • High friction — metal-on-metal contact
  • Wear — the hole gets enlarged over time

With a bearing:

  • Perfect centering — the bearing bore matches the axle diameter
  • Low friction — smooth plastic-on-metal contact
  • Durability — the bearing takes the wear instead of the metal

📐 Diagram Needed

Axle in bare hole (rattling) vs axle in bearing (centered, smooth) comparison

Always Use Bearings

There is never a reason to skip bearings on rotating axles. Press them into every hole where an axle passes through. It takes 5 seconds and saves hours of debugging friction problems.

docs.title: V5 Smart Motors
docs.text:
VEX V5 has two motor sizes:

Motor Power Default RPM Notes 5.5W Motor 5.5 watts 200 RPM Smaller, lighter, fewer applications 11W Motor 11 watts 200 RPM Standard competition motor, interchangeable cartridges

📷 Photo Needed

5.5W and 11W V5 Smart Motors side by side for size comparison

docs.title: Motor Cartridges (11W Only)
docs.text:
The 11W motor accepts interchangeable gear cartridges that change the output speed and torque:

Cartridge Color Output RPM Torque Best For Red (100 RPM) 100 Highest Heavy lifts, clamps, high-load mechanisms Green (200 RPM) 200 Medium Drivetrains (default), general purpose Blue (600 RPM) 600 Lowest Flywheels, intakes, high-speed mechanisms

📷 Photo Needed

All 3 V5 motor cartridges (red 100RPM, green 200RPM, blue 600RPM) side by side

Cartridge + External Gear Ratio

The cartridge sets the motor's internal gear ratio. You can stack an external gear ratio on top of that for even more customization. For example: green cartridge (200 RPM) with a 2:1 external speed ratio = 400 RPM output.

docs.title: Motor Axles / Shafts
docs.text:
Type Size Square Profile Used With Low Strength Shaft 1/8" Small square Light mechanisms, sensors High Strength Shaft 1/4" Large square Drivetrains, loaded mechanisms, motor outputs

Shafts have a square cross-section that locks into gears, wheels, and motor inserts — preventing them from spinning freely on the axle.

📷 Photo Needed

Low-strength 1/8 inch and high-strength 1/4 inch shafts side by side

docs.title: Shaft Collars
docs.text:
Shaft collars lock onto axles to prevent lateral (side-to-side) movement. They use a set screw to clamp onto the shaft.

Type Description Clamping Collar Set screw tightens onto the shaft — most common Rubber Shaft Collar Friction fit — quick to reposition, less secure

Collar Placement

Place shaft collars on both sides of components that shouldn't slide. The collar on each side acts as a wall, keeping the gear/wheel/sprocket exactly where you want it.

docs.title: Wheel Types
docs.text:
Wheel Key Feature Best For Traction Wheel Full rubber contact surface — maximum grip Drivetrains that need pushing power, no-slip applications Omni Wheel Small perpendicular rollers around the circumference Turning with less scrub, side-to-side movement Mecanum Wheel Angled rollers (45°) around the circumference Omni-directional movement (strafe, diagonal, rotate)

📷 Photo Needed

Traction wheel, omni wheel, and mecanum wheel side by side with labels

docs.title: When to Use Each
docs.text:
Drivetrain Type Wheel Setup Standard tank drive 4 or 6 traction wheels, or mix traction + omni Easy-turn tank drive Omni wheels on front and back, traction in middle Holonomic / X-drive 4 omni wheels at 45° angles Mecanum drive 4 mecanum wheels (specific L/R pattern)

📐 Diagram Needed

Top-down diagrams: tank drive, easy-turn tank, X-drive, mecanum — showing wheel types and placement

Activity: Friction Test Challenge

Time: 20 minutes | Format: Pairs

Objective: Build a single free-spinning wheel on an axle and achieve 5+ seconds of continuous spin from a single flick.

Requirements:

  1. Mount a wheel on a high-strength axle
  2. Support the axle in bearings pressed into C-Channel
  3. Use spacers to eliminate lateral play
  4. Use shoulder screws or screw joints where appropriate

Procedure:

  1. Build your assembly
  2. Flick the wheel and time how long it spins
  3. Iterate — adjust spacing, check bearing alignment, reduce friction
  4. Record your best time

Scoring:

Time Rating < 2 seconds Needs work — friction is too high 2–4 seconds Acceptable — room for improvement 5–7 seconds Good — solid friction management 8+ seconds Excellent — competition-quality assembly

Hint: If your wheel stops quickly, check for: axle rubbing on metal (use bearings), components pressed too tightly (add spacers), misaligned bearing holes.

docs.title: Parts Quiz
docs.text:
Module Assessment

Before moving to Module 1.2, you must pass the Parts Identification Quiz. Your mentor will present parts and ask you to:

  1. Name the part
  2. State its primary use
  3. Explain when you would choose it over an alternative (e.g., keps nut vs. nylock)

Passing score: 80%

Study the tables in this module. Handle the physical parts. The quiz is hands-on — you'll be holding real parts, not looking at pictures.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] Safety is non-negotiable — follow the rules every session
  • [x] VEX metal types have specific purposes — learn which to use where
  • [x] Shoulder screws allow rotation; standard screws lock joints rigid
  • [x] Spacers prevent friction between moving parts
  • [x] Boxing with standoffs creates dramatically stiffer structures
  • [x] Bearings are mandatory on every rotating axle
  • [x] Motor cartridges control speed/torque tradeoff
  • [x] Wheel choice affects traction, turning, and maneuverability

Next up: Module 1.2: Structure & Bracing — learn how to make structures that don't wobble.

docs.title: Module 1.2: Structure & Bracing
docs.text: A robot is only as good as its frame. In this module, you'll learn why structures wobble, how to eliminate unwanted movement, and how to build frames that are rigid in all three dimensions. The concepts here apply to every mechanism you'll ever build.

docs.title: Why Structure Matters
docs.text:
The Wobble Problem

The most common failure mode in VEX robotics isn't a broken part — it's a wobbly frame. A wobbly drivetrain means inconsistent driving, inaccurate autonomous routines, and mechanisms that don't line up. Fixing wobble after the robot is built is 10x harder than preventing it during construction.

Every structural failure comes down to one concept: Degrees of Freedom.

docs.title: Degrees of Freedom (DOF)
docs.text: A Degree of Freedom is a direction in which a joint or structure can move. The goal of structural building is to eliminate all unwanted DOFs while keeping the ones you need (like wheels spinning).

docs.title: The Single-Screw Problem
docs.text:
Take two C-Channels and connect them with a single screw at one corner.

What happens? The joint acts as a pivot point. The two pieces can rotate around that screw. That's 1 DOF you don't want.

🎬 GIF/Video Needed

Two C-Channels connected at one screw point, rotating freely around the pivot

Now imagine a rectangle made of four C-Channels, each corner connected with a single screw. You have four pivot points. Push on any corner and the whole rectangle collapses into a parallelogram.

🎬 GIF/Video Needed

Rectangle frame collapsing into parallelogram when pushed (single screw at each corner)

Activity: The Wobbly Rectangle

Time: 10 minutes | Format: Individual

  1. Build a rectangle using four C-Channels and one screw at each corner (4 screws total, one per corner joint).
  2. Hold one side flat on the table.
  3. Push the opposite side sideways.

Observation: The rectangle collapses easily. Each single-screw corner is a pivot point (1 DOF per corner).

Now improve it:

  1. Add a second screw at each corner, spaced apart from the first.
  2. Try to collapse the rectangle again.

Observation: With two screws per corner (spaced apart), the joints can no longer pivot. The rectangle holds its shape.

📷 Photo Needed

Two C-Channels connected with 2 screws spaced apart, demonstrating rigidity

Key Insight: A single screw creates a pivot. Two or more screws, spaced apart, create a rigid joint.

docs.title: Rules for Rigid Joints
docs.text: Joint Type DOFs Rigidity 1 screw 1 rotational DOF Pivot — freely rotates 2 screws (close together) Nearly locked Mostly rigid but can flex 2+ screws (spaced apart) 0 DOFs Fully rigid — no rotation, no flex Boxed connection 0 DOFs Maximum rigidity

docs.title: Mounting Pattern Matters
docs.text:
The spacing between screws determines how much they resist rotation:

Poor (screws close together):     Good (screws spread apart):

  o o                              o . . . . o
  ↑ ↑                              ↑         ↑
  Close = weak resistance          Far = strong resistance
  to rotation                      to rotation

The Farther Apart, The Better

Two screws 5 holes apart resist rotation much more than two screws 1 hole apart. Always spread your mounting screws as far as the part allows.

📐 Diagram Needed

Poor screw spacing (close, weak) vs good spacing (far apart, strong rotation resistance)

docs.title: Triangle Bracing
docs.text: The triangle is the strongest shape in structural engineering. Unlike a rectangle (which can collapse into a parallelogram), a triangle cannot deform without changing the length of one of its sides — which metal won't do under normal loads.

docs.title: Applying Triangles to VEX
docs.text:
You don't need to build literal triangles. You need to add diagonal braces that turn rectangles into triangulated structures.

Weak rectangle:          Strong triangulated rectangle:

  +----------+            +----------+
  |          |            |  \    /  |
  |          |            |    \/    |
  |          |            |    /\    |
  |          |            |  /    \  |
  +----------+            +----------+

A single diagonal brace across a rectangle converts it into two triangles — and suddenly the structure cannot collapse.

📐 Diagram Needed

Weak rectangle vs triangulated rectangle with diagonal braces

docs.title: How to Add Diagonal Braces in VEX
docs.text:
1. Flat bar diagonals — cut a 1x flat bar to length, screw across the diagonal of a rectangular frame
  2. Standoff diagonals — use a standoff as a diagonal strut between two corners
  3. Gusset plates — small L-brackets or plates at corners that add triangulation

📷 Photo Needed

Three bracing methods: flat bar diagonal, standoff diagonal, and gusset plate

When In Doubt, Add a Diagonal

If any part of your robot wobbles, look for unsupported rectangles. Add a diagonal brace and the wobble disappears.

docs.title: 3D Structural Thinking
docs.text: Most beginners build flat (2D) structures. But robots exist in three dimensions, and wobble in the third dimension is just as destructive.

docs.title: The Second Floor
docs.text:
Think of your drivetrain as the first floor of a building. Mechanisms mount above it — that's the second floor. The connection between floors must be rigid in all three planes:

Plane Direction How to Brace XY (horizontal) Side-to-side wobble Cross-bracing within each floor XZ (vertical front) Front-to-back lean Vertical braces connecting floors YZ (vertical side) Side lean Vertical braces on the sides

📐 Diagram Needed

3D robot frame showing XY, XZ, and YZ planes with bracing directions labeled

Activity: Build a Two-Story Structure

Time: 20 minutes | Format: Pairs

  1. Build a rectangular base (first floor) using four C-Channels. Make it rigid — use multiple screws per corner.
  2. Add four vertical C-Channel posts, one at each corner.
  3. Add a second rectangular frame (second floor) on top of the posts.
  4. Test: Hold the base and try to wobble the top floor side-to-side and front-to-back.

Problem: The top floor wobbles badly, even if all joints have multiple screws. Why? Because the vertical posts have no diagonal bracing — they're just columns.

📷 Photo Needed

Two-story VEX structure WITHOUT diagonal braces — visibly wobbly

Fix it:

  1. Add diagonal braces on at least two sides (front and one side).
  2. Retest. The wobble should be dramatically reduced.
  3. Add diagonal braces on the remaining sides.
  4. Retest. The structure should now feel solid.

📷 Photo Needed

Same structure WITH diagonal braces added — rigid when pushed

Key Insight: Vertical structures need diagonal bracing just like horizontal ones. If you can wobble it, it needs a brace.

docs.title: Common Bracing Mistakes
docs.text:
Mistake: Bracing Only in 2D

Building a rigid flat frame but connecting it to vertical elements with single-screw joints. The frame is strong but the vertical connection wobbles.

Fix: Use multiple screws (spaced apart) at every vertical joint, and add diagonal braces between floors.

Mistake: Over-Constraining

Adding so many screws and braces that the structure becomes impossible to assemble, disassemble, or modify.

Fix: Brace strategically. You don't need diagonals on every face — two perpendicular braced faces are usually enough to eliminate wobble in all directions.

Mistake: Ignoring Twist

A structure can be rigid against push/pull forces but still twist like a wet towel.

Fix: Boxing C-Channels and using cross-bracing in multiple planes eliminates twist.

docs.title: Structural Design Checklist
docs.text:
Before moving on from any structural assembly, verify:

  • [ ] Every joint has 2+ screws spaced apart (no single-screw pivots, unless intentional)
  • [ ] Every rectangle is triangulated (diagonal brace or gusset)
  • [ ] Vertical connections are braced against lean in both directions
  • [ ] Boxed C-Channels are used for high-load rails (drivetrain)
  • [ ] No wobble when you hold the base and push the top
  • [ ] No twist when you hold opposite corners and rotate your hands

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] A single screw creates a pivot point — always use 2+, spaced apart
  • [x] Rectangles collapse without diagonal bracing — triangles don't
  • [x] Robots are 3D — brace in all three planes, not just the horizontal
  • [x] Boxed C-Channels resist twisting better than single channels
  • [x] Test for wobble by holding the base and pushing the top
  • [x] If it wobbles, find the unsupported rectangle and add a diagonal

Next up: Module 1.3: Gear Ratios & Friction — learn how to trade speed for torque and eliminate energy-wasting friction.

docs.title: Unit 2: Programming
docs.text: A robot that can't be controlled is just a paperweight. Unit 2 teaches you to program your V5 drivetrain using VSCode, PROS, and LemLib — the same tools used by top-tier VEX teams worldwide. By the end of this unit, you'll have driver controls, button-mapped mechanisms, tuned PID, and a working autonomous routine.

docs.title: Unit Overview
docs.text: Details Modules 5 Field Challenges 2 Estimated Time 10–14 hours Prerequisite Completed Unit 1 drivetrain with mounted electronics Final Deliverable A robot with driver controls, a motorized mechanism, and autonomous capability

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
By the end of Unit 2, you will be able to:

  • [x] Set up a PROS/LemLib development environment in VSCode
  • [x] Configure and drive your robot with tank or arcade controls
  • [x] Map controller buttons to mechanism motors
  • [x] Tune PID controllers for straight driving and accurate turning
  • [x] Program a multi-step autonomous routine using odometry
  • [x] Debug and iterate on code using the V5 Brain terminal

docs.title: Why LemLib?
docs.text:
LemLib is an open-source library built on top of PROS that simplifies the hardest parts of VEX programming. It provides:

Feature What It Does Why It Matters Chassis configuration Define your drivetrain in a few lines No manual motor setup boilerplate Drive modes Tank, arcade, curvature with one function call Easy control scheme switching PID controllers Built-in tunable PID for lateral and angular movement Accurate autonomous driving Odometry Tracks robot position on the field using sensors Robot knows where it is during autonomous Motion profiling Smooth path following with acceleration control Professional-quality autonomous routines

PROS vs. VEXcode

You may have seen VEXcode (the official VEX IDE). We use PROS + LemLib instead because:

  • PROS gives full C++ access with no restrictions
  • LemLib provides competition-tested algorithms
  • VSCode is a professional IDE you'll use in your career
  • Top competitive teams overwhelmingly use PROS

docs.title: Module Roadmap
docs.text: Module Title Key Skills 2.1 VSCode & LemLib Setup IDE installation, PROS extension, project creation, first upload 2.2 Drive Config & Controls Motor ports, gear ratio config, tank/arcade drive, first drive test 2.3 Motorized Shovel Adding a mechanism, button mapping, toggle vs hold 2.4 PID Tuning P, I, D concepts, lateral tuning, angular tuning 2.5 Autonomous Routing Coordinate system, odometry, multi-step auton

docs.title: Field Challenges
docs.text:
Challenge What It Tests Pickup Relay Driver control, button mapping, mechanism function Auton Showdown PID tuning quality, autonomous routing accuracy

Challenge Prerequisite

You must have a working driver-controlled robot with a motorized mechanism before attempting the Pickup Relay. You must have a tuned autonomous routine before attempting the Auton Showdown.

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text:
• Completed Unit 1 drivetrain (with V5 Brain, Battery, Radio, Inertial Sensor)
  • 1x additional 11W motor (for the shovel mechanism in Module 2.3)
  • Metal and hardware for building the shovel mechanism
  • USB-A to USB-C cable (for Brain connection)

docs.title: Software
docs.text:
• A computer (Windows, Mac, or Linux)
  • Internet connection (for initial setup)
  • VSCode (free) — installed in Module 2.1
  • PROS extension for VSCode — installed in Module 2.1

docs.title: Tips for Success
docs.text:
Save Early, Save Often

Code disappears when you don't save. Use Ctrl+S (or Cmd+S) after every change. PROS projects are just files on your computer — back them up.

Read the Error Messages

When your code doesn't compile, the error message tells you exactly what's wrong and on which line. Read it carefully before asking for help. 90% of compilation errors are typos, missing semicolons, or mismatched brackets.

Test Incrementally

Don't write 50 lines of code and then upload. Write 5 lines, upload, test. Write 5 more, upload, test. Small changes are easy to debug. Large changes are a nightmare.

Robot Safety During Programming

Always have the robot on blocks (wheels off the ground) when testing new code for the first time. A bug in your drive code can send the robot flying off a table or into someone. Blocks first, floor second.

docs.title: Let's Get Started
docs.text: Begin with Module 2.1: VSCode & LemLib Setup — you need the tools before you can write the code.

docs.title: Module 2.5: Autonomous Routing
docs.text: The autonomous period is your robot's chance to score points without any human input. In this module, you'll learn LemLib's coordinate system, odometry basics, and how to code a multi-step autonomous routine that drives your robot through a sequence of waypoints.

docs.title: Why Autonomous Matters
docs.text:
In VEX competition, every match starts with a 15-second autonomous period. During this time, robots run pre-programmed routines — no driver input allowed. Teams that have reliable autonomous routines score first and gain a strategic advantage for the rest of the match.

Autonomous Wins Matches

Many competitive matches are decided by 2–5 points. A good autonomous routine can score 5–15 points in 15 seconds. Teams with no autonomous routine start every match behind.

docs.title: The Coordinate System
docs.text: LemLib uses a Cartesian coordinate system (X, Y) with headings measured in degrees.

docs.title: Axes
docs.text:
+Y (forward)
             ↑
             |
             |
  -X ←———— (0,0) ————→ +X
             |
             |
             ↓
            -Y (backward)
  • (0, 0) is wherever the robot starts (or wherever you set the initial position)
  • +X is to the right
  • +Y is forward
  • Heading 0° is facing up (+Y direction)
  • Heading 90° is facing right (+X direction)
  • Heading 180° is facing down (-Y direction)
  • Heading 270° is facing left (-X direction)

📐 Diagram Needed

LemLib coordinates: X/Y axes, +X right, +Y forward, heading 0°=up, 90°=right as compass rose

docs.title: Units
docs.text:
Measurement Unit X, Y position Inches Heading Degrees (0–360)

Field Reference

A VEX competition field is 144 inches x 144 inches (12 feet x 12 feet). Each field tile is 24 inches x 24 inches. Knowing this helps you plan coordinates for your autonomous routine.

📐 Diagram Needed

VEX field (144x144 in) with 24-inch tile grid overlay, example coordinates at key positions

docs.title: Odometry: How the Robot Knows Where It Is
docs.text: Odometry is the process of tracking the robot's position by measuring wheel rotations and heading changes over time.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Motor encoders measure how far each side of the drivetrain has traveled
  2. The Inertial Sensor (IMU) measures the robot's heading (which direction it's facing)
  3. LemLib combines this data to calculate the robot's (X, Y) position on the field
  4. This position updates continuously — many times per second

📐 Diagram Needed

Odometry: motor encoders (distance) + IMU (heading) → position calculator → (X, Y, heading)

docs.title: Setting the Starting Position
docs.text:
At the beginning of autonomous, tell LemLib where the robot is starting:

void autonomous() {
    // Set starting position: X=0, Y=0, heading=0°
    chassis.setPose(0, 0, 0);

    // Now all movements are relative to this starting position
}

Consistent Starting Position

The quality of your autonomous routine depends entirely on the robot starting in the exact same position and orientation every time. Use field markings, tape, or alignment jigs to position the robot consistently.

docs.title: moveToPoint
docs.text:
Drives the robot to a specific (X, Y) coordinate.

// Drive to the point (0, 24) — 24 inches forward
chassis.moveToPoint(0, 24, 5000);
//                   X   Y  timeout(ms)

The robot will calculate the path from its current position to the target and drive there using your tuned PID controllers.

docs.title: turnToHeading
docs.text:
Turns the robot to face a specific heading (absolute, not relative).

// Turn to face 90° (facing right)
chassis.turnToHeading(90, 3000);
//                    heading  timeout(ms)

docs.title: turnToPoint
docs.text:
Turns the robot to face toward a specific (X, Y) coordinate.

// Turn to face the point (24, 24)
chassis.turnToPoint(24, 24, 3000);

docs.title: moveToPose
docs.text:
Drives to a position AND faces a specific heading when it arrives. This is the most versatile command.

// Drive to (24, 24) and arrive facing 90°
chassis.moveToPose(24, 24, 90, 5000);
//                  X   Y  heading  timeout

docs.title: Timeout Parameter
docs.text:
Every movement command has a timeout in milliseconds. If the robot hasn't reached the target within this time, it gives up and moves to the next command. This prevents your robot from getting stuck forever on one step.

Timeout Use Case 2000–3000 ms Short movements and turns 4000–5000 ms Medium movements 6000–8000 ms Long drives across the field

Set Reasonable Timeouts

Too short and the robot won't finish movements. Too long and the robot wastes time if it gets stuck. Start with generous timeouts and tighten them once your routine is reliable.

docs.title: Example: Three-Step Autonomous
docs.text:
void autonomous() {
    // Set starting position
    chassis.setPose(0, 0, 0);

    // Step 1: Drive forward 24 inches
    chassis.moveToPoint(0, 24, 4000);

    // Step 2: Turn to face right (90°)
    chassis.turnToHeading(90, 3000);

    // Step 3: Drive forward 24 inches (now moving in the +X direction)
    chassis.moveToPoint(24, 24, 4000);
}

What this does:

  1. Robot starts at (0, 0) facing forward
  2. Drives straight forward to (0, 24)
  3. Turns 90 degrees to the right
  4. Drives to (24, 24)

The robot traces an L-shaped path.

📐 Diagram Needed

L-shaped path: start (0,0) → straight to (0,24) → turn → straight to (24,24), coordinates labeled

docs.title: Adding Mechanism Control
docs.text:
You can combine movement commands with mechanism commands:

void autonomous() {
    chassis.setPose(0, 0, 0);

    // Drive to the object
    chassis.moveToPoint(0, 24, 4000);

    // Lower the shovel
    shovel_motor.move_absolute(-45, 100);
    pros::delay(500); // Wait for shovel to lower

    // Drive forward to scoop the object
    chassis.moveToPoint(0, 30, 2000);

    // Lift the shovel
    shovel_motor.move_absolute(90, 100);
    pros::delay(500); // Wait for shovel to lift

    // Turn toward scoring zone
    chassis.turnToHeading(180, 3000);

    // Drive to scoring zone
    chassis.moveToPoint(0, 0, 5000);

    // Lower shovel to release object
    shovel_motor.move_absolute(0, 100);
}

docs.title: The Testing Loop
docs.text:
1. Write your autonomous routine
  2. Upload to the robot
  3. Position the robot at the exact starting location
  4. Run autonomous
  5. Observe — where did the robot go vs. where should it have gone?
  6. Measure — how far off was each waypoint?
  7. Adjust — modify coordinates, timeouts, or PID values
  8. Repeat

Safety During Autonomous Testing

The robot moves on its own during autonomous. Always stand clear. Never reach for the robot while autonomous is running. If you need to stop it, press the stop button on the Brain or turn off the controller.

docs.title: Common Issues and Fixes
docs.text: Problem Likely Cause Fix Robot doesn't move autonomous() function is empty or not called Verify code is in autonomous(), run in auton mode Robot drives the wrong distance PID not tuned (Module 2.4) Re-tune lateral PID Robot turns the wrong amount Angular PID not tuned Re-tune angular PID Robot goes to wrong position Wrong X/Y coordinates Check coordinate math, verify starting position Robot times out mid-movement Timeout too short, or robot is stuck Increase timeout, check for mechanical issues Routine is inconsistent (different every run) Starting position varies, battery level changes Use alignment markers, charge battery, check for mechanical slop

docs.title: Debugging with the Brain Screen
docs.text:
You can print the robot's current position to the Brain's LCD screen for debugging:

void initialize() {
    chassis.calibrate();
    pros::lcd::initialize();

    // Print position to screen continuously
    pros::Task screen_task([&]() {
        while (true) {
            lemlib::Pose pose = chassis.getPose();
            pros::lcd::print(0, "X: %.1f", pose.x);
            pros::lcd::print(1, "Y: %.1f", pose.y);
            pros::lcd::print(2, "Heading: %.1f", pose.theta);
            pros::delay(50);
        }
    });
}

This lets you see exactly where the robot thinks it is during autonomous, which makes debugging much easier.

📷 Photo Needed

V5 Brain screen showing LCD with X, Y, Heading values during autonomous

Use the Screen

The Brain screen is your best debugging tool. Print everything — position, heading, motor velocities, sensor values. When something goes wrong, the screen data tells you why.

docs.title: Step 1: Map the Course
docs.text:
Before writing code, sketch the path on paper:

  1. Draw the field or course layout
  2. Mark the starting position as (0, 0)
  3. Mark each waypoint with its (X, Y) coordinates
  4. Note the heading at each waypoint
  5. Note any mechanism actions at each waypoint

docs.title: Step 2: Convert to Code
docs.text:
Turn your sketch into movement commands:

Sketch:
  Start (0, 0) → Point A (0, 24) → Turn right → Point B (24, 24)

Code:
  chassis.setPose(0, 0, 0);
  chassis.moveToPoint(0, 24, 4000);
  chassis.turnToHeading(90, 3000);
  chassis.moveToPoint(24, 24, 4000);

docs.title: Step 3: Test Each Step Individually
docs.text:
Don't test the full routine at once. Test step by step:

  1. Test Step 1 alone — does the robot reach Point A?
  2. Add Step 2 — does the turn reach the correct heading?
  3. Add Step 3 — does the robot reach Point B?

Activity: Code a 3-Step Autonomous

Target: Program your robot to drive the following path:

  1. Start at (0, 0) facing forward
  2. Drive forward 36 inches to (0, 36)
  3. Turn right to face 90 degrees
  4. Drive 24 inches to (24, 36)
  5. Stop and hold position

Test criteria:

  • Place tape markers at (0, 36) and (24, 36)
  • The robot should end within 3 inches of the final marker
  • The robot should face within 5 degrees of 90 degrees
  • Run 3 times — results should be consistent

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] LemLib uses a Cartesian coordinate system with X (right), Y (forward), heading in degrees
  • [x] Odometry tracks position using motor encoders and the Inertial Sensor
  • [x] moveToPoint() drives to X, Y coordinates using tuned PID
  • [x] turnToHeading() rotates to an absolute heading
  • [x] Always set the starting pose with chassis.setPose() at the beginning of autonomous
  • [x] Test incrementally — one step at a time, not the full routine
  • [x] Consistent starting position is critical for repeatable autonomous
  • [x] Use the Brain LCD screen to debug position tracking

Next up: Time to prove your skills in the challenges. Pickup Relay tests your driver controls and mechanism, and Auton Showdown tests your autonomous routing.

docs.title: Challenge: Auton Showdown
docs.text: No driver. No controller. Just code. The Auton Showdown is an autonomous time trial where your robot must navigate a set course entirely on its own. This challenge tests the quality of your PID tuning, the accuracy of your autonomous routing, and the reliability of your odometry.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Program your robot to autonomously drive a set course. Fastest accurate completion wins.

docs.title: The Course
docs.text:
[POINT B]
                    (24, 36)
                       ↑
                       |
                       | 12 inches
                       |
   [START] ——————→ [POINT A] ——————→ [FINISH]
   (0, 0)          (24, 24)          (48, 24)
                                      STOP

📐 Diagram Needed

Auton Showdown course: START (0,0) → Point A (24,24) → Point B (24,36) → FINISH (48,24)

docs.title: Course Waypoints
docs.text: Waypoint Coordinates Action START (0, 0) facing 0° Robot starts here Point A (24, 24) Drive from start to Point A Point B (24, 36) Drive from Point A to Point B FINISH (48, 24) Drive from Point B to Finish and stop

docs.title: Physical Setup
docs.text:
• Tape markers on the field at each waypoint
  • Cones placed at Point A and Point B as visual references (the robot must pass within 6 inches of each cone without hitting it)
  • Finish line marked with tape

📷 Photo Needed

Physical course: tape markers at waypoints, cones at Point A and B, finish line

Course Customization

Your mentor may adjust the waypoint coordinates based on available space. The course layout will be announced before the challenge, giving you time to program and test.

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
Rule Details Control Fully autonomous — no driver input after pressing the start button Starting Robot must start from the designated start position and heading Timing Starts when the program begins. Stops when the robot is stationary at the finish (or timeout). Cone proximity Robot must pass within 6 inches of each waypoint cone. Missing a waypoint = penalty. Cone contact Hitting a cone = +3 second penalty Waypoint skip Missing a waypoint entirely (passing more than 6 inches away) = +10 second penalty Finish accuracy Robot must stop within 6 inches of the finish mark. Outside 6 inches = +5 seconds. Timeout If the robot has not reached the finish within 30 seconds, the run ends. Attempts 3 attempts — best score counts Tuning You may modify code and re-upload between attempts

📐 Diagram Needed

6-inch proximity rule: cone with 6-inch radius circle, robot path inside (pass) vs outside (penalty)

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text:
Final Time = Raw Time + Penalties

Event Penalty Hit a waypoint cone +3 seconds Missed a waypoint (> 6 inches away) +10 seconds Finish position > 6 inches from mark +5 seconds Did not reach finish (timeout) 30 seconds + all applicable penalties

docs.title: Example Scoring
docs.text:
Run Raw Time Cone Hits Missed WP Finish Off Final Score Attempt 1 12.3s 0 1 No 22.3s Attempt 2 14.1s 1 0 No 17.1s Attempt 3 11.8s 0 0 No 11.8s (best)

Accuracy Beats Speed

The penalty for missing a waypoint (10 seconds) is massive. A slower but accurate run almost always beats a fast but sloppy one. Tune your PID for accuracy first, speed second.

docs.title: What This Tests
docs.text: Skill How It's Tested PID tuning (2.4) Does the robot drive the right distances and turn the right angles? Autonomous routing (2.5) Are the movement commands correct? Do the coordinates match the physical course? Odometry accuracy Does the robot's internal position tracking match reality? Sensor calibration Is the IMU calibrated? Does heading tracking stay accurate? Consistency Can you get similar results across 3 attempts?

docs.title: Step 1: Measure the Course
docs.text:
Before writing code:

  1. Walk the physical course
  2. Verify the tape marker positions match the announced coordinates
  3. Note any landmarks or alignment aids
  4. Identify where you'll position the robot at the start

docs.title: Step 2: Write the Code
docs.text:
Convert the waypoints to LemLib movement commands:

void autonomous() {
    chassis.setPose(0, 0, 0); // Start position

    // Drive to Point A
    chassis.moveToPoint(24, 24, 6000);

    // Drive to Point B
    chassis.moveToPoint(24, 36, 4000);

    // Drive to Finish
    chassis.moveToPoint(48, 24, 6000);
}

Consider Using moveToPose

For more precise arrivals, moveToPose lets you specify the heading at each waypoint:

chassis.moveToPose(24, 24, 45, 5000);  // Arrive at Point A facing 45°
chassis.moveToPose(24, 36, 0, 4000);   // Arrive at Point B facing north
