chassis.moveToPose(48, 24, 90, 5000);  // Arrive at Finish facing east

docs.title: Step 3: Test and Iterate
docs.text:
Testing Protocol

  1. Dry run on blocks: Upload and run with the robot on blocks. Watch the motor movements — do they make sense?
  2. Single-step test: Comment out all but the first movement. Test just the drive to Point A. Is it accurate?
  3. Two-step test: Uncomment the second movement. Test through Point B.
  4. Full course: Run the complete routine. Measure accuracy at each waypoint.
  5. Consistency test: Run 3 times without changing code. Are results similar?

docs.title: Between Attempts
docs.text:
You have 5 minutes between attempts to:

  • Review what went wrong
  • Adjust coordinates or timeouts in code
  • Re-tune PID values if needed
  • Re-upload the modified program
  • Re-position the robot at the start

Don't Over-Adjust

If your first attempt was close but slightly off, make small adjustments. If you change too many things at once, you won't know what helped and what hurt. Change one value at a time.

docs.title: Strategy Tips
docs.text:
Tune Before the Challenge

PID tuning during the 5-minute break between attempts is stressful and rushed. Do your PID tuning in Module 2.4 and verify it works before challenge day. The challenge is for proving your tuning, not doing it.

Timeouts Are Your Safety Net

Set generous timeouts (5–6 seconds per movement). If a movement fails, the timeout prevents your robot from being stuck forever on one step. A completed-with-timeout run beats a stuck robot every time.

Watch Other Robots First

If the bracket allows, watch other teams' attempts before yours. See where robots commonly overshoot or undershoot. Learn from their mistakes.

Battery Consistency

Use a fully charged battery for every attempt. Battery voltage affects motor power, which affects how far the robot drives. A routine tuned on a full battery will undershoot on a depleted one.

docs.title: Debugging During the Challenge
docs.text:
If your first attempt goes poorly, use the 5-minute break to diagnose:

Symptom Likely Issue Quick Fix Robot drives too far on every leg Lateral kP too high, or wrong wheel size in config Reduce kP by 10-15%, verify wheel diameter Robot drives too short on every leg Lateral kP too low, or wrong wheel size Increase kP by 10-15%, verify wheel diameter Turns are inaccurate Angular PID not tuned, or IMU not calibrated Check IMU calibration, adjust angular kP First leg is perfect, later legs drift Odometry accumulation error Reduce the problem — test later legs in isolation Completely wrong direction Starting heading is wrong, or motor direction reversed Check setPose heading, verify motor port signs

docs.title: Post-Challenge Reflection
docs.text:
Document the following in your engineering notebook:

  1. Best time and penalties: What was your final score?
  2. Consistency: How different were your 3 attempts? What caused variation?
  3. Accuracy at each waypoint: Which waypoint was hardest to hit? Why?
  4. Changes between attempts: What did you adjust? Did it help?
  5. What would you do differently with unlimited tuning time?
  6. Compare to other teams: What did the winner do that you didn't?

docs.title: Looking Ahead
docs.text:
This Is Just the Beginning

The Auton Showdown uses a simple 3-waypoint course. In actual VEX competition, autonomous routines involve:

  • 10–15 sequential movements
  • Mechanism actions (intake, score, clamp) at specific positions
  • Multiple path options depending on alliance color and strategy
  • Split-second timing optimization

The skills you've built here — PID tuning, coordinate planning, iterative testing — scale directly to competition-level autonomous programming.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] Autonomous performance depends on PID tuning quality and coordinate accuracy
  • [x] Accuracy is worth more than speed due to the penalty structure
  • [x] Test incrementally — one waypoint at a time, then the full course
  • [x] Consistent starting position is the foundation of reliable autonomous
  • [x] Use the Brain screen to debug position tracking in real-time
  • [x] Small adjustments between attempts — don't change everything at once
  • [x] Battery level affects motor power — use a charged battery for every attempt

Congratulations on completing Unit 2! You now have a fully programmed robot with driver controls, a motorized mechanism, and autonomous capability. You're ready for Unit 3: Onshape CAD — where you'll learn to design before you build.

docs.title: Challenge: Pickup Relay
docs.text: Your first full-robot challenge. The Pickup Relay tests everything from Unit 2 — driver controls, button mapping, and mechanism function — in a timed competition to pick up objects and deliver them to a scoring zone.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Pick up 3 objects with your motorized shovel and place them in the scoring zone. Fastest completion time wins.

docs.title: Field Setup
docs.text:
+--------------------------------------------------+
|                                                    |
|                  [SCORING ZONE]                    |
|              (24" x 24" taped square)              |
|                                                    |
|                                                    |
|                                                    |
|          (3)                                       |
|           ○           Bean bags or balls           |
|                       placed at marked positions   |
|     (2)                                            |
|      ○                                             |
|                                                    |
|  (1)                                               |
|   ○                                                |
|                                                    |
|  [START]                                           |
+--------------------------------------------------+

📐 Diagram Needed

Pickup Relay field: scoring zone (24x24 shaded square), 3 objects at marked positions, start zone

docs.title: Object Placement
docs.text:
• 3 objects (bean bags, tennis balls, or VEX game elements) are placed at marked positions on the field
  • Positions are marked with tape — same positions for every competitor
  • Objects are spaced at least 24 inches apart
  • The scoring zone is at the far end of the field

Object Choice

Your mentor will choose the objects based on what's available. Bean bags are easiest (they don't roll). Tennis balls are harder (they roll off the shovel). Game elements add realism.

📷 Photo Needed

Real Pickup Relay field: tape on floor marking scoring zone, 3 bean bags at positions

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
Rule Details Control Driver control only — one driver, one controller Mechanism Must use the motorized shovel to pick up objects (no ramming them into the zone) One at a time Only one object may be carried at a time (pick up, deliver, return for next) Scoring Object must be fully inside the scoring zone (no part hanging over the tape line) Dropped objects If an object falls off the shovel, the driver must re-pick it with the mechanism (no touching with hands) Robot contact Driver may not touch the robot once the run starts Timing Clock starts on "GO" signal. Clock stops when the third object is fully in the scoring zone. Attempts Best of 2 attempts counts as your score DNF If all 3 objects are not scored within 3 minutes, the run is a DNF

Hands Off

Once the timer starts, you cannot touch the robot or the objects. If the robot flips over or gets stuck, your run is over (DNF). Build a stable drivetrain and drive carefully.

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text:
Final Time = Completion Time

There are no penalties — either the object is in the zone or it isn't. The only metric is how fast you complete the task.

docs.title: Scoring Breakdown
docs.text: Outcome Score All 3 objects in zone Completion time recorded 2 objects in zone (time expired) DNF — ranked below all completions 1 or 0 objects DNF — ranked by objects scored, then time

docs.title: What This Tests
docs.text: Skill How It's Tested Drive controls (2.2) Can you navigate to objects quickly and accurately? Button mapping (2.3) Can you operate the shovel while driving? Mechanism function (2.3) Does the shovel pick up and hold objects reliably? Brake mode (2.3) Does the shovel hold position while driving? Driving skill Smooth driving, no wasted movements, good spatial awareness

docs.title: Strategy Tips
docs.text:
Optimize Your Route

Plan the order you pick up objects before the timer starts. Usually, nearest-first is best — but sometimes picking up a far object first avoids backtracking.

🎬 GIF/Video Needed

Robot pickup: approach bean bag → lower shovel → scoop → lift → drive away

Slow Down for Pickups

The shovel approach is where most time is lost. Drive fast between objects, but slow down and be precise when scooping. A missed pickup costs more time than a slower approach.

Practice the Release

Dropping the object in the scoring zone cleanly is a skill. Practice lowering the shovel smoothly — don't slam it down, which can bounce the object out of the zone.

Pre-Position the Shovel

Lower the shovel to ground level as you're driving toward the object, not after you arrive. This saves 1–2 seconds per pickup.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text: Mistake Consequence Prevention Driving too fast at the object Object gets pushed away instead of scooped Approach slowly for the last 6 inches Lifting shovel too quickly Object slides off the back Lift smoothly, not jerky Not lowering shovel enough Shovel slides over the object Make sure the scoop edge is below the object Oversteering near objects Miss the object entirely Use small stick movements for fine positioning Forgetting to lower shovel in the scoring zone Driving around with the object, wasting time Practice the release sequence until it's automatic

docs.title: Qualifying
docs.text: All competitors complete their 2 attempts. The best time from each competitor is their qualifying score.

docs.title: Bracket
docs.text:
Top qualifiers are seeded into a single-elimination bracket:

  • Bracket matches are run simultaneously (2 parallel courses if space allows) or back-to-back
  • Lower final time advances
  • Third-place match for the losers of the semifinals

docs.title: Practice Routine
docs.text:
Pre-Challenge Practice

Before the challenge, run through this practice routine at least 3 times:

Round 1: Mechanism only (no driving)

  1. Place an object directly in front of the robot
  2. Lower shovel, scoop, lift, lower, release
  3. Repeat 5 times until the motion feels natural

Round 2: Drive + mechanism (one object)

  1. Place one object 36 inches away
  2. Drive to it, pick it up, deliver to a target spot
  3. Time yourself — aim for under 15 seconds

Round 3: Full run (three objects)

  1. Set up the full course
  2. Run the complete challenge
  3. Record your time
  4. Identify your slowest section — practice that specifically

docs.title: Post-Challenge Reflection
docs.text:
After the challenge, document in your engineering notebook:

  1. What was your best time? Break it down — how long did each pickup take?
  2. What went wrong? Dropped objects? Missed pickups? Slow driving?
  3. What went right? What felt smooth and consistent?
  4. What would you change about your shovel design for better pickup reliability?
  5. How did the winning team do it differently? What can you learn from their approach?

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] The Pickup Relay is a combined test of driving, button mapping, and mechanism reliability
  • [x] Route planning matters — think about object order before starting
  • [x] Smooth, controlled movements beat fast, jerky ones
  • [x] Practice the pickup-carry-release cycle until it's muscle memory
  • [x] Reliable mechanisms win over fast but inconsistent ones
  • [x] Every second lost to a missed pickup is harder to make up than just driving slower

Next challenge: Auton Showdown — can your robot drive itself?

docs.title: Module 2.2: Drive Config & Controls
docs.text: Time to make your robot move. In this module, you'll configure your drivetrain in LemLib, set up driver controls, and drive your robot for the first time.

docs.title: Configuring the Drivetrain
docs.text: LemLib needs to know the physical properties of your drivetrain: which motor ports, what gear ratio, and what wheel size.

docs.title: Motor Port Assignment
docs.text:
First, determine which ports your motors are plugged into on the V5 Brain. Each port is numbered 1–21.

Labeling Ports

Label each motor cable with tape near the Brain connection. Write the port number and which motor it is (e.g., "Port 1 - Left Front"). This saves debugging time when wires get pulled.

docs.title: LemLib Chassis Configuration
docs.text:
In src/main.cpp, add the drivetrain configuration. Here's a standard 4-motor tank drive setup:

#include "main.h"
#include "lemlib/api.hpp"

// Motor groups — negative port number means reversed direction
pros::MotorGroup left_motors({-1, -2});   // Ports 1, 2 (reversed)
pros::MotorGroup right_motors({3, 4});    // Ports 3, 4

// Drivetrain configuration
lemlib::Drivetrain drivetrain(
    &left_motors,    // Left motor group
    &right_motors,   // Right motor group
    12.5,            // Track width (distance between left and right wheels, in inches)
    3.25,            // Wheel diameter (inches)
    360,             // Drivetrain RPM (motor RPM * external gear ratio)
    2                // Chase power (leave at 2 for now)
);

Motor Direction

Motors on the left side of the robot typically need to be reversed (negative port number) because they're mounted as a mirror image of the right side. If your robot spins in circles instead of driving straight, one side's motors are reversed incorrectly.

📐 Diagram Needed

Left and right motors viewed from rear — arrows showing spin direction, why left is reversed

docs.title: How to Determine Values
docs.text:
Parameter How to Measure Port numbers Check the V5 Brain's device menu — it shows which port each motor is on Track width Measure the center-to-center distance between left and right wheels (inches) Wheel diameter Check the wheel product — 2.75", 3.25", or 4" are standard VEX sizes Drivetrain RPM Motor cartridge RPM multiplied by your external gear ratio

📷 Photo Needed

V5 Brain Devices screen showing connected motors with port numbers

📐 Diagram Needed

Top-down drivetrain with dimension line between wheel centers, labeled in inches

docs.title: RPM Calculation Example
docs.text: Cartridge External Ratio Calculation Drivetrain RPM Green (200) 36t driving, 60t driven (1:1.67) 200 / 1.67 ~120 RPM Green (200) 48t driving, 36t driven (1.33:1) 200 * 1.33 ~267 RPM Green (200) 60t driving, 36t driven (1.67:1) 200 * 1.67 ~333 RPM Blue (600) 36t driving, 60t driven (1:1.67) 600 / 1.67 ~360 RPM

docs.title: Sensors for Odometry
docs.text:
LemLib uses sensors to track the robot's position. For now, configure the Inertial Sensor (IMU) you mounted in Unit 1:

// Inertial sensor on port 10
pros::Imu imu(10);

// Odometry sensors — using IMU for heading
lemlib::OdomSensors sensors(
    nullptr, // No vertical tracking wheel (using motor encoders)
    nullptr, // No second vertical tracking wheel
    nullptr, // No horizontal tracking wheel
    nullptr, // No second horizontal tracking wheel
    &imu     // Inertial sensor
);

docs.title: Full Chassis Object
docs.text:
Combine everything into the LemLib chassis object:

// PID controllers (we'll tune these in Module 2.4 — use defaults for now)
lemlib::ControllerSettings lateral_controller(
    10,   // kP
    0,    // kI
    3,    // kD
    3,    // anti-windup
    1,    // small error range (inches)
    100,  // small error timeout (ms)
    3,    // large error range (inches)
    500,  // large error timeout (ms)
    20    // slew rate
);

lemlib::ControllerSettings angular_controller(
    2,    // kP
    0,    // kI
    10,   // kD
    3,    // anti-windup
    1,    // small error range (degrees)
    100,  // small error timeout (ms)
    3,    // large error range (degrees)
    500,  // large error timeout (ms)
    0     // slew rate (0 = disabled)
);

// Create the chassis
lemlib::Chassis chassis(
    drivetrain,
    lateral_controller,
    angular_controller,
    sensors
);

docs.title: Tank Drive vs. Arcade Drive
docs.text: There are two main ways to map controller sticks to wheel movement.

docs.title: Tank Drive
docs.text:
Each joystick controls one side of the robot:

  • Left stick up/down = left wheels forward/backward
  • Right stick up/down = right wheels forward/backward
void opcontrol() {
    pros::Controller controller(pros::E_CONTROLLER_MASTER);

    while (true) {
        int left = controller.get_analog(pros::E_CONTROLLER_ANALOG_LEFT_Y);
        int right = controller.get_analog(pros::E_CONTROLLER_ANALOG_RIGHT_Y);

        chassis.tank(left, right);

        pros::delay(25);
    }
}
Pros Cons Intuitive for beginners Driving straight requires equal stick pressure Fine-grained control of each side Harder to make smooth curves Easy to spin in place Two-handed operation required

📐 Diagram Needed

V5 controller: Tank (both sticks = left/right wheels) vs Arcade (left stick throttle, right stick turn)

docs.title: Arcade Drive
docs.text:
One joystick controls both forward/backward and turning:

  • Left stick up/down = forward/backward
  • Left stick left/right = turning (or use right stick for "split arcade")
void opcontrol() {
    pros::Controller controller(pros::E_CONTROLLER_MASTER);

    while (true) {
        int throttle = controller.get_analog(pros::E_CONTROLLER_ANALOG_LEFT_Y);
        int turn = controller.get_analog(pros::E_CONTROLLER_ANALOG_RIGHT_X);

        chassis.arcade(throttle, turn);

        pros::delay(25);
    }
}
Pros Cons One stick for driving Less individual wheel control Easy to drive straight (just push forward) Turning sensitivity takes tuning Frees up one hand for buttons Can feel less responsive for some drivers

📐 Diagram Needed

V5 controller with left stick Y-axis = Throttle, right stick X-axis = Turn labeled

Split Arcade Is the Most Popular

Most competitive teams use split arcade: left stick Y for throttle, right stick X for turning. This gives the best of both worlds — easy straight driving with one stick, and a free right hand for mechanism buttons.

docs.title: Initialize Function
docs.text:
Add calibration to your initialize() function:

void initialize() {
    chassis.calibrate(); // Calibrate IMU — robot must be still during this
    pros::lcd::initialize(); // Enable the Brain LCD screen (optional, useful for debugging)
}

Calibration Requires Stillness

When chassis.calibrate() runs, the Inertial Sensor calibrates its gyroscope. The robot must be completely still for 2–3 seconds during this process. Moving the robot during calibration causes inaccurate heading data.

docs.title: Preparation
docs.text:
1. Save your code (Ctrl+S)
  2. Compile and upload (PROS: Upload)
  3. Place the robot on blocks (wheels off the ground)
  4. Turn on the V5 Controller and pair it with the Brain
  5. Run your program

docs.title: On-Blocks Test
docs.text:
With the robot on blocks:

  1. Push the left stick forward — left wheels should spin forward
  2. Push the right stick forward — right wheels should spin forward
  3. If a side spins backward, reverse its motor port numbers (add or remove the minus sign)
  4. Push both sticks forward — all wheels should spin in the same direction

📷 Photo Needed

Robot propped on blocks with wheels spinning freely for safe testing

docs.title: Floor Test
docs.text:
Once the on-blocks test passes:

  1. Place the robot on the floor
  2. Clear the area around you
  3. Drive forward — the robot should move in a straight line
  4. Turn — the robot should rotate smoothly
  5. Drive backward — it should reverse straight

First Floor Test Safety

  • Clear the area of people and obstacles
  • Start with gentle stick movements — don't slam full throttle
  • Be ready to pull sticks to neutral if something goes wrong
  • Have someone ready to grab the robot if it behaves unexpectedly

docs.title: Troubleshooting First Drive
docs.text: Problem Likely Cause Fix Robot spins in circles One side reversed Flip the sign on that side's port numbers Robot drives backward when pushing forward Both sides reversed Flip signs on all motor ports One side is weaker Motor not plugged in, bad cable, or wrong port number Check Brain device menu, check cables Robot veers left/right Mechanical friction (not code) Fix from Unit 1, or check that RPM config matches reality Nothing happens Program not running, controller not paired Check Brain screen, re-pair controller

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] LemLib needs motor ports, track width, wheel diameter, and RPM to configure your drivetrain
  • [x] Negative port numbers reverse motor direction — left side usually needs reversal
  • [x] Tank drive = two sticks, one per side. Arcade drive = one stick for throttle + turn.
  • [x] Always test on blocks before the floor
  • [x] The pros::delay(25) in the loop is mandatory — without it, the code hogs the CPU
  • [x] Calibrate the IMU in initialize() and keep the robot still during calibration

Next up: Module 2.3: Motorized Shovel — add a mechanism and learn button mapping.

docs.title: Module 2.3: Motorized Shovel
docs.text: A drivetrain that can only drive is not a robot — it's a remote-control car. In this module, you'll build a simple motorized shovel mechanism, wire it to the Brain, and program controller buttons to operate it. This prepares you for the Pickup Relay challenge.

docs.title: The Shovel Mechanism
docs.text: The shovel (or scoop) is a simple rotating mechanism attached to the front of your robot. It pivots up and down to pick up and carry objects.

docs.title: Design Requirements
docs.text:
Requirement Details Motor 1x 11W motor (red cartridge recommended — high torque for lifting) Range of motion ~90° pivot (from ground level to carrying position) Mounting Attached to the front of your drivetrain frame Scoop surface Wide enough to capture a bean bag or small ball

📷 Photo Needed

Completed shovel mechanism on drivetrain: motor, C-channel arm, scoop plate visible

docs.title: Build Tips
docs.text:
Keep It Simple

This is your first mechanism. Don't over-engineer it. A C-Channel arm with a flat scoop plate, driven by one motor, is all you need. You can always improve it later.

Basic construction:

  1. Mount the motor to the front of your drivetrain frame
  2. Attach a C-Channel arm to the motor output shaft
  3. Attach a flat plate or bent metal piece to the end of the arm as the scoop
  4. Ensure the arm can rotate from ground level to at least 45° above horizontal
  5. Add a rubber band or two for gravity assist (helps the motor lift against the weight)

📐 Diagram Needed

Side view: shovel at ground level (dotted) and carrying position (solid), arc arrow showing pivot

Motor Placement

Mount the motor so that the scoop hangs below the axle at rest (gravity pulls it down). This way, lifting is working against gravity, and lowering is assisted by gravity. The motor only needs to work hard in one direction.

📷 Photo Needed

11W motor mounted to front of frame, output shaft connected to C-channel arm

docs.title: Wiring the Motor
docs.text:
1. Connect the shovel motor to an available port on the V5 Brain (e.g., port 5)
  2. Note the port number — you'll need it in code
  3. Run the cable neatly along the frame and secure with zip ties

Cable Routing for Moving Parts

When routing cables to mechanisms that move, leave enough slack for the full range of motion. Secure the cable so it can't get caught in gears or pinched by the mechanism. Test the full motion range before zip-tying the final routing.

docs.title: Define the Motor
docs.text:
Add the shovel motor definition near the top of main.cpp, with your other motor definitions:

// Shovel motor on port 5
pros::Motor shovel_motor(5);

If the motor spins the wrong direction, use a negative port number:

pros::Motor shovel_motor(-5); // Reversed direction

docs.title: Button Mapping
docs.text: The V5 Controller has multiple buttons available for mechanism control:

docs.title: Available Buttons
docs.text:
Button Group Buttons Typical Use Shoulder buttons L1, L2, R1, R2 Primary mechanism control (most accessible) D-pad UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT Secondary controls, presets Face buttons A, B, X, Y Additional functions

📐 Diagram Needed

V5 controller with all buttons labeled: L1/L2, R1/R2, D-pad, A/B/X/Y, joysticks

docs.title: Reading Button State
docs.text:
// Returns true if button is currently pressed
controller.get_digital(pros::E_CONTROLLER_DIGITAL_R1)

// Returns true only on the frame the button is first pressed (rising edge)
controller.get_digital_new_press(pros::E_CONTROLLER_DIGITAL_R1)

docs.title: Control Modes: Hold vs. Toggle
docs.text: There are two common patterns for mechanism control. Choose the one that fits best.

docs.title: Hold Mode
docs.text:
The mechanism moves only while the button is held down. Release the button and it stops.

void opcontrol() {
    pros::Controller controller(pros::E_CONTROLLER_MASTER);

    while (true) {
        // Drive code (from Module 2.2)
        int throttle = controller.get_analog(pros::E_CONTROLLER_ANALOG_LEFT_Y);
        int turn = controller.get_analog(pros::E_CONTROLLER_ANALOG_RIGHT_X);
        chassis.arcade(throttle, turn);

        // Shovel control — HOLD MODE
        if (controller.get_digital(pros::E_CONTROLLER_DIGITAL_R1)) {
            shovel_motor.move(127);   // Lift up at full speed
        } else if (controller.get_digital(pros::E_CONTROLLER_DIGITAL_R2)) {
            shovel_motor.move(-127);  // Lower down at full speed
        } else {
            shovel_motor.move(0);     // Stop when no button pressed
        }

        pros::delay(25);
    }
}
Pros Cons Intuitive — press and hold Requires constant button pressure Immediate stop on release Ties up a finger during operation Easy to control speed (use partial values) Can be tiring during long matches

docs.title: Toggle Mode
docs.text:
Press a button once to start an action, press again (or a different button) to stop. The mechanism moves to a preset position and holds.

void opcontrol() {
    pros::Controller controller(pros::E_CONTROLLER_MASTER);
    bool shovel_up = false;

    while (true) {
        // Drive code
        int throttle = controller.get_analog(pros::E_CONTROLLER_ANALOG_LEFT_Y);
        int turn = controller.get_analog(pros::E_CONTROLLER_ANALOG_RIGHT_X);
        chassis.arcade(throttle, turn);

        // Shovel control — TOGGLE MODE
        if (controller.get_digital_new_press(pros::E_CONTROLLER_DIGITAL_R1)) {
            shovel_up = !shovel_up; // Flip the state
        }

        if (shovel_up) {
            shovel_motor.move_absolute(90, 100); // Move to 90° at speed 100
        } else {
            shovel_motor.move_absolute(0, 100);  // Move to 0° at speed 100
        }

        pros::delay(25);
    }
}
Pros Cons Press once, mechanism moves on its own Less fine-grained control Frees up your finger immediately Needs position tuning (the 90° value) Consistent positions every time Slightly more complex code

📐 Diagram Needed

Hold mode (pressed=run, released=stop) vs Toggle mode (press=move, press again=move back)

Which Mode to Use?

For the shovel, hold mode is recommended for beginners — it's simpler to code and easier to understand. Switch to toggle mode once you're comfortable with the basics and want more precise control.

docs.title: Brake Mode
docs.text:
When you stop sending power to a motor, it can either coast (spin freely) or brake (resist movement):

void initialize() {
    chassis.calibrate();

    // Set shovel motor to hold position when stopped
    shovel_motor.set_brake_mode(pros::E_MOTOR_BRAKE_HOLD);
}
Brake Mode Behavior Best For COAST Motor spins freely when stopped Flywheels, intakes BRAKE Motor resists movement when stopped General mechanisms HOLD Motor actively holds its position when stopped Arms, lifts, shovels

Use HOLD for the Shovel

BRAKE_HOLD makes the motor actively resist being moved, which keeps the shovel at whatever position you leave it. Without this, gravity will pull the shovel down when you release the button.

docs.title: On-Blocks Test
docs.text:
1. Upload your code
  2. With the robot on blocks, press R1 — the shovel should lift
  3. Press R2 — the shovel should lower
  4. Release both — the shovel should stop and hold position (if using HOLD brake mode)
  5. Check motor direction — if it's backwards, negate the port number

docs.title: Floor Test
docs.text:
1. Place the robot on the floor with a bean bag or ball in front
  2. Drive forward to the object
  3. Lower the shovel to ground level
  4. Drive forward to scoop the object
  5. Lift the shovel to carry the object
  6. Drive to a target area and lower to release

Practice Drill

Set up 3 objects (bean bags, balls, or game elements) in a line. Practice the full cycle:

  1. Drive to object
  2. Lower shovel
  3. Scoop object
  4. Lift shovel
  5. Drive to scoring zone
  6. Lower shovel to release
  7. Back up, drive to next object

Goal: Complete all 3 objects in under 60 seconds. This directly prepares you for the Pickup Relay challenge.

🎬 GIF/Video Needed

Full pickup cycle: approach → lower shovel → scoop → lift → drive → release

docs.title: Debugging Common Issues
docs.text: Problem Likely Cause Fix Motor doesn't move Wrong port number, cable unplugged Check Brain device menu, check cable Motor moves wrong direction Port polarity Negate the port number (add/remove minus sign) Shovel falls when button released Brake mode not set to HOLD Add set_brake_mode(pros::E_MOTOR_BRAKE_HOLD) in initialize() Motor stutters or stalls Mechanism binding, too much load Check mechanical build — is something jamming? Add rubber bands for assist. Button does nothing Wrong button constant in code Double-check the E_CONTROLLER_DIGITAL_ constant matches the physical button Shovel moves on drive input Code logic error — shovel code in wrong block Make sure shovel code is separate from drive code, not inside an if-else

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] A single motor with a simple arm creates a functional pick-up mechanism
  • [x] get_digital() reads current button state (for hold mode)
  • [x] get_digital_new_press() detects button press edges (for toggle mode)
  • [x] Brake mode HOLD keeps mechanisms in position when the motor stops
  • [x] Hold mode is simpler; toggle mode gives preset positions
  • [x] Always test on blocks first, then on the floor with real objects

Next up: Module 2.4: PID Tuning — make your robot drive straight and turn accurately.

docs.title: Module 2.4: PID Tuning
docs.text: PID is what separates robots that sort of work from robots that work consistently. In this module, you'll learn what PID is, why it matters, and how to tune LemLib's PID controllers so your robot drives straight and turns to exact angles.

docs.title: What Is PID?
docs.text: PID stands for Proportional, Integral, Derivative — three components that work together to move your robot to a target position smoothly and accurately.

docs.title: The Problem PID Solves
docs.text:
Without PID, if you tell a robot to drive 24 inches forward, one of two things happens:

  1. Too little power: The robot moves slowly and stops short of the target
  2. Too much power: The robot blasts past the target and overshoots

PID dynamically adjusts motor power based on how far the robot is from its target. Far away = lots of power. Getting close = ease off. Past the target = reverse a little.

docs.title: The Analogy
docs.text:
PID Is Like Parking a Car

Imagine pulling into a parking spot:

  • Far from the spot: You drive at normal speed (Proportional)
  • Getting close: You slow down so you don't overshoot (Derivative)
  • Stopped just short: You creep forward slowly to close the gap (Integral)

That's PID in a nutshell. Each term handles a different phase of reaching your target.

📐 Diagram Needed

Car approaching parking spot: far away (fast, P), getting close (slowing, D), last inch (creeping, I)

docs.title: P — Proportional
docs.text:
"How far am I from the target?"

The P term applies power proportional to the error (distance from target). Large error = large power. Small error = small power.

Power = kP × error
  • kP too low: Robot moves sluggishly, may never reach target
  • kP too high: Robot overshoots the target, oscillates back and forth
  • kP just right: Robot approaches target quickly but may still overshoot slightly

📐 Diagram Needed

Graph: position vs time with P-only — undershoot (kP low), oscillation (kP high), correct (kP good)

P Alone Is Rarely Enough

Pure proportional control usually causes oscillation — the robot overshoots, corrects, overshoots again, corrects again. That's where D comes in.

docs.title: D — Derivative
docs.text:
"How fast am I approaching the target?"

The D term applies a braking force proportional to the rate of change of the error. If the error is decreasing quickly (robot moving fast toward target), D slows it down to prevent overshoot.

Power = kP × error - kD × rate_of_change
  • kD too low: Doesn't prevent overshoot — oscillation continues
  • kD too high: Robot approaches target extremely slowly, feels sluggish
  • kD just right: Robot approaches target smoothly, minimal overshoot

📐 Diagram Needed

Graph: P+D control — kD too low (oscillates), too high (sluggish), just right (smooth)

docs.title: I — Integral
docs.text:
"Have I been stuck short of the target?"

The I term accumulates error over time. If the robot is consistently a small amount short of the target (steady-state error), the I term builds up power to close that last gap.

Power = kP × error + kI × accumulated_error - kD × rate_of_change
  • kI too low: Small steady-state error persists (robot stops 1 inch short)
  • kI too high: Power builds up and causes overshoot or oscillation
  • kI just right: Slowly eliminates steady-state error without causing instability

I Is Dangerous

The Integral term is the most likely to cause problems. For most VEX applications, kI should be 0 or very small. Only add I if you have a persistent steady-state error that P and D can't fix.

docs.title: PID in LemLib
docs.text:
LemLib has two separate PID controllers:

Controller What It Controls When It's Used Lateral Forward/backward movement Driving to a point, moving straight Angular Rotation/turning Turning to a heading, facing a point

Both are configured in the chassis constructor (you already set default values in Module 2.2). Now we'll tune them.

📐 Diagram Needed

Top-down robot: lateral PID (forward/back arrow) vs angular PID (rotation arc arrow)

docs.title: The Configuration Structure
docs.text:
lemlib::ControllerSettings lateral_controller(
    kP,              // Proportional gain
    kI,              // Integral gain
    kD,              // Derivative gain
    anti_windup,     // Anti-windup (limits I accumulation)
    small_error,     // Small error range (inches)
    small_timeout,   // Time to settle in small error range (ms)
    large_error,     // Large error range (inches)
    large_timeout,   // Time to settle in large error range (ms)
    slew             // Maximum power change per update (acceleration limit)
);

docs.title: Step 1: Tune Lateral PID (Straight Driving)
docs.text: Goal: Robot drives exactly 24 inches forward (6 tiles on the field, roughly 2 field tiles).

docs.title: Start with P Only
docs.text:
1. Set kI = 0 and kD = 0
  2. Start with a low kP value (e.g., kP = 5)
  3. Add a test movement to your autonomous function:
void autonomous() {
    chassis.moveToPoint(0, 24, 5000); // Move 24 inches forward, 5-second timeout
}
  1. Upload and run autonomous
  2. Measure how far the robot actually traveled

docs.title: P Tuning Table
docs.text:
Observation Adjustment Robot barely moves or stops way short Increase kP (try 2x) Robot reaches target but slowly kP is close — increase by 10-20% Robot overshoots and comes back kP is too high — decrease by 30-50% Robot oscillates back and forth kP is way too high — cut in half

Activity: Tune Lateral P

Target: Robot drives 24 inches forward and stops.

  1. Start with kP = 5
  2. Run autonomous, measure distance traveled
  3. Adjust kP based on the table above
  4. Repeat until the robot reaches approximately 24 inches (within 2-3 inches)
  5. Record your final P value

Expected range for most drivetrains: kP = 5–15

docs.title: Add D
docs.text:
Once P gets the robot close to the target:

  1. Keep your tuned kP value
  2. Start with kD = kP × 0.3 (rough starting point)
  3. Run the same 24-inch test
  4. Observe overshoot behavior
Observation Adjustment Still overshooting Increase kD by 25% Robot slows down too early, stops short Decrease kD by 25% Robot approaches smoothly, minimal overshoot You're close — fine-tune Robot vibrates/chatters near target kD is way too high — cut in half

Activity: Tune Lateral D

  1. Start with kD = kP × 0.3
  2. Run the 24-inch test
  3. Adjust based on overshoot behavior
  4. Goal: Robot drives 24 inches, decelerates smoothly, stops within 1 inch of target

docs.title: Add I (Only If Needed)
docs.text:
If the robot consistently stops 0.5–1 inch short of the target after tuning P and D:

  1. Start with kI = kP × 0.01 (very small)
  2. Set anti_windup = 3 (limits how much I can accumulate)
  3. Run the test
  4. If the steady-state error is eliminated, you're done
  5. If not, slowly increase kI

I Tuning Patience

Most LemLib users set kI to 0 and get excellent results. Only add I if you have a measurable, consistent shortfall that P and D cannot resolve. Adding unnecessary I usually makes things worse.

docs.title: Step 2: Tune Angular PID (Turning)
docs.text:
Goal: Robot turns exactly 90 degrees and stops.

The process is identical, but for the angular controller.

void autonomous() {
    chassis.turnToHeading(90, 5000); // Turn to 90° heading, 5-second timeout
}

docs.title: Angular Tuning Guide
docs.text:
1. Start with P only: kP = 2, kI = 0, kD = 0
  2. Run the 90-degree turn test
  3. Measure the actual angle turned (use the Brain's IMU readout or measure on the field)
  4. Tune P until the robot turns approximately 90 degrees
  5. Add D to eliminate overshoot
  6. Add I only if there's persistent steady-state error
Observation Adjustment Robot turns only ~45° Double kP Robot turns ~85° and stops (steady-state error) Increase kP slightly, or add small kI Robot turns ~100° and comes back kP is slightly high, add more kD Robot spins in circles kP is way too high — reduce to 1/4 value

Activity: Tune Angular PID

Target: Robot turns exactly 90 degrees from its starting heading.

  1. Mark the robot's starting position and heading on the floor with tape
  2. Run the turn command
  3. Measure the actual angle turned
  4. Adjust P, D, (I) following the same process as lateral tuning
  5. Goal: Robot turns 90° with less than 3° error

docs.title: Tuning Tips
docs.text:
Small Changes, Big Effects

When tuning PID, change one value at a time, and change it by small amounts (10-25%). Large jumps make it impossible to find the sweet spot.

Consistency Test

Run the same movement 5 times in a row. If the results are inconsistent (sometimes overshoots, sometimes undershoots), you likely have a mechanical problem (friction, loose parts, low battery) rather than a tuning problem.

Battery Level Matters

A fully charged battery delivers more power than a depleted one. Always tune with a fully charged battery, and re-test periodically as the battery drains. A well-tuned PID should handle moderate battery variation.

Document Your Values

Write down every set of PID values you test and the results. This prevents re-testing values you've already tried and helps you see the pattern of improvement.

docs.title: Tuning Log Template
docs.text: Attempt kP kI kD Target Actual Notes 1 5 0 0 24 in 18 in Way short, increase P 2 10 0 0 24 in 26 in Slight overshoot, add D 3 10 0 3 24 in 24.5 in Very close, good enough

docs.title: Final PID Values
docs.text:
Once you've tuned both controllers, update your chassis configuration with the final values:

lemlib::ControllerSettings lateral_controller(
    10,   // kP — your tuned value
    0,    // kI — probably 0
    3,    // kD — your tuned value
    3,    // anti-windup
    1,    // small error range (inches)
    100,  // small error timeout (ms)
    3,    // large error range (inches)
    500,  // large error timeout (ms)
    20    // slew
);

lemlib::ControllerSettings angular_controller(
    2,    // kP — your tuned value
    0,    // kI — probably 0
    10,   // kD — your tuned value
    3,    // anti-windup
    1,    // small error range (degrees)
    100,  // small error timeout (ms)
    3,    // large error range (degrees)
    500,  // large error timeout (ms)
    0     // slew
);

Save Your Values

Once you find good PID values, write them down somewhere permanent (engineering notebook, phone notes, comment in your code). Losing tuned PID values and having to re-tune from scratch is a painful waste of time.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] PID dynamically adjusts motor power based on distance from target
  • [x] P (Proportional) — power based on error magnitude
  • [x] D (Derivative) — braking based on approach speed
  • [x] I (Integral) — correction for persistent small errors (use sparingly)
  • [x] Tune in order: P first, then D, then I only if needed
  • [x] Change one value at a time, in small increments
  • [x] Test with a charged battery and consistent conditions
  • [x] Lateral PID controls straight driving; Angular PID controls turning

Next up: Module 2.5: Autonomous Routing — use your tuned PID to make the robot drive itself.

docs.title: Module 2.1: VSCode & LemLib Setup
docs.text: Before you write a single line of robot code, you need the right tools installed and working. This module walks you through setting up Visual Studio Code, the PROS extension, and creating your first LemLib project.

docs.title: Step 1: Install Visual Studio Code
docs.text: VSCode is a free, professional code editor made by Microsoft. It's what most software developers use, and it's what we use for VEX programming.

docs.title: Download and Install
docs.text:
1. Go to https://code.visualstudio.com
  2. Download the installer for your operating system (Windows, Mac, or Linux)
  3. Run the installer with default settings
  4. Launch VSCode when installation completes

Accept All Defaults

During installation, the default options are fine. On Windows, check the box for "Add to PATH" if it appears — this lets you open VSCode from the terminal.

docs.title: Step 2: Install the PROS Extension
docs.text: PROS (Purdue Robotics Operating System) is the development environment for V5 programming. The VSCode extension integrates PROS directly into your editor.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
1. Open VSCode
  2. Click the Extensions icon in the left sidebar (or press Ctrl+Shift+X)
  3. Search for "PROS"
  4. Find the extension by Purdue ACM SIGBots and click Install
  5. Wait for installation to complete — this may take a few minutes as it downloads the PROS toolchain

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

VSCode Extensions sidebar (Ctrl+Shift+X) with search bar visible

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

PROS extension in VSCode marketplace by Purdue ACM SIGBots, Install button visible

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
1. Open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P on Windows, Cmd+Shift+P on Mac)
  2. Type PROS — you should see several PROS commands appear
  3. If you see commands like PROS: New Project, the installation was successful

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

VSCode with PROS icon visible in the left sidebar activity bar

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P) with 'PROS' typed, showing available commands

Troubleshooting

If PROS commands don't appear:

  • Restart VSCode completely (close and reopen)
  • Check your internet connection — the toolchain needs to download
  • On Windows, try running VSCode as Administrator for the first install
  • Check the PROS extension output panel for error messages

docs.title: Step 3: Create a LemLib Project
docs.text: LemLib is installed as a template on top of a PROS project.

docs.title: Option A: Use the LemLib Template (Recommended)
docs.text:
1. Open a terminal in VSCode (Ctrl+`` or Terminal → New Terminal)
  2. Navigate to the folder where you want your project
  3. Run the PROS project creation command:
pros c n my-robot v5 --template lemlib

If that doesn't work (template not cached), use Option B.

docs.title: Option B: Create PROS Project + Add LemLib
docs.text:
1. Open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P)
  2. Run PROS: New Project
  3. Select a folder location for your project
  4. Choose V5 as the target platform
  5. Accept the latest kernel version

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

PROS New Project dialog showing V5 platform selection

  1. Once the project is created, add LemLib by running in the terminal:
pros c fetch lemlib
pros c apply lemlib

docs.title: Option C: Clone from GitHub
docs.text:
1. Go to the LemLib documentation site for the latest template link
  2. Clone or download the template repository
  3. Open the folder in VSCode

docs.title: Step 4: Understand the Project Structure
docs.text:
Once your project is created, you'll see this file structure:

my-robot/
├── include/
│   ├── api.h              ← PROS API header
│   ├── lemlib/             ← LemLib headers
│   │   ├── api.hpp         ← Main LemLib include
│   │   └── ...
│   ├── main.h             ← Your main header (includes everything)
│   └── pros/              ← PROS system headers
├── src/
│   └── main.cpp           ← YOUR CODE GOES HERE
├── Makefile               ← Build configuration
└── project.pros           ← PROS project metadata

The only file you'll edit for now is src/main.cpp. This is where all your robot code lives.

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

VSCode file explorer: include/, src/main.cpp, Makefile, project.pros with lemlib/ expanded

docs.title: The Structure of main.cpp
docs.text:
#include "main.h"

// Runs when the program starts (before competition control)
void initialize() {
    // Setup code: calibrate sensors, start tasks
}

// Runs during the autonomous period of a match
void autonomous() {
    // Autonomous driving code
}

// Runs during the driver control period of a match
void opcontrol() {
    // Driver control code — runs in a loop
    while (true) {
        // Read controller, move motors
        pros::delay(25); // Don't hog CPU — always delay in loops
    }
}

// Other functions: disabled(), competition_initialize()
// (you won't need these yet)

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

main.cpp with syntax highlighting showing initialize(), autonomous(), opcontrol() functions

The Three Core Functions

Function When It Runs What To Put Here initialize() Once, at program start Sensor calibration, LemLib setup autonomous() During 15-second auton period Automated driving routines opcontrol() During 1:45 driver control period Controller input handling

docs.title: Step 5: Connect to the V5 Brain
USB Connection
docs.text:
1. Connect your computer to the V5 Brain using a USB-A to USB-C cable
  2. Power on the V5 Brain
  3. VSCode should detect the Brain automatically through PROS

📷 Photo Needed

USB cable connecting V5 Brain to laptop

docs.title: Verify Connection
docs.text:
1. Open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P)
  2. Run PROS: List Devices
  3. You should see your V5 Brain listed with its port

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

PROS List Devices terminal output showing detected V5 Brain

USB Port Issues

If the Brain isn't detected:

  • Try a different USB cable (some cables are charge-only, not data)
  • Try a different USB port on your computer
  • Make sure the Brain is powered on
  • On Windows, you may need to install V5 drivers (install VEXcode once to get drivers, then you can go back to PROS)

docs.title: Step 6: First Upload
docs.text: Before writing any real code, verify that the full pipeline works: write code, compile, upload, run.

docs.title: The Test
docs.text:
Your main.cpp should already have the basic structure from project creation. We'll upload it as-is.

  1. Save main.cpp (Ctrl+S)
  2. Open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P)
  3. Run PROS: Upload (or click the upload button in the PROS sidebar)
  4. Wait for compilation and upload to complete
  5. You should see Upload successful in the terminal

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Terminal showing 'Upload successful' message after PROS upload

docs.title: Verify on the Brain
docs.text:
1. On the V5 Brain screen, navigate to Programs
  2. You should see your program listed
  3. Run it — nothing visible will happen (the code doesn't do anything yet), but it should start without errors

📷 Photo Needed

V5 Brain screen showing Programs menu with uploaded program

If Upload Succeeds

Congratulations — your development environment is fully set up. You can write code on your computer, compile it, upload it to the robot, and run it. This is the pipeline you'll use for every future module.

If Upload Fails

Common fixes:

  • Compilation errors: Read the error message. It tells you the file, line number, and what's wrong. Usually a typo.
  • Upload timeout: Check USB connection. Try a different cable or port.
  • "No device found": Brain isn't connected. Check cable, power, and PROS device list.
  • Permission denied (Linux/Mac): You may need to add your user to the dialout group.

docs.title: Recommended Extensions (Beyond PROS)
docs.text: Extension Purpose C/C++ (Microsoft) Syntax highlighting, IntelliSense for C++ Error Lens Shows errors inline in your code (highly recommended)

docs.title: Keyboard Shortcuts to Learn
docs.text: Shortcut Action Ctrl+S Save file Ctrl+Shift+P Command Palette Ctrl+`` Toggle terminal Ctrl+/ Comment/uncomment line Ctrl+Z Undo Ctrl+Shift+Z Redo

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• [x] VSCode + PROS extension is your development environment
  • [x] LemLib is installed as a template on top of PROS
  • [x] src/main.cpp is where your code lives — focus on initialize(), autonomous(), and opcontrol()
  • [x] Always test the upload pipeline before writing real code
  • [x] Read error messages — they tell you exactly what's wrong
  • [x] Save constantly, test incrementally

Before Moving On

Make sure you can: open your project in VSCode, compile it without errors, upload it to the Brain, and run it. If any of these steps fail, fix them now. Every future module depends on this pipeline working.

Next up: Module 2.2: Drive Config & Controls — make your robot actually move.

docs.title: Unit 3: Onshape CAD
docs.text: Design it before you build it. Onshape CAD is the tool competitive robotics teams use to design, iterate, and communicate their robot designs before a single piece of metal is cut. Unit 3 takes you from zero CAD experience to designing full VEX robot assemblies — all in the browser.

docs.title: Unit Overview
docs.text: Details Modules 5 Field Challenges 3 Estimated Time 10–14 hours Final Deliverable A fully modeled, rendered drivetrain assembly with mates and gear relations

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
By the end of Unit 3, you will be able to:

  • [x] Navigate the Onshape interface confidently
  • [x] Create fully constrained 2D sketches
  • [x] Use extrude, fillet, chamfer, and wrap features to create 3D parts
  • [x] Design parts specifically for 3D printing
  • [x] Import and use the VEX V5 Parts Library
  • [x] Assemble parts using Fastened, Revolute, and Tangent mates
  • [x] Use gear relations and replicate to build efficient assemblies
  • [x] Render your designs for documentation and presentation

docs.title: Module Roadmap
docs.text: Module Title Key Skills 3.1 Interface & Sketching Account setup, navigation, sketch planes, constraints, dimensions 3.2 3D Modeling & Printing Extrude, fillet, chamfer, text, designing for 3D printing 3.3 Advanced Features Wrap feature on flat, curved, and spherical surfaces 3.4 VEX Parts & Mates Parts library, fastened/revolute/tangent mates, gear relations, replicate 3.5 Render & Present Render Studio, exporting screenshots, presenting your design

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text:
After completing all five modules, prove your CAD skills:

Challenge What It Tests 3D Print Keychain Creative design, 3D printing constraints, feature usage CAD Battle Speed modeling, measurement accuracy, feature selection CAD vs Real Design-to-reality accuracy, tolerance understanding, iteration

docs.title: Lesson System
docs.text:
All CAD lessons are delivered through the Onshape Classes system. You will find every lesson and challenge under:

Classes > V5 CT Education

Each lesson contains tutorial videos, guided activities, and checkpoints. Follow along step by step — pausing and rewinding is encouraged.

Why Onshape?

Onshape runs entirely in your browser. No downloads, no installations, no license fees. Your work is saved automatically in the cloud, and you can access it from any computer. This is the same tool used by professional engineering teams and top-tier VEX teams worldwide.

docs.title: Tips for Success
docs.text:
CAD Is a Skill, Not a Talent

Nobody is born knowing how to CAD. The students who get good at it are the ones who practice deliberately — not just following tutorials, but experimenting on their own. After each lesson, spend 10 minutes building something that wasn't assigned.

Constraints Are Your Best Friend

A fully constrained sketch turns black in Onshape. If your sketch lines are still blue, you have unconstrained geometry. Always fully constrain your sketches before extruding — it prevents unexpected behavior later.

docs.title: Let's Get Started
docs.text: Begin with Module 3.1: Interface & Sketching — you need to know your way around Onshape before you can design anything.

docs.title: Module 3.2: 3D Modeling & Printing
docs.text: Sketches are just the beginning. In this module, you will learn to turn 2D sketches into real 3D parts using the Extrude feature, then design a custom keychain that you will actually 3D print and take home.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Use the Extrude feature to create 3D geometry from a sketch
  • Apply Extrude-Cut to remove material from a part
  • Add fillets and chamfers to round or bevel edges
  • Use the Text tool to add lettering to a part
  • Design a part optimized for 3D printing
  • Understand overhangs, supports, and wall thickness

docs.title: Lesson 3: Extrude Feature
docs.text: Open Lesson 3 in Classes > V5 CT Education.

docs.title: What Is Extrude?
docs.text: Extrude takes a 2D sketch and pushes it into the third dimension. A rectangle becomes a box. A circle becomes a cylinder. A complex profile becomes a complex solid.

docs.title: Extrude Options
docs.text:
Option What It Does New Creates a brand new solid body Add Adds material to an existing body Remove Cuts material away from an existing body Intersect Keeps only the overlapping volume Blind Extrudes a specific distance Up to face Extrudes until it hits a selected face Through all Extrudes through the entire part Symmetric Extrudes equally in both directions from the sketch plane

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Extrude dialog: New/Add/Remove/Intersect options, Blind/Symmetric depth

docs.title: Activity: Follow the Tutorial, Then Make Your Own
docs.text:
1. Follow the Lesson 3 tutorial video exactly — build the sample part
  2. When complete, start a new document
  3. Design your own simple part using at least:
    • One sketch with full constraints
    • One Add extrude
    • One Remove extrude (a hole or cutout)

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Side-by-side: Extrude Add (adding material) vs Remove (cutting a hole)

Extrude Direction Matters

Pay attention to the arrow direction when extruding. You can flip it by clicking the arrow or entering a negative value. Getting the direction wrong is one of the most common beginner mistakes — and it is easy to miss if you are not looking at the model from the right angle.

docs.title: Lesson 4: Designing to 3D Print — Nametag Keychain
docs.text:
Open Lesson 4 in Classes > V5 CT Education.

This is your first real design project. You will create a Nametag Keychain that will be 3D printed and is yours to keep.

This Keychain Is Yours to Keep

This is not just a learning exercise — you are designing a real, physical object that you will hold in your hand. Make it something you are proud of.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Your keychain must use at least 3 of the following features (aim for all 5):

Feature What It Does Example Use Constraints Fully defined sketch geometry Centering your name on the keychain Extrude-Cut Remove material Keychain hole, decorative cutouts Text Add lettering Your name or initials Fillet Round edges Smooth corners so it is comfortable to hold Chamfer Bevel edges at an angle Decorative edge treatment

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Same edge with fillet (rounded) vs chamfer (beveled) applied

docs.title: Design Steps
docs.text:
1. Create a new sketch on the Top plane
  2. Draw the outline shape of your keychain (rectangle, rounded rectangle, custom shape)
  3. Add a circle for the keychain ring hole (at least 4mm diameter)
  4. Extrude the base shape (3–5mm thick works well for 3D printing)
  5. Add text using the Text tool — sketch your name on the top face

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Text tool typing on a part face, text sketch visible

  1. Extrude the text either up (raised lettering) or down (engraved lettering)
  2. Apply fillets to any sharp corners
  3. Apply chamfers for style if desired

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Finished keychain in Onshape with text, fillets, and keychain hole

docs.title: Designing for 3D Printing
docs.text: Not everything that looks good in CAD prints well. Here are the critical rules for designing parts that will actually print successfully.

docs.title: Wall Thickness
docs.text:
Guideline Minimum Recommended Wall thickness 0.8mm 1.2mm+ Feature detail 0.4mm 0.6mm+ Text height 5mm 8mm+ Text depth/raise 0.5mm 1mm+

Thin Walls Break

If your walls are thinner than 0.8mm, the 3D printer may not be able to print them at all, or they will snap off immediately. When in doubt, make it thicker.

docs.title: Overhangs and Supports
docs.text:
• Overhangs are surfaces that angle outward with nothing below them
  • Angles less than 45 degrees from vertical generally print fine without support
  • Angles greater than 45 degrees need support material, which adds print time and leaves rough surfaces
  • Best practice: design your part so it sits flat on the print bed with no overhangs

📐 Diagram Needed

Overhang angles: under 45° (OK, no support) vs over 45° (needs support)

docs.title: Bridge Distance
docs.text:
• A bridge is a horizontal span between two supports
  • Short bridges (under 10mm) print fine
  • Long bridges sag — redesign to avoid them or add supports

docs.title: Orientation Matters
docs.text:
The direction your part sits on the print bed affects:

  • Strength — parts are weakest between layers (Z-axis)
  • Surface quality — the bottom face touching the bed is the smoothest
  • Overhang requirements — rotating 90 degrees can eliminate overhangs entirely

docs.title: Activity: Design and Print Your Keychain
docs.text:
1. Complete Lesson 4 tutorial
  2. Design your custom keychain using at least 3 of the 5 features listed above
  3. Have your coach review the design for printability
  4. Export as STL (right-click the Part > Export > STL)
  5. Slice and print (your coach will help with slicer settings)

docs.title: Design Checklist
docs.text:
Before exporting, verify:

  • [ ] Keychain hole is at least 4mm diameter
  • [ ] Base thickness is 3–5mm
  • [ ] No walls thinner than 1mm
  • [ ] Text is large enough to read (8mm+ height recommended)
  • [ ] No unsupported overhangs
  • [ ] All sketches fully constrained
  • [ ] Part sits flat on a surface (no rocking)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Extrude is the most-used feature in CAD — master Add, Remove, and Symmetric modes
  2. Fillets and chamfers turn rough designs into polished ones — use them generously
  3. 3D printing has physical constraints — wall thickness, overhangs, and orientation all matter
  4. Design with manufacturing in mind — the best CAD model in the world is useless if it cannot be built

docs.title: Next Up
docs.text: Module 3.3: Advanced Features — learn the Wrap feature to apply graphics and logos to curved surfaces.

docs.title: Module 3.3: Advanced Features
docs.text: Flat surfaces are easy. Curved surfaces are where CAD gets interesting. The Wrap feature lets you apply sketches, logos, and text onto any surface — flat, cylindrical, or spherical. This module teaches you three different wrapping techniques and puts them to the test with a creative challenge.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Use the Wrap feature to emboss or deboss on flat surfaces
  • Wrap sketches onto cylindrical (curved) surfaces
  • Wrap sketches onto spherical surfaces
  • Combine multiple features to create complex, detailed parts
  • Complete Challenge 1 in the V5 CT Education class

docs.title: Lesson 5: Wrap Feature
docs.text: Open Lesson 5 in Classes > V5 CT Education.

docs.title: What Is Wrap?
docs.text:
The Wrap feature takes a 2D sketch and projects it onto a 3D surface, then adds or removes material along that surface. Think of it like applying a sticker to a curved object — except the sticker becomes part of the object itself.

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Wrap feature highlighted in feature toolbar

docs.title: Wrap vs Extrude
docs.text:
Feature Best For Surface Type Extrude Adding/removing material in a straight line Flat surfaces only Wrap Adding/removing material that follows a surface contour Flat, curved, or spherical

If you try to extrude text onto a cylinder, it will cut straight through at weird angles. Wrap follows the curvature of the surface, giving you a clean result every time.

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Extrude through cylinder (wrong, cuts straight) vs Wrap (correct, follows curve)

docs.title: Walkthrough 1: Flat Surface Wrap
docs.text: The simplest use of Wrap — applying a sketch to a flat face.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Create a box (sketch rectangle, extrude)
  2. Start a new sketch on the top face of the box
  3. Draw or type the content you want to wrap (text, logo, pattern)
  4. Exit the sketch
  5. Select Wrap from the feature toolbar
  6. Choose your sketch as the source
  7. Select the top face as the target surface
  8. Set the wrap type:
    • Add — raises the sketch out of the surface (emboss)
    • Remove — cuts the sketch into the surface (deboss)
  9. Set the depth (0.5–1mm works well for detail work)

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Flat wrap: box with sketch → Wrap dialog → embossed result

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Wrap dialog: Add (emboss) vs Remove (deboss) options with depth setting

When to Use Wrap on Flat Surfaces

You might wonder why not just use Extrude for flat surfaces. The answer: Wrap handles complex sketch geometry (especially imported SVGs and text with curves) more reliably than Extrude-Add on certain face configurations. If Extrude gives you trouble with text on a face, try Wrap instead.

docs.title: Walkthrough 2: Curved Surface Wrap
docs.text: Wrapping onto a cylinder — like adding a label to a can.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Create a cylinder (sketch circle, extrude)
  2. Start a new sketch on the Front plane (not on the cylinder face)
  3. Draw your content — text, shapes, logos
  4. Position the sketch so it overlaps where you want the wrap to appear on the cylinder
  5. Exit the sketch
  6. Select Wrap
  7. Choose your sketch as the source
  8. Select the curved face of the cylinder as the target surface
  9. The sketch will bend to follow the curvature automatically
  10. Set Add or Remove and specify depth

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Curved wrap on cylinder: sketch on Front plane → wrapped result

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text: Problem Fix Wrap fails with error Make sure your sketch does not extend beyond the edges of the target face Text appears mirrored Check which side of the plane your sketch is on — flip if needed Depth looks wrong For curved surfaces, smaller depths (0.3–0.5mm) look better than large ones

docs.title: Walkthrough 3: Sphere Wrap
docs.text: The most advanced wrap — applying a sketch to a spherical surface.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Create a sphere (sketch circle on Front plane, use Revolve feature to spin it 360 degrees around a center axis)
  2. Start a new sketch on the Front plane
  3. Draw your content — keep it simple for spheres (a star, initials, simple logo)
  4. Position the sketch so it overlaps the sphere where you want the wrap
  5. Exit the sketch
  6. Select Wrap
  7. Choose your sketch as the source
  8. Select the spherical face as the target
  9. Set depth and wrap type

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Sphere wrap: Revolve sphere → sketch on Front plane → wrapped result

Sphere Wrap Limitations

Wrapping onto a sphere distorts your sketch geometry — straight lines become curved, and shapes near the edges stretch more than shapes near the center. Keep your wrap content small relative to the sphere size and centered on the nearest point of the sphere for best results.

docs.title: Feature Combinations
docs.text: The real power of CAD comes from combining features. Here are some patterns you should practice:

docs.title: Embossed Logo on a Curved Handle
docs.text:
1. Extrude a grip shape
  2. Add fillets for ergonomics
  3. Wrap a logo onto the curved surface
  4. Add material with Wrap-Add for a raised logo

docs.title: Engraved Serial Number on a Flat Plate
docs.text:
1. Extrude a plate
  2. Chamfer the edges
  3. Wrap text with Wrap-Remove for engraved numbering

docs.title: Textured Sphere
docs.text:
1. Create a sphere with Revolve
  2. Wrap a pattern (grid, dots, lines) onto the surface
  3. Use Wrap-Remove for a textured golf-ball effect

docs.title: Challenge 1: Tennis Racquet and Ball
docs.text: Navigate to Classes > V5 CT Education > Challenge 1.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Design a tennis racquet and ball, both featuring wrapped logos.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Tennis racquet with a handle, frame, and string pattern
  • Tennis ball (sphere) with a logo or text wrapped onto its surface
  • At least one flat wrap and one curved/spherical wrap must be used
  • Both objects should be in the same document but can be separate Part Studios

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Example completed tennis racquet and ball with wrapped logos

docs.title: Judging Criteria
docs.text: Criteria Weight Correct use of Wrap feature 30% Geometric accuracy (does it look like a racquet and ball?) 25% Clean modeling (no errors, no extra features) 20% Creative logo/text design 15% Fully constrained sketches 10%

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Start with the ball — it is simpler and builds confidence for the racquet
  • For the racquet strings, use a sketch pattern of lines, then Wrap-Remove them from the racquet head
  • The handle is a great place to practice the curved surface wrap
  • Keep your logos simple — complex SVGs often cause Wrap to fail on curved surfaces

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Wrap follows surface contour — unlike Extrude, which cuts in a straight line
  2. Three surface types — flat, curved, and spherical all work with Wrap
  3. Keep it simple on curves — complex geometry distorts more on curved/spherical surfaces
  4. Combine features — the best designs use Extrude, Fillet, Chamfer, and Wrap together

docs.title: Next Up
docs.text: Module 3.4: VEX Parts & Mates — import real VEX parts and learn to assemble them with proper mates and gear relations.

docs.title: Challenge: 3D Print Keychain
docs.text: Design something real, print something yours. This challenge puts your Onshape skills to the test by asking you to design a custom keychain from scratch. The best part? You get to 3D print it and take it home.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Design and 3D print a personalized keychain that showcases your CAD skills. This is a creative design challenge — there is no single correct answer.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Your keychain must include all of the following:

Requirement Details Your name or initials Use the Text tool — must be legible on the printed part At least one fillet or chamfer Smooth or bevel at least two edges A creative shape Not a plain rectangle — show personality in the outline Keychain hole At least 4mm diameter, positioned for a keyring Printable dimensions No larger than 60mm x 40mm x 6mm

docs.title: Feature Checklist
docs.text:
Use at least 4 of these features in your design:

  • [ ] Fully constrained sketch with dimensions
  • [ ] Extrude-Add (base shape)
  • [ ] Extrude-Remove (cutouts, holes, engraving)
  • [ ] Text tool
  • [ ] Fillet (rounded edges)
  • [ ] Chamfer (beveled edges)
  • [ ] Wrap (optional — for curved sections)
  • [ ] Mirror or pattern (optional — for symmetry)

docs.title: Shape Ideas
docs.text:
Do not just make a rectangle with your name on it. Consider:

  • Silhouette shapes — a gear, a robot, a shield, your state outline
  • Rounded forms — use arcs and fillets for an organic feel
  • Geometric patterns — hexagons, interlocking shapes, tessellations
  • Functional shapes — a bottle opener keychain, a phone stand, a mini tool

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Grid of 4-6 example keychain shapes (gear, robot, shield, hexagon) in Onshape

docs.title: Text Tips
docs.text:
• Minimum text height: 8mm for readability after printing
  • Raised text (Extrude-Add) is easier to read than engraved text on small parts
  • If using engraved text, set depth to at least 0.8mm
  • Sans-serif fonts print cleaner than serif fonts at small sizes

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Raised text (Extrude-Add) vs engraved text (Extrude-Remove) on keychain

docs.title: Printability Reminders
docs.text:
• Minimum wall thickness: 1.2mm
  • No unsupported overhangs greater than 45 degrees
  • The keychain hole needs a bridge across the top if it is near the edge — make sure there is at least 2mm of material around it
  • Flat bottom surface = best print quality

📐 Diagram Needed

Keychain with callouts: wall thickness, hole diameter, overhang areas, flat bottom

docs.title: Step 1: Sketch It on Paper First (5 minutes)
docs.text: Before opening Onshape, sketch your idea on paper. This is faster than iterating in CAD and helps you make creative decisions without getting bogged down in tool mechanics.

docs.title: Step 2: Model It in Onshape (30-45 minutes)
docs.text:
1. Create a new document named [YourName]-Keychain
  2. Build your design using the techniques from Modules 3.1 and 3.2
  3. Fully constrain all sketches
  4. Apply fillets/chamfers last (they are easier to modify if applied at the end)

docs.title: Step 3: Coach Review (5 minutes)
docs.text:
Before printing, have your coach review for:

  • Printability (wall thickness, overhangs, dimensions)
  • Feature usage (did you meet the requirements?)
  • Structural integrity (will it survive being on a keyring?)

docs.title: Step 4: Export and Print
docs.text:
1. Right-click the Part in the feature tree
  2. Select Export > STL
  3. Use default settings (Fine resolution)
  4. Hand the STL file to your coach for slicing and printing

docs.title: Step 5: Post-Print
docs.text:
• Remove support material if any
  • Light sanding if needed
  • Thread a keyring through the hole
  • It is yours to keep

docs.title: Judging
docs.text:
This Is Not a Competition — It Is a Celebration

Everyone gets to print their keychain. There are no losers. The class vote is just for fun and to celebrate creative design thinking.

docs.title: Class Vote Categories
docs.text: Category What It Means Most Creative Shape The most interesting or unexpected outline Best Use of Features The design that uses the most CAD features effectively Cleanest Print The design that printed the best (good design-for-manufacturing) Most Likely to Actually Use The keychain you would actually put on your keys

docs.title: How Voting Works
docs.text:
1. All printed keychains are displayed on a table
  2. Each student gets 2 votes (you cannot vote for yourself)
  3. Votes are anonymous
  4. Winners are announced and recognized — bragging rights only

docs.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
docs.text: Mistake Why It Is a Problem Fix Text too small Unreadable after printing Use 8mm+ height Walls too thin Breaks during or after printing 1.2mm minimum No keychain hole Cannot attach to a keyring Add 4mm+ hole Unconstrained sketch Part dimensions change unexpectedly Fully constrain before extruding Too complex for first design Frustration, long print time Start simple, add detail if time allows

docs.title: Deliverables
docs.text:
• [ ] Onshape document with completed keychain design
  • [ ] All sketches fully constrained
  • [ ] At least 4 features used from the checklist
  • [ ] STL exported and reviewed by coach
  • [ ] Printed keychain in hand

docs.title: Next Challenge
docs.text: CAD Battle — can you model a mystery object under time pressure?

docs.title: Challenge: CAD Battle
docs.text: Speed. Accuracy. Clean modeling. The CAD Battle tests your ability to observe a physical object, measure it, and reproduce it in Onshape as fast and accurately as possible. No tutorials. No hand-holding. Just you, a mystery object, and a 15-minute clock.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Given a mystery physical object revealed by the coach, be the first to accurately model it in Onshape. Fastest accurate model wins.

docs.title: Format
docs.text: Details Type Individual, timed Duration 15 minutes Rounds 1-3 (coach's discretion, escalating difficulty) Tools Allowed Onshape, calipers, ruler Tools NOT Allowed Importing existing models, copying from classmates

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
1. All students open a new, blank Onshape document before the round starts
  2. Name it CAD-Battle-[YourName]-Round[X]
  3. Calipers and rulers are on the table
  4. Do not touch the mystery object until the coach says go

docs.title: The Reveal
docs.text:
1. Coach places the mystery object in the center of the table
  2. Coach says "Go" and starts the 15-minute timer
  3. Students may approach the object, measure it, photograph it, and handle it
  4. Only one student may measure the object at a time — form a line if needed, be respectful

docs.title: Mystery Object Types
docs.text:
The coach will select from objects of increasing difficulty:

Difficulty Example Objects Easy A VEX standoff, a simple L-bracket, a spacer stack Medium A VEX gear, a motor cartridge, a custom 3D printed part Hard A household item (mug, stapler), a multi-feature VEX assembly

📷 Photo Needed

Example mystery objects at each difficulty: standoff, gear, household item

docs.title: During the Battle
docs.text:
• You may measure the object as many times as needed
  • You may take photos of the object with your phone for reference
  • You may not communicate with other students about dimensions or strategy
  • You may not search the internet for existing models of the object
  • Work in a single Part Studio — no imports

docs.title: Submission
docs.text:
1. When you are satisfied with your model, raise your hand
  2. Coach notes your finish time
  3. You may continue to refine until the 15-minute mark
  4. At 15 minutes, all work stops — hands off keyboards

docs.title: Judging Criteria
docs.text:
Each submission is scored out of 100 points:

Criteria Points What Judges Look For Dimensional Accuracy 40 Key dimensions within 1mm of the real object Feature Accuracy 25 Correct features used (holes, fillets, chamfers match the real object) Clean Modeling 20 No extra sketches, no failed features, organized feature tree Speed Bonus 15 Points awarded inversely by finish time (first = 15pts, second = 12pts, etc.)

docs.title: Accuracy Measurement
docs.text:
The coach will compare your model to the real object by checking:

  • Overall length, width, and height
  • Hole diameters and positions
  • Fillet/chamfer radii
  • Feature count (did you miss any holes, cuts, or details?)

Accuracy Tolerance

Dimensions within 1mm of the actual measurement receive full credit. Dimensions within 2mm receive half credit. Anything beyond 2mm receives no credit for that dimension.

docs.title: The Winning Approach
docs.text:
Measure First, Sketch Second, Extrude Last

The biggest mistake in CAD Battle is rushing to Onshape before understanding the object. Spend the first 2-3 minutes measuring and noting key dimensions on paper. A student who measures for 3 minutes and models for 12 minutes will almost always beat a student who starts modeling immediately and has to remeasure repeatedly.

docs.title: Measurement Priority
docs.text:
1. Bounding box — overall length, width, height
  2. Primary features — large holes, main cutouts, dominant geometry
  3. Secondary features — small holes, fillets, chamfers, details
  4. Fine details — only if you have time remaining

docs.title: Modeling Priority
docs.text:
1. Base sketch — the main cross-section shape, fully constrained
  2. Primary extrude — get the 3D volume correct
  3. Major cuts — large holes, main cutouts
  4. Fillets and chamfers — add these last, they are easy to apply quickly
  5. Fine details — only if ahead of the clock

docs.title: Time Management
docs.text:
Phase Time Focus Measure 0:00 - 2:30 All critical dimensions, written on paper Base model 2:30 - 7:00 Main shape and primary features Details 7:00 - 12:00 Holes, cuts, secondary features Polish 12:00 - 15:00 Fillets, chamfers, clean up feature tree

📐 Diagram Needed

Timeline: Measure (0-2:30) → Base Model (2:30-7:00) → Details (7:00-12:00) → Polish (12:00-15:00)

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text: Mistake Consequence Prevention Starting to model before measuring Constant rework, wasted time Write dimensions on paper first Modeling features in the wrong order Later features break earlier ones Big shapes first, details last Forgetting to constrain sketches Dimensions shift when adding features Constrain and dimension every sketch fully Over-detailing early Run out of time before capturing major features Get the big picture right first Guessing dimensions instead of measuring Inaccurate model, low score Always measure — never eyeball

docs.title: Awards
docs.text: Award Given To Speed Demon Fastest submission with 70+ accuracy score Precision Master Highest accuracy score regardless of time CAD Battle Champion Highest total score (accuracy + speed)

docs.title: Next Challenge
docs.text: CAD vs Real — build the same mechanism in CAD and with real VEX parts, then compare.

docs.title: Challenge: CAD vs Real
docs.text: The moment of truth — does your design survive reality? In this challenge, you will build the same mechanism twice: once in Onshape and once with real VEX parts. Then you compare them side by side. The gap between your CAD model and your physical build reveals exactly how well you understand design, tolerances, and real-world constraints.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Build the same mechanism in both Onshape CAD and physical VEX parts. The team whose physical build most closely matches their CAD model wins.

docs.title: Format
docs.text: Details Type Individual or pairs (coach's discretion) Duration 60–90 minutes total Phase 1 CAD modeling (30 minutes) Phase 2 Physical build (30-45 minutes) Phase 3 Comparison and judging (15 minutes)

docs.title: The Assignment
docs.text:
