The coach assigns a simple mechanism that can be both CAD modeled and physically built within the time limit. Examples:

Difficulty Mechanism Key Features Easy Geared axle assembly Two gears on axles, mounted in C-channels, specific ratio Medium 4-bar linkage Four bars with revolute joints, specific bar lengths Hard Geared arm with mount Arm on pivot, gear-driven, mounted to a base plate

Everyone Gets the Same Assignment

All students/pairs receive the same mechanism. This ensures fair comparison and creates interesting discussion about why different people interpreted the same design differently.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
1. Create a new Onshape document named CAD-vs-Real-[YourName]
  2. Use the VEX V5 Parts Library — import real VEX parts, do not model them from scratch
  3. Assemble with proper mates (Fastened, Revolute as appropriate)
  4. The mechanism must be fully defined — no floating parts
  5. Take a screenshot of your completed CAD model from a 3/4 isometric view

docs.title: CAD Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] All parts imported from the VEX library (not custom-modeled)
  • [ ] First part fastened to origin
  • [ ] All connections properly mated
  • [ ] Mechanism moves correctly when animated (if it has moving parts)
  • [ ] Screenshot saved for comparison
  • [ ] Assembly is clean — no extra unused parts

docs.title: What to Document
docs.text:
Before moving to Phase 2, write down:

  • Exact parts used (e.g., "2x 2x10 C-Channel, 4x 8-32 x 0.5" screws, 2x bearings")
  • Key dimensions (distances between holes, axle positions)
  • Gear ratio if applicable
  • Any specific spacing (number of spacers, standoff lengths)

Your CAD Model Is Your Blueprint

Treat Phase 1 like creating a build guide. The more precise your CAD model, the easier Phase 2 will be. Students who rush through CAD always struggle more during the physical build.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
1. Build the exact mechanism you modeled in Phase 1
  2. Use the same parts you specified in your CAD model
  3. Follow your CAD model as your build guide
  4. When complete, place the physical build next to your screen showing the CAD model

docs.title: Build Tips
docs.text:
• Count holes carefully — the most common error is being off by one hole
  • Measure spacing with calipers if needed
  • If a part does not fit the way your CAD shows, you have found a gap between design and reality — note it
  • Screw joints should be firm but allow rotation where the CAD has revolute mates
  • Use nylock nuts where the CAD shows permanent connections

docs.title: What to Track
docs.text:
As you build, note every discrepancy between your CAD model and reality:

Discrepancy Type Example Part interference Two parts collide that did not collide in CAD Spacing issues Axle is too tight or too loose — spacer count was wrong Alignment problems Holes do not line up because of accumulated tolerance Missing hardware Forgot to include standoffs or spacers in the CAD model Functional failure Mechanism does not move smoothly despite looking correct

📷 Photo Needed

Physical build with callouts showing discrepancies: interference, spacing, misaligned holes

docs.title: Side-by-Side Comparison
docs.text:
1. Place your physical build next to your laptop showing the CAD model

📷 Photo Needed

Physical VEX build next to laptop showing matching CAD model

  1. The coach and peers will walk around examining each pair
  2. Be prepared to explain any differences between your CAD and physical build

docs.title: Judging Criteria
docs.text:
Criteria Points What Judges Look For Visual Match 30 Does the physical build look like the CAD model from the same angle? Dimensional Match 25 Are key dimensions the same? (hole spacing, axle positions, overall size) Part Match 20 Same exact parts used in both? No substitutions? Functionality 15 Does the physical mechanism move the same way the CAD animation shows? Documentation 10 Did the student track and explain discrepancies?

Honesty Earns Points

The documentation score rewards students who honestly identified and explained the gaps between their CAD and physical build. Pretending everything is perfect when it clearly is not earns zero documentation points. The best engineers are the ones who can articulate what went wrong and why.

docs.title: The Design-Reality Gap
docs.text:
Every engineer encounters this gap. CAD models assume:

  • Perfect dimensions — real parts have manufacturing tolerances
  • Zero friction — real joints have friction that affects motion
  • Infinite rigidity — real structures flex under load
  • Exact spacing — real assemblies accumulate small errors across multiple connections

docs.title: Tolerance Stack-Up
docs.text:
When you stack 5 spacers, each with a 0.1mm tolerance, you could be off by up to 0.5mm total. In CAD, every spacer is exactly the nominal dimension. In reality, they vary slightly. This is called tolerance stack-up, and it is why experienced builders always check fit during assembly rather than trusting the drawing alone.

📐 Diagram Needed

Tolerance stack-up: 5 spacers × 0.1mm tolerance = 0.5mm total error

docs.title: Iteration Is Not Failure
docs.text:
If your physical build does not match your CAD model, that is not a failure — it is a learning opportunity. The iteration cycle is:

  1. Design in CAD
  2. Build physically
  3. Compare and identify gaps
  4. Update the CAD model to match reality (or redesign to fix the issue)
  5. Repeat

This cycle is how every competition robot is developed. Teams that iterate faster build better robots.

📐 Diagram Needed

Design cycle: CAD → Build → Compare → Update → Repeat (circular)

docs.title: Discussion Questions
docs.text:
After judging, the coach will lead a group discussion:

  1. What was the most common discrepancy across all teams?
  2. Did anyone find something in the physical build that worked better than the CAD design? Why?
  3. How would you change your CAD modeling approach next time to reduce the gap?
  4. What VEX parts were hardest to model accurately? Why?
  5. How does this experience change how you think about designing your competition robot?

docs.title: Awards
docs.text: Award Given To Perfect Match Closest match between CAD and physical build Best Documentation Most thorough and honest gap analysis Most Improved Student whose second attempt (if time allows) improved the most

docs.title: Return to Unit 3
docs.text: Unit 3: Onshape CAD — review all modules and challenges.

docs.title: Module 3.1: Interface & Sketching
docs.text: Before you can design a robot, you need to speak the language of CAD. This module gets you set up in Onshape and teaches you the two most fundamental skills: navigating the 3D workspace and creating precise 2D sketches that form the foundation of every 3D model.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Sign into Onshape and navigate to the V5 CT Education class
  • Orbit, pan, and zoom in the 3D workspace
  • Create a new sketch on any standard plane
  • Draw lines, rectangles, circles, and arcs
  • Apply geometric constraints to control sketch geometry
  • Add dimensions to fully define a sketch

docs.title: Step 1: Account Setup
docs.text:
You will use your CT (Caution Tape) account to sign into Onshape. Do not create a personal account — the team account gives you access to all shared documents and the class lesson system.

  1. Go to onshape.com and click Sign In
  2. Sign in with your CT account credentials (provided by your coach)
  3. Once signed in, you should see your Onshape dashboard with Documents, Classes, and Teams in the left sidebar

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Onshape dashboard showing Documents, Classes, and Teams in left sidebar

Use Your CT Account

Do not sign up with a personal email. The CT account is linked to the team's education plan, which gives you access to the V5 CT Education lessons, shared parts libraries, and team documents. If you use a personal account, you will not have access to any of these resources.

docs.title: Step 2: Navigate to the Lesson System
docs.text:
All Foundation Program CAD lessons live inside the Onshape Classes system:

  1. Click Classes in the left sidebar of your Onshape dashboard
  2. Find and open V5 CT Education
  3. You will see a list of lessons organized by number — Lesson 1 through Lesson 13+
  4. Open Lesson 1: Interface and Navigation

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Classes view with V5 CT Education class and lesson list visible

docs.title: Lesson 1: Interface and Navigation
docs.text:
Watch the tutorial video in Lesson 1. It covers:

  • Feature toolbar — the row of tools across the top (Sketch, Extrude, Fillet, etc.)

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Feature toolbar: Sketch, Extrude, Fillet, Chamfer, etc. labeled

  • Feature list — the timeline on the left showing every operation you've performed

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Feature list panel showing operations timeline on the left

  • Graphics area — the 3D viewport where your model lives
  • View controls — orbit (right-click drag), pan (middle-click drag), zoom (scroll wheel)
  • Standard planes — Top, Front, Right — the three default planes every document starts with

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Top, Front, and Right standard planes visible in 3D workspace

docs.title: Navigation Shortcuts
docs.text:
Action Mouse Keyboard Orbit Right-click + drag — Pan Middle-click + drag — Zoom Scroll wheel — Zoom to fit — F Front view — 1 on numpad Top view — 2 on numpad Right view — 3 on numpad Isometric view — 0 on numpad

Get Comfortable Orbiting

Spend a few minutes just orbiting, panning, and zooming around the default cube or any sample part. Navigation should feel natural and automatic — you will be doing it thousands of times. If you are fighting the view controls, everything else will feel harder than it needs to be.

docs.title: Lesson 2: Sketching and Constraints
docs.text: Open Lesson 2: Sketching and Constraints in Classes > V5 CT Education.

docs.title: What Is a Sketch?
docs.text: A sketch is a 2D drawing on a flat plane. Every 3D feature in Onshape starts as a sketch. Think of it as the blueprint — you draw the cross-section shape, then later extrude it into 3D.

docs.title: Creating a Sketch
docs.text:
1. Click the Sketch tool in the feature toolbar
  2. Select a plane (Top, Front, or Right) or any flat face on an existing part
  3. You are now in sketch mode — the view flattens to look straight at your chosen plane

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Sketch mode active: view flattened to plane, sketch toolbar visible

docs.title: Drawing Tools
docs.text:
Tool What It Does When to Use Line Draws straight line segments Edges, outlines, angular shapes Rectangle Draws a rectangle by two corners Plates, brackets, rectangular cutouts Center Rectangle Rectangle defined by center point Symmetrical rectangular features Circle Circle defined by center and radius Holes, shafts, rounded features Arc Curved line segment Fillets, rounded corners, curved profiles Slot Elongated hole shape Adjustment slots, sliding features

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Sketch tools: Line, Rectangle, Center Rectangle, Circle, Arc, Slot

docs.title: Constraints — The Key to Good Sketches
docs.text:
Constraints are rules that control how sketch geometry behaves. When you add a constraint, Onshape enforces that rule even as you move things around. A fully constrained sketch is a predictable sketch.

Constraint What It Does Icon Coincident Forces two points to share the same location Point on point Parallel Forces two lines to stay parallel Two parallel lines Perpendicular Forces two lines to meet at 90 degrees L-shape Equal Forces two lines or circles to have the same size = sign Tangent Forces a line and arc to meet smoothly Curved touch Horizontal Forces a line to be horizontal H Vertical Forces a line to be vertical V Midpoint Forces a point to the midpoint of a line Point on center Concentric Forces two circles to share the same center Nested circles

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Constraints toolbar: Coincident, Parallel, Perpendicular, Equal, Tangent

docs.title: Dimensions
docs.text:
After applying geometric constraints, add dimensions to lock in exact sizes:

  • Click the Dimension tool (or press D)
  • Click a line to set its length
  • Click two lines to set the distance between them
  • Click an arc or circle to set its radius/diameter

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Dimension tool applying a measurement to a line with dialog box visible

Blue vs Black Sketch Lines

  • Blue lines = under-constrained (still free to move)
  • Black lines = fully constrained (locked in place)
  • Red lines = over-constrained (conflicting constraints — fix this immediately)

Your goal is always all black lines before you exit the sketch.

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Blue (under-constrained), black (fully constrained), red (over-constrained) sketch lines

docs.title: Activity: Follow Along
docs.text:
Complete both Lesson 1 and Lesson 2 tutorials in the V5 CT Education class. For each lesson:

  1. Watch the full video first without pausing
  2. Watch it again, this time following along step by step in your own Onshape document
  3. When finished, your sketch should be fully constrained (all black lines)

docs.title: Practice Challenge
docs.text:
After completing the lessons, create a new document and sketch the following on the Top plane:

  • A rectangle that is exactly 4 inches wide and 2 inches tall
  • A circle centered on the rectangle with a 0.5 inch diameter
  • All geometry fully constrained (no blue lines)

If you can do this without looking at the tutorial, you are ready for Module 3.2.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Onshape is browser-based — no software to install, your work saves automatically
  2. Navigation must be muscle memory — practice orbiting, panning, and zooming until it is effortless
  3. Sketches are the foundation — every 3D feature starts as a 2D sketch
  4. Constraints control geometry — use them to make your sketch predictable and robust
  5. Fully constrain every sketch — all black lines, no blue, never red

docs.title: Next Up
docs.text: Module 3.2: 3D Modeling & Printing — turn your 2D sketches into 3D parts, and design something you can 3D print and take home.

docs.title: Module 3.5: Render & Present
docs.text: A great design that nobody can see is a wasted design. Render Studio transforms your gray CAD model into a photorealistic image with materials, lighting, and backgrounds. This module teaches you to render your drivetrain and present it like a professional engineer.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Open and configure Render Studio in Onshape
  • Apply materials (metal, plastic, rubber) to parts
  • Set up lighting and camera angles
  • Export high-resolution rendered images
  • Take effective screenshots for documentation
  • Present your design with clarity and confidence

docs.title: Lesson 9: Render Studio
docs.text: Open Lesson 9 in Classes > V5 CT Education.

docs.title: Accessing Render Studio
docs.text:
1. Open your completed drivetrain assembly
  2. Click the Render Studio tab at the bottom of the screen (next to your Assembly tabs)
  3. If you do not see Render Studio, right-click the tab bar and select Add Render Studio

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Render Studio tab highlighted in bottom tab bar

docs.title: The Render Studio Interface
docs.text:
Panel What It Controls Scene Background environment, floor, shadows Camera Position, angle, field of view, depth of field Appearances Material and color assignments for each part Lighting Environment light intensity, additional light sources Render Output resolution, quality, and export

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Render Studio: Scene, Camera, Appearances, Lighting, Render panels

docs.title: Step 1: Apply Materials
docs.text:
1. Click the Appearances panel
  2. Select a part or group of parts in the viewport
  3. Choose a material from the library:
Part Type Recommended Material C-channels, plates Brushed aluminum or anodized aluminum Gears Steel or dark gray plastic Wheels Black rubber Screws, hardware Chrome or stainless steel V5 Brain Default plastic (or custom green) Motors Dark plastic with silver accents

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Appearances panel with part selected, material library (aluminum, rubber, chrome)

Color-Code Your Subsystems

Use different aluminum colors for different subsystems — silver for the drivetrain frame, blue for the intake, red for the lift. This makes your render easier to read and helps during design reviews when pointing out specific subsystems.

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Drivetrain with materials: aluminum channels, rubber wheels, chrome screws

docs.title: Step 2: Configure the Scene
docs.text:
1. Choose a background environment — studio (clean white), outdoor, or custom
  2. Enable floor shadow for grounding — this makes the robot look like it is sitting on a real surface
  3. Adjust environment rotation to get the best lighting angle on your robot

docs.title: Step 3: Set the Camera
docs.text:
1. Orbit to the angle you want — isometric (3/4 view) is the standard for design presentations
  2. Adjust field of view:
    • Lower FOV (30-40) = more telephoto, less distortion, professional look
    • Higher FOV (60+) = wider view, more context, but can look distorted
  3. Optionally enable depth of field for a blurred background effect (subtle looks best)

docs.title: Step 4: Render and Export
docs.text:
1. Click Render in the render panel
  2. Select resolution:
    • 1920x1080 for presentations and documentation
    • 3840x2160 (4K) for posters or print
  3. Set quality to High (Low is for quick previews only)
  4. Wait for the render to complete (may take 30 seconds to a few minutes)
  5. Click Download to save the image

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Final polished 1920x1080 render of completed drivetrain — hero image

docs.title: Screenshots for Documentation
docs.text: Not every image needs to be a full render. For engineering notebooks and quick documentation, screenshots are faster and often more useful.

docs.title: Effective Screenshot Techniques
docs.text: Purpose Camera Angle Background What to Show Overall design Isometric (3/4 view) Clean white Full robot, all subsystems visible Mechanism detail Close-up, straight-on Transparent or white Single mechanism, gears visible Assembly instructions Step-by-step, same angle White Progressive build stages Dimension callouts Orthographic (front/side/top) White Key measurements annotated

docs.title: Screenshot Workflow
docs.text:
1. Hide parts you do not want visible (right-click > Hide)
  2. Set the view angle
  3. Use View > Screenshot or your operating system's screenshot tool
  4. Annotate in a separate tool if adding dimensions or callouts

Render vs Screenshot — When to Use Which

  • Renders are for presentations, competition boards, and anything the public sees. They look polished and professional.
  • Screenshots are for engineering notebooks, team documentation, and build guides. They are fast and informational.

Do not waste time rendering images that will only be seen by your team during a build meeting. Save renders for when polish matters.

docs.title: Presenting Your Design
docs.text: Designing a robot is only half the job. At competitions, you will present your design to judges, alliance partners, and teammates. Here is how to do it well.

docs.title: Structure Your Presentation
docs.text:
1. The Problem — What does the game require? What challenges did you identify?
  2. The Design — What did you build and why? Show your render/screenshots.
  3. Key Decisions — Why did you choose this gear ratio? This material? This linkage type?
  4. Testing Results — What worked? What did you iterate on?
  5. Next Steps — What would you improve with more time?

docs.title: Presentation Tips
docs.text:
Do Do Not Speak to the image on screen — point out specific features Read a script word for word Explain why, not just what List features without context Show before/after when you iterated Only show the final version Admit what did not work and how you fixed it Pretend everything was perfect Keep it under 3 minutes Ramble without structure

The 30-Second Elevator Pitch

Practice describing your entire robot design in 30 seconds. If you can explain the key decisions and trade-offs in half a minute, you truly understand your design. If you cannot, you need to think more deeply about why you made the choices you did.

docs.title: Activity: Render Your Drivetrain
docs.text:
1. Open your completed drivetrain assembly from Lessons 6-8
  2. Apply appropriate materials to all visible parts
  3. Set up a studio environment with floor shadows
  4. Position the camera at an isometric angle
  5. Render at 1920x1080 resolution, High quality
  6. Export and save the image
  7. Take 2-3 additional screenshots from different angles for documentation
  8. Prepare a 2-minute presentation covering:
    • Your drivetrain design and gear ratio
    • One decision you made and why
    • One thing you would change

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Renders communicate professionalism — a well-rendered design earns credibility with judges and partners
  2. Materials make models readable — color-coding subsystems helps everyone understand your robot at a glance
  3. Screenshots are for speed, renders are for polish — use the right tool for the audience
  4. Presenting is a skill — practice explaining your design decisions, not just listing features

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text:
You have completed all 5 modules in Unit 3. Time to prove your skills:

  • 3D Print Keychain — Design and print your own custom keychain
  • CAD Battle — Race to model a mystery object
  • CAD vs Real — Build the same mechanism in CAD and physically

docs.title: Module 3.4: VEX Parts & Mates
docs.text: You know how to model parts from scratch. Now it is time to work with real VEX components. This module teaches you to import parts from the VEX V5 Parts Library, assemble them using mates, and build functional mechanisms in CAD — starting with a 4-bar linkage and ending with a full drivetrain.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Find and label the CTRC V5 Parts Library in Onshape
  • Import VEX parts into your own assemblies
  • Use Fastened, Revolute, and Tangent mates
  • Build a 4-bar linkage assembly with proper mates
  • Apply gear relations for non-1:1 gear ratios
  • Use the Replicate feature to efficiently place repeated components like screws

docs.title: VEX Part Library Setup
docs.text: Before you can assemble anything, you need access to the parts. The CTRC V5 Parts Library contains every standard VEX V5 component — C-channels, gears, screws, motors, wheels, and more.

docs.title: Step-by-Step: Finding and Labeling the Library
docs.text:
1. Open Onshape and sign in with your CT account
  2. Click the search bar at the top of the dashboard
  3. Search for CTRC V5 Parts Library (or search for the public VEX V5 library if the team library is unavailable)

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Onshape search bar with 'CTRC V5 Parts Library' results showing

  1. Open the document to verify it contains the parts you need — you should see folders organized by part type (Structure, Motion, Hardware, Electronics)

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Parts library document folders: Structure, Motion, Hardware, Electronics

  1. Label it for quick access:
    • Click the three-dot menu (⋮) next to the document name
    • Select Add Label
    • Create or select a label like Parts Library or VEX V5
  2. Verify: return to your dashboard, click Labels in the left sidebar, and confirm the library appears under your new label

Label It Once, Find It Forever

You will be importing from this library dozens of times throughout the program. Labeling it now saves you from searching for it every single time. Treat it like bookmarking your most-visited website.

docs.title: Importing Parts Into Your Assembly
docs.text:
1. Open your assembly document
  2. Click the Insert button in the toolbar
  3. In the Insert dialog, search for the part name (e.g., "2x25 C-Channel" or "36T Gear")
  4. Select the part from the CTRC V5 Parts Library
  5. Click to place it in your assembly
  6. The part appears at the origin — you will position it using mates

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Insert dialog searching for a part (e.g., C-Channel) from parts library

Part Studios vs Assemblies

  • Part Studio = where you design individual parts from scratch
  • Assembly = where you bring multiple parts together and define how they connect and move

You have been working in Part Studios so far. Starting now, you will work primarily in Assemblies.

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Bottom tab bar: Part Studio tabs vs Assembly tabs highlighted

docs.title: Lesson 6: Mates
docs.text:
Open Lesson 6 in Classes > V5 CT Education.

Mates define how parts connect to each other — whether they are bolted together, can rotate around a pivot, or touch along a surface.

docs.title: Mate Types
docs.text:
Mate Type Degrees of Freedom Real-World Example Fastened 0 (locked in place) Bolted bracket, screwed plate Revolute 1 (rotation around axis) Wheel on axle, arm on pivot Slider 1 (linear translation) Drawer on rails, piston in cylinder Planar 3 (slide and rotate on plane) Book on table Cylindrical 2 (rotate + slide along axis) Nut on bolt Ball 3 (rotate in any direction) Ball joint, shoulder joint Tangent Contact along surfaces Gear teeth touching, ball on ground

For this module, focus on Fastened, Revolute, and Tangent — these cover 90% of VEX assemblies.

📐 Diagram Needed

3 mate types: Fastened (locked), Revolute (rotates), Tangent (touching) with motion arrows

docs.title: Building a VEX 4-Bar Linkage
docs.text:
Follow the Lesson 6 tutorial to build a 4-bar linkage. This is a classic mechanism used in VEX for lifts and arms.

Assembly Order:

  1. Insert the base C-channel — Fastened mate to the origin (this anchors your assembly)

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

First C-channel fastened to origin — mate dialog with origin selected

  1. Insert the two side links — Revolute mate at the pivot points

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Revolute mate at pivot point showing mate connectors and rotation axis

  1. Insert the top bar — Revolute mate at both connection points
  2. Test motion — right-click the assembly and select Animate to watch the 4-bar move

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Completed 4-bar linkage assembly with all pivot points visible

🎬 GIF/Video Needed

4-bar linkage animated in Onshape, showing full range of motion

Always Fasten Your First Part

The first part in any assembly should be Fastened to the origin. If you skip this, your entire assembly will float freely in space, and every subsequent mate will be harder to define. Think of it as anchoring your building to the ground before constructing the walls.

docs.title: Mate Connector Tips
docs.text:
• Mate connectors are the points on a part where mates attach — usually hole centers, face centers, or edges
  • You can create custom mate connectors by right-clicking a point and selecting "Create Mate Connector"
  • Implied mate connectors appear automatically at hole centers and face centers — look for the green circles

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Implied mate connectors (green circles) at hole centers and face centers

  • Always verify mate alignment — if parts appear rotated 90 degrees, you may need to flip the mate connector

docs.title: Lesson 7: Gear Relations
docs.text: Open Lesson 7 in Classes > V5 CT Education.

docs.title: What Are Gear Relations?
docs.text: A gear relation tells Onshape that two rotating parts are connected by gears — when one turns, the other turns at the correct ratio. This is essential for modeling drivetrains, intakes, and any mechanism with geared power transfer.

docs.title: Setting Up a Gear Relation
docs.text:
1. Both gears must already have Revolute mates on their respective axles
  2. Select Gear Relation from the mate relations menu
  3. Choose the two revolute mates you want to connect
  4. Enter the gear ratio:
    • For two meshing gears: ratio = driven teeth / driving teeth
    • Example: 60T driven by 36T = 60:36 = 5:3 ratio

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Gear Relation dialog with two revolute mates and ratio (60:36)

  1. Set the direction (same or reverse — depends on whether gears mesh directly or through a chain/belt)

docs.title: Activity: Half a Drivetrain
docs.text:
Design one side of a drivetrain with a non-1:1 gear ratio:

  1. Insert two C-channels for the drivetrain rails
  2. Add a motor with a 36T gear on the output
  3. Add a driven axle with a 60T gear
  4. Add wheels to the driven axle
  5. Apply a gear relation with the correct ratio
  6. Animate to verify the wheels spin when the motor turns

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

36T and 60T gears meshing in assembly with gear relation applied

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Half drivetrain: motor with 36T, driven axle with 60T, wheels attached

Check Your Ratio

Animate the assembly and count rotations. If the motor gear (36T) spins 5 times, the wheel gear (60T) should spin 3 times. If it does not match, your gear relation ratio is wrong.

docs.title: Lesson 8: Replicate
docs.text: Open Lesson 8 in Classes > V5 CT Education.

docs.title: What Is Replicate?
docs.text: Replicate copies a group of parts and their mates to a new location defined by a pattern of mate connectors. It is the fastest way to add repeated hardware — screws, nuts, spacers — across an assembly.

docs.title: Why Replicate Matters
docs.text:
A single drivetrain rail might have 20+ screws. Inserting and mating each one individually would take hours. Replicate lets you:

  1. Insert and mate one screw correctly
  2. Select a pattern of holes where you want copies
  3. Replicate — all screws appear, properly mated, in seconds

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Before (one screw) vs after (all screws replicated across holes)

docs.title: Activity: Complete Build-Ready Drivetrain
docs.text:
Using Replicate, finish your drivetrain assembly:

  1. Start with the half-drivetrain from Lesson 7
  2. Mirror or duplicate for the other side
  3. Insert one screw with a Fastened mate into a mounting hole
  4. Use Replicate to copy that screw to all remaining mounting holes
  5. Add spacers, nuts, and standoffs as needed
  6. Insert the V5 Brain, Battery, and Radio as reference parts

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Complete drivetrain assembly: both sides, hardware, brain, battery, radio

docs.title: Replicate Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Source screw is properly mated (Fastened, correct orientation)
  • [ ] Target mate connectors are all selected (every hole that needs a screw)
  • [ ] Replicated screws all appear in correct orientation
  • [ ] No floating or misaligned hardware
  • [ ] Assembly animates smoothly (wheels spin, no interference)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. The Parts Library is your toolbox — label it, know what is in it, import efficiently
  2. Fasten your first part to the origin — every assembly needs an anchor
  3. Revolute mates = rotation — use them for wheels, pivots, arms, and anything that spins
  4. Gear relations make assemblies realistic — they enforce correct rotational relationships
  5. Replicate saves hours — never manually mate 20 screws when Replicate can do it in seconds

docs.title: Next Up
docs.text: Module 3.5: Render & Present — make your drivetrain look professional with renders and learn to present your design to the team.

docs.title: Unit 4: Mechanisms
docs.text: The drivetrain moves your robot. Mechanisms make your robot do things. Unit 4 teaches you to design, CAD, and build real game mechanisms — wedges for pushing, intakes for collecting, and linear sliders for reaching. Every mechanism follows the same process: CAD it first, get coach approval, then build it.

docs.title: Unit Overview
docs.text: Details Modules 3 Field Challenges 3 Estimated Time 10–14 hours Final Deliverable A drivetrain with at least two functional mechanisms attached

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
By the end of Unit 4, you will be able to:

  • [x] Design and build a 2-bar wedge for pushing strategies
  • [x] Design and build a top roller intake with correct compression
  • [x] Design and build a linear slider mechanism with deployment
  • [x] Understand and apply compression and deadzone concepts
  • [x] Free-spin test mechanisms before adding motors
  • [x] Compete in mechanism-specific field challenges

docs.title: Module Roadmap
docs.text: Module Title Key Skills 4.1 Wedge & Pushing 2-bar wedge, screw joints, leverage, pushing strategy 4.2 Intakes Top roller intake, compression, deadzone, free-spin testing 4.3 Linear Slider Slider joints, rails, one-time release deployment

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text:
After building your mechanisms, compete head-to-head:

Challenge What It Tests Mechanism Used Sumo Bots Pushing power, strategy, driving Wedge (Module 4.1) Sack Attack Intake speed, tuning, driver skill Intake (Module 4.2) Ball Sweep Mechanism speed, strategy, pressure Intake + Linear Slider

docs.title: The Build Process
docs.text:
Every mechanism in Unit 4 follows the same workflow:

CAD It → Coach Approval → Build It → Test It → Compete

docs.title: Why CAD First?
docs.text:
Measure Twice, Cut Never

By CADing your mechanism before building, you catch design mistakes when they are free to fix — in the computer. Once you start cutting metal and assembling, mistakes cost time and parts. The 20 minutes you spend in Onshape saves 2 hours of rebuilding.

docs.title: Coach Approval Gate
docs.text:
Before you build anything physical, your coach must review and approve your CAD model. They will check for:

  • Structural integrity (will it break under load?)
  • Correct part selection (are you using the right screws, gears, spacers?)
  • Interference (do parts collide when the mechanism moves?)
  • Feasibility (can this actually be built with the parts we have?)

docs.title: Materials Needed
docs.text:
Each student or pair will need access to:

  • Completed drivetrain from Unit 1
  • Additional C-channels and flat bars
  • Flex wheels or rubber rollers (for intake)
  • Linear slide rails (for slider)
  • Additional motors (11W or 5.5W)
  • Screw joints hardware (shoulder screws, spacers, nylock nuts)
  • Various gears (for intake gearing)
  • Zipties and rubber bands

docs.title: Tips for Success
docs.text:
Free-Spin First, Motor Second

Before attaching any motor to any mechanism, spin it by hand. If it does not spin freely and smoothly, adding a motor will not fix the problem — it will burn out the motor. Fix the friction first, then add power.

Do Not Skip CAD

Students who skip the CAD step and jump straight to building almost always rebuild their mechanism at least once. Students who CAD first almost never do. The data is clear — CAD saves time.

docs.title: Let's Get Started
docs.text: Begin with Module 4.1: Wedge & Pushing — the simplest mechanism and the most satisfying to use in competition.

docs.title: Challenge: Ball Sweep
docs.text: Offense and defense at the same time. Ball Sweep is a 1v1 match where the field is split in half and each side starts with equal balls. Your job: use your intake and linear slider to sweep balls to your opponent's side. When the buzzer sounds, the side with fewer balls wins.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: End the 60-second match with fewer balls on your half of the field than your opponent has on theirs.

docs.title: Format
docs.text: Details Type 1v1 head-to-head Duration 60 seconds per match Matches Best of 3 rounds Tournament Single elimination or round robin (coach's discretion) Mechanisms Used Intake (Module 4.2) and/or Linear Slider (Module 4.3)

docs.title: Field Setup
docs.text:
• Field: Standard VEX field or 8' x 8' taped area
  • Dividing line: A clear center line splits the field in half (tape or barrier)
  • Balls: Equal number on each side (8-12 per side, depending on availability)
  • Starting position: Each robot starts in the far corner of their half, facing the center line

docs.title: Field Layout
docs.text:
📐 Diagram Needed

Ball Sweep field: center line, equal balls each side, robot starting positions

📷 Photo Needed

Field with center line dividing two halves, balls on each side

Robots Can Cross the Line

There is no rule preventing you from crossing the center line. The line only defines which balls count on which side at the final buzzer. You can drive anywhere on the field.

docs.title: Before the Match
docs.text:
1. Equal balls placed on each side (counted and verified by the coach)
  2. Robots inspected — all mechanisms functional, battery charged
  3. Robots placed in starting positions
  4. Drivers ready with controllers

docs.title: During the Match
docs.text:
1. Coach signals "3, 2, 1, Go!" — 60 second timer starts
  2. Robots may drive anywhere on the field
  3. Use any mechanism (intake, linear slider, wedge, pushing) to move balls to the opponent's side
  4. No holding balls — you may intake and eject, but you cannot trap balls inside your robot permanently
  5. No stacking balls on your robot to remove them from play
  6. Contact between robots is allowed (pushing, blocking) but not intentional damage
  7. If a ball leaves the field entirely, it is removed from play (does not count for either side)

docs.title: At the Buzzer
docs.text:
1. Timer reaches 0:00 — all robot movement must stop immediately
  2. Count the balls on each half of the field
  3. Balls on the center line: if more than 50% is on one side, it counts for that side. If exactly on the line, it does not count for either side
  4. Fewer balls on your side = you win the round

docs.title: Tie-Breaking
docs.text:
• If both sides have equal balls, the round is a draw
  • If the match is tied 1-1 after best of 3, a sudden-death 30-second round is played

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text: Result Round Outcome Fewer balls on your half You win the round More balls on your half You lose the round Equal balls on both halves Draw

docs.title: Example Match
docs.text:
Start:    Side A: 10 balls  |  Side B: 10 balls
End:      Side A: 7 balls   |  Side B: 12 balls  (1 left the field)

Winner: Robot A (7 < 12)

docs.title: Offensive Strategies
docs.text: Strategy Description Risk Level Rapid sweep Drive across your half fast, sweeping balls forward with intake/slider Low risk, consistent Ball barrage Intake multiple balls, drive to opponent's side, eject all at once Medium risk, high reward Line push Push balls across the center line in groups using your wedge/front Low risk, low per-ball effort Corner dump Sweep opponent's balls into their corners where they are hard to recover Medium risk, wastes opponent's time

docs.title: Defensive Strategies
docs.text:
Strategy Description When to Use Block the line Park on the center line to prevent opponent from sweeping balls across When you are ahead with 20 seconds left Return fire Immediately sweep back any balls the opponent sends to your side When opponent is using the barrage strategy Body block Use your robot to physically prevent the opponent from reaching balls on your side When you have a pushing advantage

📷 Photo Needed

Two robots sweeping balls across center line

| Disruption | Push the opponent away from ball clusters, wasting their time | When your opponent's intake is faster than yours |

docs.title: Time Management
docs.text:
Phase Time Focus Opening 0:00 - 0:20 Sweep the easy balls closest to the center line Mid-game 0:20 - 0:45 Aggressive offense — clear remaining balls from your side Endgame 0:45 - 1:00 Defensive positioning OR one final push

The Last 10 Seconds Matter Most

Balls swept in the last 10 seconds cannot be swept back. If you are holding balls in your intake, eject them on the opponent's side in the final moments. If you are ahead, play defense and block the center line.

docs.title: Intake for Ball Sweep
docs.text:
• Intake balls from your side, drive to center, eject onto opponent's side
  • Reverse the intake motor to eject
  • Faster intake = faster cycle time = more balls moved

docs.title: Linear Slider for Ball Sweep
docs.text:
• Deploy the ball rake to sweep multiple balls in a single pass
  • The wider the rake, the more balls you sweep per pass
  • Combine with forward driving for maximum coverage

docs.title: Wedge for Ball Sweep
docs.text:
• Use the wedge to bulldoze balls across the line
  • Less precise than intake but moves multiple balls at once
  • Best for the opening rush when balls are clustered

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text: Mistake Why It Loses Fix Focusing on only one ball at a time Opponent sweeps 5 while you move 1 Use sweeping motions, move groups Never playing defense Opponent sends back everything you sweep Switch to defense in the last 15 seconds if ahead Crossing the line and getting stuck Waste time on opponent's side Sweep from your side, minimize time across the line Ignoring balls near the walls Those balls count against you Sweep wall balls early — they are harder to reach later Getting into a pushing match Both robots stuck in the middle while balls sit untouched Disengage, go collect balls instead of fighting

docs.title: Awards
docs.text: Award Criteria Ball Sweep Champion Tournament winner Best Strategist Voted by peers — smartest use of offense and defense Most Balls Moved Highest total balls moved across all rounds (tracked by coaches) Comeback King/Queen Won a match after being behind in round 1

docs.title: Post-Challenge Discussion
docs.text:
1. Which mechanism was most effective for moving balls: intake, slider, or wedge?
  2. When did defensive strategy make sense vs offensive? What was the turning point?
  3. How did time pressure change your driving compared to practice?
  4. What would you redesign for a second Ball Sweep tournament?
  5. How does this challenge simulate real VEX competition match dynamics?

docs.title: Return to Unit 4
docs.text: Unit 4: Mechanisms — review all modules and challenges.

docs.title: Challenge: Sack Attack
docs.text: Grab everything. Score everything. Sixty seconds. Sack Attack tests your intake mechanism under time pressure. Bean bags are scattered across the field, and your robot must intake as many as possible in one minute. Every tuning decision you made in Module 4.2 — compression, deadzone, roller speed — shows up here.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Intake and hold or score as many bean bags as possible in 60 seconds using your top roller intake mechanism from Module 4.2.

docs.title: Format
docs.text: Details Type Individual, timed run Duration 60 seconds per run Attempts 2 runs per student, best score counts Mechanism Required Intake from Module 4.2 Game Objects 10+ bean bags (or substitute soft game objects)

docs.title: Field Setup
docs.text:
• Field size: Standard VEX field or a taped-off 8' x 8' area
  • Bean bags: 10-15 bean bags scattered randomly across the field
  • Starting position: Robot starts in a corner, intake facing the field
  • Scoring zone: A designated area (box or tray) where intaked bean bags must be deposited

docs.title: Field Layout
docs.text:
📐 Diagram Needed

Sack Attack field: bean bag positions, start zone, scoring zone labeled

📷 Photo Needed

Real field with bean bags scattered and scoring zone visible

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
1. Robot starts in the designated corner with intake facing the field
  2. Bean bags are placed by the coach in random positions
  3. Driver holds the controller and waits for the start signal
  4. Timer is visible to the driver

docs.title: During the Run
docs.text:
1. Coach says "Go" — 60 second timer starts
  2. Driver controls the robot to intake bean bags
  3. Robot must intake the bean bag (pull it into the robot using the intake mechanism) — pushing or bulldozing does not count

📷 Photo Needed

Robot intaking a bean bag during a timed run

  1. Intaked bean bags must be deposited in the scoring zone to earn points
  2. Bean bags that fall out of the robot before reaching the scoring zone do not count
  3. If a bean bag is only partially intaked (hanging out of the robot), it does not count unless it reaches the scoring zone

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text: Action Points Bean bag successfully intaked AND deposited in scoring zone 1 point Bean bag intaked but dropped before scoring zone 0 points Bean bag pushed into scoring zone (not intaked) 0 points

docs.title: End of Run
docs.text:
1. Timer reaches 0:00 — driver must stop immediately
  2. Count bean bags in the scoring zone that were properly intaked
  3. Record the score
  4. Reset the field for the next run or next student

docs.title: Route Planning
docs.text:
Before your run, look at the field layout and plan your route:

Strategy Description Best When Nearest first Drive to the closest bean bag, intake, score, repeat Bean bags are spread evenly Sweep line Drive in a straight line across the field, intaking everything in your path Bean bags are clustered in a line Cluster attack Target the area with the most bean bags first Bean bags are grouped together Perimeter sweep Drive around the edges first, then hit the center Bean bags are near the walls

docs.title: Driving for Intake Success
docs.text:
1. Approach straight — your intake works best when the bean bag enters dead center
  2. Slow down on contact — driving too fast means the bean bag bounces off instead of being intaked
  3. Confirm the intake — glance at your robot to verify the bean bag is fully inside before moving to the next one
  4. Score frequently — do not try to hold 5 bean bags at once. Intake 1-2, score, repeat

docs.title: Intake Tuning Reminders
docs.text:
If your intake is not performing well during practice runs:

Symptom Likely Cause Quick Fix Bean bag slips under the rollers Compression too low Lower the intake or add roller wrap Bean bag gets stuck in the intake Compression too high or deadzone Raise the intake slightly or add a guide ramp Bean bag bounces off on approach Driving too fast or intake too slow Slow down approach, increase roller speed Intake stalls when grabbing Motor overloaded, too much compression Reduce compression, check free-spin Bean bag falls out while driving No retention mechanism Add a back plate or second roller

Two Minutes of Tuning Can Win the Challenge

Between your two attempts, you get time to adjust. If your first run revealed any issues, fix them immediately. Loosen or tighten compression, adjust roller height, change driving strategy. The students who improve between attempts are the ones who win.

docs.title: Scoring Example
docs.text:
Student Run 1 Run 2 Best Score Alex 4 6 6 Jordan 5 5 5 Sam 3 7 7 Taylor 6 4 6

Winner: Sam with 7 points (best single run).

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text: Mistake Impact Fix Not planning a route Wasted time driving randomly Scan the field for 10 seconds before starting Trying to intake from the side Intake only works from the front Always approach head-on Holding too many bean bags Robot gets heavy, intake jams Score after every 1-2 intakes Not practicing between runs Same mistakes repeated Use the gap between runs to tune and strategize Panicking at the clock Sloppy driving, missed intakes Practice under time pressure before the challenge

docs.title: Awards
docs.text: Award Criteria Sack Attack Champion Highest single-run score Most Consistent Smallest difference between Run 1 and Run 2 Best Improvement Biggest improvement from Run 1 to Run 2 Smoothest Intake Voted by coaches — cleanest intake mechanism performance

docs.title: Post-Challenge Discussion
docs.text:
1. What compression settings worked best? What was the sweet spot?
  2. Did anyone find and fix a deadzone between runs?
  3. How did driving speed affect intake success?
  4. What would you redesign about your intake for a real competition?
  5. How does scoring under time pressure differ from casual testing?

docs.title: Next Challenge
docs.text: Ball Sweep — 1v1, head-to-head, fewer balls on your side wins.

docs.title: Challenge: Sumo Bots
docs.text: Last robot standing wins. Sumo Bots is the ultimate test of your wedge design, drivetrain power, and driving skill. Two robots enter the circular sumo field. One gets pushed off. Simple rules, deep strategy.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Push your opponent's robot off the circular sumo field. The last robot remaining on the platform wins the round.

docs.title: Format
docs.text: Details Type 1v1 head-to-head Matches Best of 3 rounds per match Tournament Double elimination bracket Round Duration 60 seconds (if no one is pushed off, judges decide) Mechanism Required Wedge attachment from Module 4.1

docs.title: The Sumo Field
docs.text:
• Shape: Circular platform
  • Surface: Standard VEX field tiles or smooth flat surface
  • Boundary: Clearly marked edge — any robot that touches the ground beyond the edge is eliminated for that round
  • Starting positions: Robots start on opposite sides of the circle, facing each other

docs.title: Field Diagram
docs.text:
📐 Diagram Needed

Circular sumo field: Start A, Start B positions, center, boundary edge labeled

📷 Photo Needed

Actual circular sumo platform with boundary markings and starting positions

docs.title: Before the Match
docs.text:
1. Robots must pass a safety inspection before competing:
    • No sharp edges or points that could damage other robots or the field
    • All screws tightened, no loose parts
    • Wedge securely attached
    • Battery secured and wiring managed
  2. Robots must use their wedge attachment from Module 4.1
  3. Robot must fit within standard VEX size constraints (18" x 18" x 18")

docs.title: During the Match
docs.text:
1. Coach says "3, 2, 1, Go!" — drivers begin
  2. Driving only — no autonomous period for this challenge
  3. Contact is expected — pushing, wedging, and shoving are the entire point
  4. If a robot is fully off the platform (all wheels past the edge), that robot loses the round
  5. If a robot is partially off (some wheels over the edge) but recovers, play continues
  6. If neither robot is eliminated after 60 seconds, judges award the round based on aggression and field control

📷 Photo Needed

Two robots pushing on sumo field, wedges engaged

No Weapon Attachments

Sumo is about pushing, not damaging. Wedges only — no spinning mechanisms, no launchers, no parts designed to break the opponent's robot. Any robot deemed to have a "weapon" is disqualified. The wedge must be a passive, non-powered attachment.

docs.title: Penalties
docs.text: Violation Penalty Intentionally damaging opponent's robot Disqualification Starting before "Go" Round restart (first offense), round loss (second offense) Robot falls apart during match Round loss (rebuild and continue bracket if possible) Unsportsmanlike conduct Disqualification from tournament

docs.title: The Fundamentals
docs.text:
Principle Application Get under them Lower wedge angle wins — you lift their wheels, they lose traction Control the center The robot in the center has more room to maneuver Face them head-on Your wedge only works from the front — never show your side or back Push toward the edge Once you are under them, drive straight toward the nearest edge

📷 Photo Needed

One robot's wedge getting under another, lifting opponent's wheels

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
• Drive forward at medium speed toward your opponent
  • Aim your wedge at their front wheels or the gap between their wedge and the ground
  • Do not charge at full speed — you will overshoot and lose position

docs.title: The Lock
docs.text:
• Once your wedge is under them, accelerate to full power
  • Keep driving straight — any turning reduces your pushing force
  • Maintain pressure until they are off the edge

docs.title: The Recovery
docs.text:
• If they get under YOU, reverse immediately and create distance
  • Reset and approach from a different angle
  • Try to circle to their side where their wedge is not protecting them

docs.title: The Edge Game
docs.text:
• When near the edge, keep your center of gravity low and centered
  • Avoid spinning near the edge — momentum can carry you off
  • If you are being pushed toward the edge, turn 90 degrees to redirect the push along the edge instead of over it

docs.title: Advanced Strategy
docs.text:
Watch Their Wheels

When your wedge is under the opponent and their wheels are spinning in the air, they have zero traction. That is your moment to push. If their wheels are still on the ground, your wedge is not low enough — readjust.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text: Mistake Why It Loses Charging at full speed from the start You overshoot and end up behind them Turning while pushing Reduces pushing force, lets them escape Getting pushed sideways Your wedge is only on the front — reposition Ignoring field position Fighting near your own edge means one mistake ends the round Giving up after losing the wedge battle Circle and re-engage — one good push can still win

docs.title: Double Elimination Format
docs.text:
• Winners bracket: Win and advance, lose and drop to losers bracket
  • Losers bracket: Win and keep going, lose and you are eliminated
  • Finals: Winners bracket champion vs losers bracket champion
  • If the losers bracket champion wins the first finals match, a second match is played (true double elimination)

docs.title: Seeding
docs.text: Seeds are determined by: 1. Module 4.1 build quality score (coach assessment) 2. Free-spin test results from Unit 1 3. Random draw for ties

docs.title: Awards
docs.text: Award Criteria Sumo Champion Tournament winner Iron Wall Robot that was pushed off the fewest times across all rounds Best Driver Voted by peers — best driving skill and strategy Best Wedge Design Voted by coaches — most effective wedge

docs.title: Post-Challenge Discussion
docs.text:
After the tournament, the coach will lead a debrief:

  1. What wedge designs were most effective? Why?
  2. Did any driving strategy consistently beat others?
  3. How did center of gravity affect pushing ability?
  4. What would you change about your wedge for next time?
  5. How does this challenge connect to real VEX competition strategy?

docs.title: Next Challenge
docs.text: Sack Attack — test your intake mechanism under pressure.

docs.title: Module 4.2: Intakes
docs.text: A wedge pushes things. An intake grabs things. The top roller intake is one of the most versatile and commonly used mechanisms in VEX robotics. It uses spinning rollers to pull game objects into your robot, hold them, and score them. This module teaches you to design, tune, and troubleshoot an intake with the right compression and zero deadzone.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Design a top roller intake in Onshape CAD
  • Understand compression and calculate it for your game object
  • Identify and eliminate deadzone in an intake
  • Free-spin test a mechanism before adding a motor
  • Build, power, and tune an intake on your drivetrain

docs.title: What Is a Top Roller Intake?
docs.text:
A top roller intake uses one or more spinning rollers mounted above the path of the game object. As the robot drives forward, the rollers spin inward and pull the object into the robot. Think of it like a conveyor belt — but instead of a belt, you use individual rollers.

📷 Photo Needed

Completed top roller intake on drivetrain: rollers, arms, gearing, motor visible

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text:
Component Purpose Roller Spinning cylinder (flex wheels, rubber rollers) that contacts the game object Roller axle Shaft the roller is mounted on Intake arms C-channels that hold the roller axles in position Motor Powers the roller rotation Gearing Transfers motor power to the roller at the correct speed Compression zone The gap between the roller and the floor/backplate that squeezes the game object

📐 Diagram Needed

Intake components labeled: roller, axle, arms, motor, gearing, compression zone

docs.title: CAD Activity: Top Roller Intake
docs.text: Open Lesson 13: Top Roller Intake in Classes > V5 CT Education.

docs.title: Design Steps
docs.text:
1. Open your drivetrain assembly with the wedge from Module 4.1
  2. Determine intake position — typically at the front of the robot, above or replacing the wedge
  3. Insert intake arm C-channels — two parallel channels, one on each side
  4. Add roller axles with revolute mates
  5. Mount flex wheels or rollers on the axles
  6. Add gearing — motor gear to roller gear with appropriate ratio
  7. Position the rollers so the compression zone is correct for your game object
  8. Animate to verify rollers spin in the correct direction (inward, toward the robot)

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Intake assembly in Onshape showing rollers, gearing, correct positioning

docs.title: Compression
docs.text: Compression is the most important parameter of any intake. It determines how firmly the rollers grip the game object as it passes through.

docs.title: The Formula
docs.text:
Compression = Game Object Size - Intake Gap

Where: - Game Object Size = the diameter or height of the object being intaked - Intake Gap = the distance between the roller surface and the floor/backplate

📐 Diagram Needed

Game object size vs intake gap with compression zone highlighted

docs.title: Example Calculation
docs.text:
For a standard VEX ball: 

Ball diameter:  3.28 inches
Intake gap:     2.85 inches
Compression:    3.28 - 2.85 = 0.43 inches

docs.title: Compression Guidelines
docs.text:
Compression Result Too low (< 0.2") Object slips through without being gripped, inconsistent intake Good (0.3" - 0.5") Object is firmly gripped and pulled through reliably Too high (> 0.6") Object gets stuck, motor stalls, rollers deform, mechanism jams

📷 Photo Needed

Ball pulled through with ideal compression — smooth intake action

More Compression Is Not Better

Students often assume that squeezing harder means better grip. Past a certain point, excess compression causes the motor to stall, the flex wheels to deform permanently, and the game object to get stuck. Find the sweet spot through testing.

docs.title: Deadzone
docs.text: Deadzone is the gap in your intake where the game object has no contact with any roller. During this gap, the object stops moving and the intake flow breaks.

docs.title: What Causes Deadzone
docs.text:
• Rollers spaced too far apart
  • Gap between the intake entry and the first roller
  • Gap between the last roller and the scoring position
  • Roller diameter too small for the spacing

📐 Diagram Needed

Gap between rollers where object loses contact — the deadzone concept

docs.title: How to Identify Deadzone
docs.text:
1. Manually push a game object through the intake (motor off)
  2. Watch for any point where the object stops being pulled
  3. If there is a gap where the object just sits — that is a deadzone
  4. Mark the location and measure the gap

docs.title: How to Fix Deadzone
docs.text: Fix When to Use Add another roller Large gap between existing rollers Use larger diameter rollers Rollers are too small to bridge the gap Reposition rollers closer Spacing is adjustable in the mounting Add a ramp or guide Object needs to be directed from one roller to the next Use a continuous belt/chain Multiple small rollers are impractical

docs.title: Free-Spin Test
docs.text: Before attaching any motor, every mechanism must pass the free-spin test.

docs.title: How to Free-Spin Test
docs.text:
1. Assemble the intake fully — all rollers, axles, gears, and bearings in place
  2. Do not attach the motor
  3. Spin each roller by hand
  4. Each roller should spin freely for at least 3 full rotations after a flick
  5. Spin the entire gear train — all connected rollers should spin smoothly together

docs.title: What Free-Spin Reveals
docs.text:
Observation Diagnosis Roller barely spins Axle binding — check bearing alignment, spacers, nut tightness Roller spins but gear train does not Gears not meshing properly — check center-to-center distance Grinding noise Metal-on-metal contact — add spacers, check for interference Uneven spinning Bent axle or misaligned bearings — straighten or replace

🎬 GIF/Video Needed

Hand flicking roller — spins freely for 3+ rotations

If It Does Not Free-Spin, It Will Not Work With a Motor

A motor does not fix friction — it fights friction and loses. A motor that is constantly fighting friction will overheat, draw excess current, and eventually burn out. Fix the friction first, then add power. This is the single most important rule of mechanism building.

docs.title: Build Instructions
docs.text: Once your coach approves your CAD model:

docs.title: Step 1: Build the Intake Frame
docs.text:
1. Cut or select intake arm C-channels
  2. Mount them to your drivetrain frame
  3. Verify they are parallel and properly spaced

docs.title: Step 2: Install Rollers
docs.text:
1. Press flex wheels or rubber rollers onto axles
  2. Insert axles into the intake arms with bearings
  3. Add spacers to eliminate lateral play
  4. Free-spin test each roller individually

docs.title: Step 3: Add Gearing
docs.text:
1. Install gears on each roller axle
  2. Mesh the gears together (check center-to-center distance)
  3. Free-spin test the entire gear train

docs.title: Step 4: Power the Intake
docs.text:
1. Mount the motor to the intake frame
  2. Connect the motor gear to the gear train
  3. Wire the motor to the V5 Brain
  4. Test at low speed first — verify rollers spin the correct direction (inward)

docs.title: Step 5: Tune
docs.text:
1. Place a game object in front of the intake
  2. Drive forward slowly — does the object get intaked?
  3. If not, check: compression, deadzone, roller direction, motor speed
  4. Iterate until the intake reliably pulls in game objects 9 out of 10 times

docs.title: Activity: Tune Your Intake
docs.text:
After building, run these tuning tests:

Test Pass Criteria Single object intake Object is pulled in on first contact, 9/10 attempts Moving intake Object is intaked while robot is driving forward at half speed Continuous flow Feed 3 objects in sequence — no jams, no stalls Reverse Motor reversed, object is ejected cleanly

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Compression is king — calculate it, build it, test it, tune it
  2. Deadzone kills flow — identify and eliminate every gap in contact
  3. Free-spin before motor — if it does not spin freely by hand, a motor will not save it
  4. Iterate on the field — the best intake is tuned through testing, not just designed in CAD

docs.title: Next Up
docs.text: Module 4.3: Linear Slider — build a mechanism that extends and retracts for reaching objectives.

docs.title: Module 4.3: Linear Slider
docs.text: Sometimes you need to reach farther than your robot is wide. A linear slider extends and retracts on rails, letting you push, rake, or deploy mechanisms beyond your robot's footprint. In this module, you will design and build a linear slider "ball rake" that deploys on a one-time release mechanism.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Understand linear motion concepts (rails, bearings, travel distance)
  • Design a linear slider mechanism in Onshape CAD
  • Build a functional slider using VEX linear slide rails
  • Implement a one-time release deployment mechanism
  • Test and tune slider travel and deployment

docs.title: What Is a Linear Slider?
docs.text:
A linear slider is a mechanism that moves in a straight line — forward and back, up and down, or side to side. Unlike rotational mechanisms (arms, 4-bars), a slider provides pure linear translation.

📷 Photo Needed

Completed linear slider with ball rake on drivetrain

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text:
Component Purpose Rails Guide tracks that define the direction of motion Carriage The moving platform that rides on the rails Bearings/bushings Reduce friction between the carriage and rails End stops Limit the travel distance at both ends Deployment mechanism Releases the slider from its stowed position Ball rake The end effector — a bar or plate that sweeps game objects

📐 Diagram Needed

Slider components labeled: rails, carriage, bearings, end stops, deployment mechanism, rake

docs.title: Linear vs Rotational Motion
docs.text: Linear Slider Rotational Arm Motion path Straight line Arc Reach Consistent height at all extensions Height changes with angle Complexity Moderate (rails, bearings) Simple (single pivot) Force direction Constant along travel axis Changes with angle Best for Pushing/sweeping at consistent height Lifting and placing

docs.title: CAD Activity: Linear Slider Ball Rake
docs.text: Open Lesson 12: Linear Slider in Classes > V5 CT Education.

docs.title: Design Steps
docs.text:
1. Open your drivetrain assembly with existing mechanisms
  2. Determine slider position — front or side of the robot, depending on game strategy
  3. Insert linear slide rails from the VEX library — these are the guide tracks
  4. Design the carriage — a plate or C-channel that rides on the rails
  5. Add a Slider mate between the carriage and the rails
  6. Mount the ball rake — a flat bar or angled plate on the front of the carriage
  7. Design the deployment mechanism — a pin, latch, or rubber band release
  8. Set travel limits — the carriage should not slide off the rails
  9. Animate to verify smooth linear motion

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Linear slider in Onshape with slider mate and travel limits visible

docs.title: Design Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Guideline Travel distance 6-12 inches typical for VEX competitions Rail length Travel distance + carriage length + 2 inches for end stops Carriage width Match the rail spacing — too wide = binding, too narrow = wobble Rake height Slightly above the game object resting height Deployment force Rubber bands — enough to deploy quickly but not violently

docs.title: Rails
docs.text:
Rails define the path of motion. In VEX, you can use:

  • VEX linear slide kit — purpose-built rails with low-friction surfaces
  • C-channel as rails — budget option, use standoffs as bearing surfaces
  • Drawer slides — if rules allow non-VEX parts (check your competition rules)

docs.title: Bearings and Friction
docs.text:
The carriage must slide smoothly on the rails with minimal friction. Options:

Bearing Type Pros Cons Delrin bushings Low friction, durable Requires precise sizing Roller bearings on rails Very smooth Complex to mount Nylon spacers on screws Simple, available Moderate friction PTFE tape on contact surfaces Cheap, easy Wears out, needs replacement

Free-Spin Applies to Sliders Too

Hold the rails at an angle. The carriage should slide freely under gravity. If it sticks, binds, or hesitates, you have too much friction. Fix it before adding any deployment force.

docs.title: Travel Distance
docs.text:
• Too short = mechanism does not reach the game objects effectively
  • Too long = increased weight, more flex, higher chance of binding
  • Measure your need — how far past the robot frame do you need to reach?

docs.title: One-Time Release Deployment
docs.text: The ball rake slider uses a one-time release mechanism — it deploys once at the start of a match (or on a specific trigger) and stays deployed for the rest of the match.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Stowed position: The carriage is held retracted by a pin or latch
  2. Trigger: A servo, pneumatic, or manual release pulls the pin
  3. Deployment: Rubber bands or a spring pull the carriage to the extended position
  4. Lock: End stops prevent the carriage from retracting

📐 Diagram Needed

4-step one-time release: stowed → trigger → deployment → locked

docs.title: Release Mechanism Options
docs.text: Mechanism Complexity Reliability Pin and string Low — pull string to release High — simple mechanics Servo latch Medium — programmatic trigger High — precise timing control Rubber band breakaway Low — force threshold release Medium — inconsistent threshold Pneumatic pin Medium — air-powered Very high — fast and consistent

docs.title: Rubber Band Deployment
docs.text:
For the Foundation Program, use rubber bands for deployment force:

  1. Attach rubber bands from the carriage to the robot frame
  2. When the carriage is retracted, the bands are stretched
  3. When released, the bands pull the carriage forward to the deployed position
  4. Use 2-4 rubber bands — test to find the right amount of force

📷 Photo Needed

Rubber bands stretched from carriage to frame in stowed position

Rubber Band Safety

When testing deployment, keep your face and hands clear of the deployment path. A carriage under rubber band tension deploys fast and hard. Always deploy away from people, and never hold the carriage with your fingers when testing the release.

🎬 GIF/Video Needed

One-time release deploying: pin pulled, carriage snaps forward

docs.title: Build Instructions
docs.text: Once your coach approves your CAD model:

docs.title: Step 1: Mount the Rails
docs.text:
1. Attach linear slide rails to your drivetrain frame
  2. Rails must be parallel — misalignment causes binding
  3. Verify rails are secure and do not flex under load

docs.title: Step 2: Build the Carriage
docs.text:
1. Assemble the carriage plate with bearing surfaces
  2. Test-fit on the rails before adding the ball rake
  3. Check for binding at all positions along the travel

docs.title: Step 3: Add the Ball Rake
docs.text:
1. Mount the rake bar or plate to the front of the carriage
  2. Verify it does not drag on the field surface
  3. Check that it is wide enough to sweep game objects effectively

docs.title: Step 4: Install the Release Mechanism
docs.text:
1. Install the retaining pin or latch
  2. Attach rubber bands from carriage to frame
  3. Retract the carriage and engage the latch
  4. Test deployment — it should be clean and complete

docs.title: Step 5: Test and Tune
docs.text:
1. Deploy the mechanism 10 times — it should work reliably every time
  2. Drive the robot with the slider deployed — check for wobble or drag
  3. Sweep game objects with the ball rake — verify effective contact

docs.title: Testing Checklist
docs.text: Test Pass Criteria Free slide Carriage slides full travel under gravity on tilted rails Deployment Carriage deploys fully and consistently, 10/10 attempts Driving with deployment Robot drives normally with slider deployed, no dragging Ball sweep Rake contacts and moves game objects effectively Durability No loosening or binding after 20 deployment cycles

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Linear motion is different from rotational — sliders provide consistent reach at constant height
  2. Rail alignment is critical — parallel, straight rails prevent binding
  3. Free-slide before deployment — if the carriage does not slide freely, rubber bands will not fix it
  4. One-time release is simple and reliable — do not over-engineer the deployment mechanism
  5. Test deployment safety — rubber band energy is real, keep hands and faces clear

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text:
You have completed all 3 modules in Unit 4. Time to compete:

  • Sumo Bots — push your opponent off the field
  • Sack Attack — intake as many bean bags as possible
  • Ball Sweep — sweep balls to the opponent's side

docs.title: Module 4.1: Wedge & Pushing
docs.text: The simplest mechanism is often the most effective. A well-designed wedge turns your drivetrain into a pushing machine — getting under opponents, controlling field position, and dominating Sumo matches. This module teaches you to design and build a 2-bar wedge with proper leverage, screw joints, and pushing strategy.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Understand leverage and pivot point mechanics
  • Design a 2-bar wedge in Onshape CAD
  • Use screw joints for controlled rotation
  • Build and attach a wedge to your drivetrain
  • Apply pushing strategy concepts (center of gravity, traction, wedge angle)

docs.title: What Is a 2-Bar Wedge?
docs.text:
A 2-bar wedge is a hinged plate that sits at the front of your robot, angled low to the ground. When you drive into another robot, the wedge slides under them, lifting their wheels off the ground and giving you control.

📷 Photo Needed

Completed 2-bar wedge attached to front of drivetrain, resting angle visible

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text:
Component Purpose Wedge plate The angled surface that contacts the opponent or field Pivot bar Connects the wedge to the drivetrain frame, allows rotation Screw joints The pivot points that allow the wedge to angle up and down Hard stops Limit how far the wedge can rotate (prevents over-extension) Bracing Keeps the wedge rigid under impact loads

📐 Diagram Needed

Wedge components labeled: wedge plate, pivot bar, screw joints, hard stops, bracing

docs.title: Why 2-Bar?
docs.text: The "2-bar" refers to the two structural bars (the wedge plate and the mounting bar on the drivetrain). This is the simplest linkage type — one degree of freedom, easy to build, easy to control. More complex wedge systems exist, but the 2-bar is the best starting point.

docs.title: CAD Activity: Design Your Wedge
docs.text: Open Lesson 10: 2-Bar Wedge in Classes > V5 CT Education.

docs.title: Design Steps
docs.text:
1. Open your drivetrain assembly from Unit 3
  2. Identify the mounting points — where will the wedge attach to the front of the frame?
  3. Insert C-channel for the wedge plate — typically a 1x25 or 2x25 flat bar
  4. Add a Revolute mate at the pivot point (this is where the screw joint will be)
  5. Set rotation limits — the wedge should angle from about 5 degrees below horizontal to 30 degrees above
  6. Add bracing — cross-supports to prevent the wedge from bending laterally
  7. Animate the assembly to verify the wedge moves correctly and does not collide with the drivetrain

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

Wedge assembly in Onshape with revolute mate and rotation limits

docs.title: Design Considerations
docs.text:
Parameter Target Why Wedge angle 15-25 degrees from ground Too steep = opponent drives over you. Too flat = no lifting force Ground clearance 1-2mm at tip Close enough to get under opponents, but not dragging Width Full robot width or wider Wider wedge = harder for opponents to drive around Material Steel flat bar or doubled aluminum Must withstand repeated impacts Pivot height As low as possible Lower pivot = better leverage for getting under opponents

📷 Photo Needed

Correct wedge angle (15-25°) vs too steep vs too flat, side by side

docs.title: Screw Joints
docs.text: A screw joint is a connection where a shoulder screw acts as the axle and the parts rotate around it. This is the standard pivot mechanism in VEX robotics.

docs.title: Anatomy of a Screw Joint
docs.text:
Screw head → Spacer → Rotating part → Spacer → Bearing → Fixed part → Nylock nut

📐 Diagram Needed

Screw joint stack: screw head → spacer → rotating part → spacer → bearing → frame → nylock

docs.title: Building a Good Screw Joint
docs.text:
1. Use a shoulder screw (the smooth section acts as the axle)
  2. Add spacers on both sides of the rotating part for lateral stability
  3. Press-fit a bearing into the fixed part to reduce friction
  4. Tighten the nylock nut until snug — the rotating part should spin freely
  5. Test: the rotating part should swing under its own weight when tilted

📷 Photo Needed

Real screw joint close-up: shoulder screw, spacers, bearing, nylock nut visible

Tightness Kills Screw Joints

The most common mistake is over-tightening the nut. A screw joint is not a structural bolt — it is a pivot. If you tighten it like a structural connection, the parts will not rotate. Tighten until the nylock nut just engages, then stop.

docs.title: Screw Joint Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Shoulder screw used (not a standard bolt)
  • [ ] Bearing pressed into fixed part
  • [ ] Spacers on both sides of rotating part
  • [ ] Part rotates freely under its own weight
  • [ ] No lateral wobble (side-to-side play)
  • [ ] Nylock nut engaged but not over-tightened

docs.title: Build Instructions
docs.text: Once your coach approves your CAD model:

docs.title: Step 1: Prepare the Wedge Plate
docs.text:
1. Select your C-channel or flat bar
  2. Mark and drill any additional holes if needed
  3. Deburr all cut edges (sharp metal = cut fingers)

docs.title: Step 2: Install Screw Joints
docs.text:
1. Identify the pivot holes on both the wedge and the drivetrain frame
  2. Install shoulder screws with proper spacer and bearing stack
  3. Test rotation — the wedge should swing freely

docs.title: Step 3: Add Hard Stops
docs.text:
1. Install standoffs or screw heads that limit the wedge rotation
  2. The wedge should rest at your designed angle under gravity
  3. It should not be able to rotate past horizontal (or it will dig into the field)

docs.title: Step 4: Add Bracing
docs.text:
1. Cross-brace the wedge if it flexes laterally
  2. A triangle brace from the wedge to the frame adds rigidity without adding weight
  3. Verify bracing does not interfere with wedge rotation

docs.title: Step 5: Test
docs.text:
1. Place the robot on a flat surface
  2. Verify the wedge tip is 1-2mm off the ground
  3. Push the robot forward into a wall — the wedge should deflect up, then return to resting position
  4. Drive into another robot (gently) to test the getting-under action

docs.title: Pushing Strategy
docs.text: A good wedge is only half the battle. Pushing strategy determines whether you win the match.

docs.title: Center of Gravity
docs.text:
• Lower center of gravity = harder to push — your robot resists tipping
  • Add weight low on the robot (battery on the bottom, brain in the center)
  • Avoid top-heavy configurations

docs.title: Wheel Traction
docs.text:
• More traction = more pushing force — but also slower turning
  • Traction wheels grip the field mat better than omni wheels
  • Consider a mix: traction wheels in the center, omni wheels for turning

docs.title: Wedge Angle and Approach
docs.text:
Approach Result Hit head-on, straight Best pushing force, wedge gets under cleanly Hit at an angle Opponent may slide off the wedge sideways Hit from the side Wedge is not in play, becomes a straight push Get hit from behind You lose — your wedge is on the wrong end

🎬 GIF/Video Needed

Wedge sliding under opponent robot, lifting their wheels off ground

Drive Into Them, Not Past Them

New drivers tend to drive too fast and overshoot. Approach at medium speed, make contact with the wedge, then accelerate to full power. The goal is to get under them and control the push, not to ram them and bounce off.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Simple mechanisms work — a well-built wedge beats a poorly built complex mechanism
  2. Screw joints are the foundation of all VEX pivot mechanisms — learn them well
  3. CAD before build — your coach must approve the design before you touch metal
  4. Strategy matters — the best wedge in the world loses if the driver cannot position it

docs.title: Next Up
docs.text: Module 4.2: Intakes — build a mechanism that actively collects game objects.

docs.title: Unit 5: Competition Ready
docs.text: Everything comes together here. You have built a drivetrain, programmed it, designed mechanisms in CAD, and built them physically. Unit 5 is where you integrate all of those systems into a single competition-ready robot, develop autonomous strategies, and compete in 2v2 alliance matches — just like a real VEX tournament.

docs.title: Unit Overview
docs.text:
Details Modules 3 Field Challenges 1 (multi-round tournament) Estimated Time 8–12 hours Final Deliverable A fully integrated robot competing in 2v2 alliance matches

📷 Photo Needed

Fully integrated robot: all mechanisms, clean wiring, competition-ready

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
