By the end of Unit 5, you will be able to:

  • [x] Integrate multiple mechanisms onto a single robot platform
  • [x] Manage wiring, cable routing, and V5 Brain port assignments
  • [x] Design and program autonomous routines for specific game objectives
  • [x] Analyze a game and develop match strategy
  • [x] Scout opponents and select alliance partners
  • [x] Compete in 2v2 alliance matches with autonomous and driver control periods

docs.title: Module Roadmap
docs.text: Module Title Key Skills 5.1 Robot Integration Wiring, cable management, multi-mechanism mounting, final checklist 5.2 Autonomous Strategy Game analysis, LemLib paths, autonomous testing and iteration 5.3 Match Strategy Scouting, alliance selection, match roles, communication

docs.title: The CTRC Foundation Championship
docs.text:
The capstone of the entire Foundation Program:

Challenge What It Tests 2v2 Alliance Matches Everything — robot quality, autonomous, driving, strategy, teamwork

This is not just a challenge — it is a full tournament. Alliance selection, scouting sheets, autonomous periods, and awards. It simulates the real VEX competition experience so that when you walk into your first actual tournament, nothing feels unfamiliar.

docs.title: How This Unit Is Different
docs.text:
Units 1-4 taught you individual skills in isolation. Unit 5 is about integration — making all of those skills work together under pressure.

Previous Units Unit 5 Build one thing at a time Multiple systems must coexist on one robot Test in controlled conditions Compete against real opponents Work individually Work as an alliance team Unlimited time 60-second matches, every second counts One goal at a time Balance offense, defense, autonomous, and strategy simultaneously

This Is What Competition Feels Like

Real VEX tournaments are chaotic. Robots break. Autonomous routines miss. Alliance partners do unexpected things. The teams that succeed are the ones who prepared for chaos — not the ones who only practiced in perfect conditions. Unit 5 is your preparation for chaos.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting Unit 5, you must have:

  • [x] Completed drivetrain from Unit 1 (functional, reliable)
  • [x] Working driver controls from Unit 2 (responsive, all mechanisms mapped)
  • [x] At least two mechanisms built and attached (from Unit 4)
  • [x] Basic autonomous routine from Unit 2 (drive forward, turn)

If any of these are incomplete, finish them before proceeding. Unit 5 assumes a working robot — there is no time for foundational builds.

docs.title: Tips for Success
docs.text:
Integration Is Where Most Teams Struggle

Building individual mechanisms is the easy part. Making them all work together on one robot without interfering with each other — that is the hard part. Budget extra time for integration. Things that worked perfectly in isolation will fight each other when combined.

Reliability Beats Performance

A robot that scores 20 points every match beats a robot that scores 40 points one match and breaks the next two. Focus on making your systems reliable before making them fast or powerful.

docs.title: Let's Get Started
docs.text: Begin with Module 5.1: Robot Integration — bring all your systems together into one competition-ready machine.

docs.title: Module 5.2: Autonomous Strategy
docs.text: The first 15 seconds of a match are the only seconds where your driving skill does not matter — only your code does. A strong autonomous routine can win or lose a match before the driver even touches the controller. This module teaches you to analyze a game, design autonomous paths, and test until your routine is reliable.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Analyze a game's scoring objectives to prioritize autonomous actions
  • Design autonomous routines that score points efficiently
  • Use LemLib to create accurate movement paths
  • Test and iterate autonomous routines to achieve high reliability
  • Select between multiple autonomous routines based on match situation

docs.title: Step 1: Analyze the Game
docs.text: Before writing a single line of autonomous code, you need to understand the game deeply. For the Foundation Championship, the games are Sack Attack and Ball Sweep from Unit 4.

docs.title: Game Analysis Questions
docs.text: Question Why It Matters What scoring actions give the most points? Prioritize high-value actions in your limited time What can a robot do without an opponent interfering? Autonomous has no defense — focus on uncontested actions Where do game objects start? Your path depends on starting positions What is the fastest path to the first scoring action? Every second counts — minimize travel time What does your alliance partner's autonomous do? Do not compete with your partner for the same objects

docs.title: Sack Attack Autonomous Analysis
docs.text:
Scoring: 1 point per bean bag intaked and scored
Starting condition: Bean bags scattered across the field
No opponent interference during autonomous

Priority actions:
  1. Drive to nearest bean bag cluster (2-3 seconds)
  2. Intake 1-2 bean bags (3-4 seconds)
  3. Drive to scoring zone (3-4 seconds)
  4. Eject bean bags (1-2 seconds)
  5. Repeat if time allows (unlikely in 15 seconds)

Realistic autonomous goal: 1-2 bean bags scored

📐 Diagram Needed

Autonomous path for Sack Attack: start → drive to cluster → intake → scoring zone → eject

docs.title: Ball Sweep Autonomous Analysis
docs.text:
Scoring: Fewer balls on your side at match end
Starting condition: Equal balls on each side
No opponent interference during autonomous

Priority actions:
  1. Drive forward to nearest ball cluster (2-3 seconds)
  2. Sweep/push balls across the center line (5-6 seconds)
  3. Return to favorable position for driver control (3-4 seconds)

Realistic autonomous goal: 3-5 balls swept to opponent's side

📐 Diagram Needed

Autonomous path for Ball Sweep: start → drive forward → sweep balls → return

docs.title: Path Planning Basics
docs.text:
An autonomous path is the sequence of movements your robot makes. Each movement is defined by:

  • Target position (X, Y coordinates on the field)
  • Target heading (which direction the robot faces)
  • Speed (how fast to drive)
  • Actions (run intake, deploy slider, etc.)

docs.title: Using LemLib for Paths
docs.text:
LemLib (from Unit 2) provides odometry-based movement. Your autonomous uses the same chassis.moveTo() and chassis.turnTo() commands you learned earlier.

// Example: Sack Attack autonomous
void autonomous() {
    // Drive to the nearest bean bag cluster
    chassis.moveTo(24, 36, 90, 2000);  // x, y, heading, timeout

    // Start intake
    intake.spin(forward, 100, pct);

    // Drive through the bean bags to intake them
    chassis.moveTo(24, 48, 90, 2000);

    // Drive to scoring zone
    chassis.moveTo(60, 12, 180, 3000);

    // Eject
    intake.spin(reverse, 100, pct);
    wait(500, msec);
    intake.stop();
}

docs.title: Path Design Tips
docs.text:
1. Minimize turns — turning takes time and introduces error
  2. Use straight lines when possible — they are faster and more accurate
  3. Slow down for precision — reduce speed when approaching a scoring position
  4. Add timeouts — if a movement gets stuck, the timeout prevents the robot from waiting forever
  5. Run mechanisms in parallel — start the intake while driving, not after arriving

A Good Autonomous That Works 90% of the Time Beats a Perfect One That Works 50%

Reliability is more important than ambition. A routine that scores 1 bean bag every match is worth more than a routine that tries for 3 but fails half the time. Start simple, prove it works, then add complexity.

docs.title: The Testing Loop
docs.text: Code It → Run It → Measure Error → Adjust → Repeat

docs.title: Testing Protocol
docs.text:
📷 Photo Needed

Field with tape marks at starting position, ready for autonomous testing

  1. Reset robot to exact starting position every time (use tape marks on the field)
  2. Run the autonomous routine from start to finish
  3. Record the result: Did it score? Where did it end up? What went wrong?
  4. Measure the error: How far off was the final position from the target?
  5. Adjust: Modify coordinates, speeds, or timeouts based on the error
  6. Repeat until the routine succeeds 9 out of 10 consecutive runs

docs.title: Testing Log
docs.text:
Keep a written log of every test:

Run Scored? End Position Error Notes 1 No 3" right of target Drifted right on second movement 2 No 2" right Adjusted Y coordinate by -2" 3 Yes 1" left Close, may need minor adjustment 4 Yes < 0.5" Good run 5 Yes < 0.5" Consistent

docs.title: Common Autonomous Issues
docs.text: Issue Likely Cause Fix Robot drifts to one side Unequal wheel friction, IMU calibration Check wheels, recalibrate IMU, adjust heading Robot overshoots target Driving too fast, insufficient braking Reduce speed near target, add explicit braking Robot stops short of target Timeout too short, wheels slipping Increase timeout, check traction Intake misses game object Position error, object moved Increase intake width, add tolerance to position Routine works sometimes Inconsistent starting position Mark exact starting position, reset precisely

docs.title: Step 4: Multiple Autonomous Routines
docs.text:
For the Foundation Championship, you may want different autonomous routines depending on:

  • Which game is being played (Sack Attack vs Ball Sweep)
  • Which starting position you are assigned (left vs right side)
  • What your alliance partner's autonomous does

docs.title: Autonomous Selector
docs.text:
Program a simple selector on the V5 Brain screen:

int autonSelection = 0;

void pre_auton() {
    // Use the Brain screen to select autonomous
    Brain.Screen.printAt(10, 30, "Select Autonomous:");
    Brain.Screen.printAt(10, 60, "1: Sack Attack - Left Start");
    Brain.Screen.printAt(10, 90, "2: Sack Attack - Right Start");
    Brain.Screen.printAt(10, 120, "3: Ball Sweep - Aggressive");
    Brain.Screen.printAt(10, 150, "4: Ball Sweep - Conservative");

    // Read selection from Brain buttons or touch screen
    // Store in autonSelection variable
}

void autonomous() {
    switch(autonSelection) {
        case 1: sackAttackLeft(); break;
        case 2: sackAttackRight(); break;
        case 3: ballSweepAggressive(); break;
        case 4: ballSweepConservative(); break;
        default: simpleForward(); break;
    }
}

🖥️ Screenshot Needed

V5 Brain screen showing autonomous selector menu with 4 options

docs.title: When to Use Each Routine
docs.text: Situation Recommended Routine Partner has no autonomous Use your most aggressive routine — you need to score alone Partner has a strong autonomous Use a complementary routine — cover different field areas You are unsure what will happen Use a conservative routine — score 1 guaranteed point You need to win this match Use your most tested, reliable routine — no experiments

docs.title: Autonomous Strategy for Alliance Play
docs.text: In 2v2 matches, autonomous coordination with your alliance partner is critical.

docs.title: Pre-Match Communication
docs.text:
Before every match, discuss with your partner:

  1. "What does your autonomous do?" — Know their path to avoid collisions
  2. "Which side of the field do you cover?" — Divide the field
  3. "Do you score any specific objects?" — Avoid targeting the same ones
  4. "Where does your robot end up?" — Avoid blocking each other for driver control

docs.title: Collision Avoidance
docs.text:
Autonomous Alliance Collisions Are Preventable

If your robot and your partner's robot are both driving to the same game object during autonomous, they will collide. This wastes both teams' autonomous periods. Always coordinate starting positions and paths before the match.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Analyze before coding — understand the game deeply before writing autonomous
  2. Simple and reliable beats complex and flaky — prove each step works before adding the next
  3. Test 10 times minimum — if it does not work 9/10, it is not ready
  4. Have multiple routines — different situations need different strategies
  5. Coordinate with your partner — autonomous collisions are embarrassing and preventable

docs.title: Next Up
docs.text: Module 5.3: Match Strategy — learn to scout opponents, select alliance partners, and win matches with your brain, not just your robot.

docs.title: Challenge: 2v2 Alliance Matches — The CTRC Foundation Championship
docs.text: This is it. Everything you have learned across all five units comes together here. The Foundation Championship is a full tournament experience — alliance selection, autonomous periods, driver control, scouting, strategy, and awards. When you walk off this field, you will know what a real VEX competition feels like.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Compete in 2v2 alliance matches using the Sack Attack and Ball Sweep game formats from Unit 4. Alliances score points through autonomous routines and driver control, competing in a bracket to determine the Foundation Champion.

docs.title: Tournament Format
docs.text: Details Team Size 2 robots per alliance (4 robots per match) Games Played Sack Attack and Ball Sweep (alternating or coach's choice) Match Format Autonomous period (15 sec) + Driver control (45 sec) = 60 sec total Qualification Round robin (every alliance plays every other alliance) Elimination Top 4 alliances, single elimination bracket Final Best of 3

docs.title: Seeding
docs.text:
Individual students are seeded based on their performance across all previous challenges:

Factor Weight Source Sumo Bots placement 20% Unit 4 Sack Attack best score 20% Unit 4 Ball Sweep record 20% Unit 4 CAD challenge scores 15% Unit 3 Robot reliability rating 15% Coach assessment Autonomous routine score 10% Unit 2 / 5.2

docs.title: Selection Order
docs.text:
1. Students are ranked by their seed score
  2. The #1 seed announces their pick — any other student
  3. That student accepts or declines (one decline allowed)
  4. The #2 seed picks, and so on
  5. Process continues until all alliances are formed
  6. If there is an odd number of students, the last student joins the lowest-seeded alliance as a third member (rotates driving duties)

Alliance Selection Is a Skill

This is not random pairing. You have been scouting all through Units 3 and 4. Use that information. Pick a partner whose robot complements yours and whose autonomous does not conflict with yours. This is the most important decision of the tournament.

docs.title: Match Structure
docs.text:
📷 Photo Needed

Full competition field set up for 2v2 matches with game objects placed

docs.title: Pre-Match (2 minutes)
docs.text:
1. Alliances meet at the field
  2. Partners discuss and confirm:
    • Autonomous selection (which routine each robot runs)
    • Starting positions (left vs right, which side of the field)
    • Match roles (offense vs defense)
    • Communication plan (key callouts)
  3. Robots placed in starting positions
  4. Drivers ready with controllers

docs.title: Autonomous Period (15 seconds)
docs.text:
📷 Photo Needed

Robots running autonomous — drivers with hands off controllers

  1. Coach signals "Autonomous!" — autonomous routines begin
  2. Drivers may not touch controllers during autonomous
  3. Both alliances' robots execute their programmed routines simultaneously
  4. After 15 seconds, coach signals "Driver control!"

docs.title: Driver Control Period (45 seconds)
docs.text:
📷 Photo Needed

2v2 match in progress — all four robots on field

  1. Drivers take control of their robots
  2. Normal gameplay — score game objects, play defense, execute strategy
  3. All mechanisms allowed (intake, slider, wedge)
  4. Alliance communication encouraged (callouts, direction)

docs.title: End of Match
docs.text:
1. Coach signals "Match over!" at 60 seconds
  2. All robots stop immediately
  3. Scoring is tallied:
    • Sack Attack: Count bean bags in each alliance's scoring zone
    • Ball Sweep: Count balls on each half of the field

docs.title: Sack Attack Scoring
docs.text: Action Points Bean bag scored during autonomous 2 points (double value!) Bean bag scored during driver control 1 point Autonomous bonus (alliance that scores more in auto) 3 points

docs.title: Ball Sweep Scoring
docs.text: Result Points Alliance with fewer balls on their half Win Autonomous bonus: alliance that sweeps more balls in auto First ball placement advantage for driver control Tie at buzzer Autonomous winner wins the match

docs.title: Scouting Sheets
docs.text: Scouting sheets are provided to all students before the tournament begins. Use them to track every match you observe.

docs.title: What to Record
docs.text:
For each match you are NOT playing in:

Match: ___  |  Game: Sack Attack / Ball Sweep

Alliance Red:
  Robot A: ________  Autonomous: Y/N  Scored: ___
  Robot B: ________  Autonomous: Y/N  Scored: ___
  Strategy: Offense/Defense/Hybrid
  Communication: Good / Average / Poor

Alliance Blue:
  Robot A: ________  Autonomous: Y/N  Scored: ___
  Robot B: ________  Autonomous: Y/N  Scored: ___
  Strategy: Offense/Defense/Hybrid
  Communication: Good / Average / Poor

Winner: ___________  Score: ___ - ___

Key observations:
________________________________
________________________________

Scout Every Match, Even If You Think You Know

Robots improve during a tournament. A team that lost in qualifications might fix their intake between matches and dominate in eliminations. Keep updating your scouting data after every match.

docs.title: Phase 1: Qualification Rounds (Round Robin)
docs.text:
Every alliance plays every other alliance at least once. The results determine seeding for elimination.

Qualification Result Impact Win +2 ranking points Tie +1 ranking point Loss 0 ranking points Autonomous bonus win +1 additional ranking point

docs.title: Phase 2: Elimination Bracket (Top 4)
docs.text:
Semifinal 1: #1 Seed vs #4 Seed
  Semifinal 2: #2 Seed vs #3 Seed

  Final: Winner of SF1 vs Winner of SF2
  (Best of 3)

📐 Diagram Needed

Single elimination bracket for top 4 alliances: SF1, SF2, Final

docs.title: Between Matches
docs.text:
Teams get 3-5 minutes between matches for:

  • Quick repairs (no major rebuilds)
  • Strategy discussion with partner
  • Autonomous routine adjustments
  • Battery swaps

docs.title: Robot Rules
docs.text:
• Robots must pass inspection before the tournament (size, safety, no weapons)
  • Robots must be the student's own build from Units 1-4 (no outside robots)
  • One robot per student (no sharing or swapping robots between matches)
  • Battery must be charged above 50% at match start

docs.title: Match Rules
docs.text:
• No intentional damage to opponent robots
  • No pinning an opponent for more than 5 seconds (must release)
  • No reaching outside the field boundary
  • Autonomous routines must be the student's own code
  • If a robot disconnects, the match continues — no restarts for disconnections

docs.title: Conduct Rules
docs.text:
• Gracious Professionalism — respect every opponent, partner, and coach
  • No trash talking or unsportsmanlike behavior
  • Celebrate wins respectfully, accept losses gracefully
  • Help opponents if their robot is stuck (after the match)
  • Thank your alliance partner after every match, win or lose

Gracious Professionalism Is Not Optional

In VEX Robotics, Gracious Professionalism is a core value. Teams that win with poor sportsmanship are not respected. Teams that lose with grace are. The Foundation Championship enforces this — repeated unsportsmanlike conduct results in disqualification.

docs.title: Awards
docs.text:
The Foundation Championship concludes with an awards ceremony recognizing excellence across multiple categories:

Award Criteria Tournament Champion Winning alliance in the elimination bracket Best Driver Voted by peers and coaches — smoothest, most effective driver Best Autonomous Most reliable, highest-scoring autonomous routine Best Strategy Alliance that demonstrated the smartest match strategy Most Improved Student who showed the greatest improvement from Unit 1 to Unit 5 Gracious Professional Student who best exemplified sportsmanship and teamwork Best Design Best overall robot design (CAD quality + build quality + functionality)

docs.title: Award Voting
docs.text:
• Tournament Champion: Determined by match results
  • Best Driver, Best Strategy: Voted by peers (anonymous ballot)
  • Best Autonomous, Best Design: Selected by coaches
  • Most Improved, Gracious Professional: Selected by coaches with peer input

docs.title: Post-Tournament Debrief
docs.text: After awards, the coach leads a group debrief covering:

docs.title: Individual Reflection
docs.text:
Each student answers (written or verbal):

  1. What am I most proud of from the Foundation Program?
  2. What was the hardest thing I learned to do?
  3. What would I do differently if I started over?
  4. How did working with an alliance partner change my approach?
  5. What do I want to learn next?

docs.title: Group Discussion
docs.text:
1. What robot designs were most successful? What did they have in common?
  2. How important was autonomous in deciding match outcomes?
  3. What role did strategy play compared to pure robot quality?
  4. How did communication between partners affect alliance performance?
  5. What surprised you most about the competition experience?

docs.title: What Comes Next
docs.text:
The Foundation Program is complete. You have:

  • [x] Built a drivetrain from scratch
  • [x] Programmed driver controls and autonomous routines
  • [x] Designed robot components in Onshape CAD
  • [x] Built and integrated multiple game mechanisms
  • [x] Competed in simulated match play with strategy and scouting

You are now ready for the CTRC Competition Season. The skills you learned here are the foundation — now it is time to apply them to a real VEX game with real stakes.

The Foundation Is Just the Beginning

Everything you learned in this program was foundational. Real competition robots are more complex, real tournaments are longer, and real challenges are harder. But you now have the vocabulary, the skills, and the mindset to tackle all of it. Go build something great.

docs.title: Return to Foundation Program
docs.text: Foundation Program Home — review the full program structure.

docs.title: Module 5.3: Match Strategy
docs.text: The best robot does not always win. The best team does. Match strategy is about using information to make decisions — scouting opponents, selecting the right alliance partner, choosing the right role during a match, and communicating with your partner in real time. This module teaches you to think strategically, not just mechanically.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Scout other robots and record their capabilities
  • Analyze scouting data to make alliance selection decisions
  • Define and execute match roles (offensive vs defensive)
  • Communicate effectively with alliance partners during matches
  • Adapt strategy mid-tournament based on results

docs.title: What Is Scouting?
docs.text: Scouting is the systematic observation and recording of other robots' performance during matches. It answers the question: "What can that robot do, and how well does it do it?"

docs.title: What to Scout
docs.text: Category What to Observe Why It Matters Drivetrain Speed, turning ability, pushing power Predicts mobility and matchup strength Mechanisms Which mechanisms, how fast, how reliable Predicts scoring ability Autonomous Does it have one? Does it score? Does it collide? Predicts autonomous period outcome Driver skill Smooth driving, quick reactions, good positioning Driver skill wins close matches Reliability Does it break down? Does it stall? Disconnects? Unreliable robots lose matches they should win Strategy Offensive or defensive? Aggressive or passive? Predicts how they will play in your match

docs.title: Scouting Sheet
docs.text:
Use a simple sheet for each robot you observe:

Robot: _______________    Match: _______________

Drivetrain:    [ ] Fast  [ ] Medium  [ ] Slow
Turning:       [ ] Agile  [ ] Average  [ ] Sluggish
Pushing power: [ ] Strong  [ ] Average  [ ] Weak

Mechanisms:
  Intake:      [ ] Yes  [ ] No    Speed: __/10
  Slider/Arm:  [ ] Yes  [ ] No    Speed: __/10
  Wedge:       [ ] Yes  [ ] No    Effectiveness: __/10

Autonomous:
  Has one:     [ ] Yes  [ ] No
  Scores:      [ ] Yes  [ ] No    Points: ___
  Reliable:    [ ] Yes  [ ] No

Driver Skill:  __/10
Reliability:   __/10

Overall Ranking: __/10
Notes: ___________________________________

📷 Photo Needed

Student with clipboard watching match from sideline

docs.title: Scouting Tips
docs.text:
Watch the Losses, Not Just the Wins

A robot that wins every match does not tell you much about its weaknesses. Watch their closest match or their loss — that is where you learn what they struggle with. Every robot has a weakness; your job is to find it.

📷 Photo Needed

Filled-out scouting sheet with real observations

  • Scout every robot, not just the top teams
  • Update your scouting data after every match (robots improve during tournaments)
  • Watch for patterns: does a robot always struggle with a specific task?
  • Note mechanical failures — a robot that broke once will likely break again

docs.title: How Alliance Selection Works
docs.text:
In the Foundation Championship (and real VEX tournaments):

  1. Teams are seeded based on their individual challenge performance
  2. The #1 seed picks first, choosing any other team as their alliance partner
  3. The #2 seed picks next, and so on
  4. If a team is picked, they may accept or decline (in real VEX, they can decline once)
  5. Alliances are locked for the elimination rounds

docs.title: How to Pick the Right Partner
docs.text: Factor Weight Why Autonomous compatibility 30% Two good autonomouses that do not collide = huge advantage Mechanism complementarity 25% Partner should cover what you cannot do Driver skill 20% A great driver with a mediocre robot beats a bad driver with a great robot Reliability 15% A partner who disconnects mid-match costs you the round Communication 10% A partner you can talk to and coordinate with in real time

docs.title: Alliance Selection Mistakes
docs.text: Mistake Why It Is Wrong Picking the robot with the most mechanisms More mechanisms = more things to break Picking your friend regardless of performance Friendship does not win matches — pick the best available partner Ignoring autonomous compatibility Two autonomouses that collide = both teams score 0 Picking a fast robot without checking reliability Speed without reliability = inconsistent results Not scouting before selections You are guessing instead of deciding

docs.title: Match Roles
docs.text: In 2v2 matches, each robot on an alliance should have a defined role. Trying to do the same thing leads to confusion, collisions, and missed opportunities.

docs.title: Offensive Role
docs.text:
The offensive robot focuses on scoring points:

  • Collect and score game objects as fast as possible
  • Target the highest-value scoring actions
  • Avoid getting into pushing matches that waste time
  • Use the entire match time for point-generating actions

Best for: Robots with fast, reliable scoring mechanisms

docs.title: Defensive Role
docs.text:
The defensive robot focuses on preventing the opponents from scoring:

  • Block opponent robots from reaching game objects
  • Push opponents away from scoring zones
  • Use your wedge to control opponent positioning
  • Protect scored objects from being taken back

Best for: Robots with strong drivetrains and good wedges, even if scoring mechanisms are slower

📐 Diagram Needed

Field diagram: offensive robot at scoring zones, defensive robot blocking opponents

docs.title: Hybrid Role
docs.text:
Sometimes both robots play a hybrid role:

  • Each covers half the field (left/right or front/back)
  • Both score, but each focuses on different areas
  • If one robot breaks down, the other can cover the whole field

Best for: Alliances where both robots have similar capabilities

docs.title: Role Assignment Checklist
docs.text:
Before each match, discuss with your partner:

  1. "Who plays offense and who plays defense?"
  2. "Which side of the field does each of us cover?"
  3. "If the plan is not working at 30 seconds, what do we switch to?"
  4. "What do we do if one of us breaks down?"

docs.title: Pre-Match (30 seconds before start)
docs.text:
• Confirm roles: "I am offense, you are defense"
  • Confirm autonomous: "My auto goes left, yours goes right"
  • Confirm contingency: "If I break down, you switch to offense"

docs.title: During the Match
docs.text:
Effective communication during a 60-second match must be short, loud, and clear:

Good Callout What It Means "I've got left side!" I am covering the left, you cover the right "Ball at the wall!" There is an uncontested game object by the wall "Block number 3!" Prevent opponent robot #3 from scoring "I'm stuck!" I need you to come push me free "Switch!" Change to our backup plan "Ten seconds!" Time is almost up — last push

docs.title: Communication Mistakes
docs.text:
Mistake Problem Not talking at all Robots duplicate effort or leave areas uncovered Talking too much Driver cannot focus on driving and talking Using long sentences Takes too long, driver misses the point Giving vague instructions "Get that thing!" — what thing? Where? Disagreeing mid-match Wastes time, creates confusion

Practice Communication Before the Tournament

Run 2-3 practice matches with your alliance partner before the real tournament. The goal is not just to practice driving — it is to practice talking to each other under pressure. Develop a shared vocabulary so that one word communicates an entire strategy.

docs.title: Between Matches
docs.text:
After every match, do a 2-minute debrief with your partner:

  1. What worked? — Keep doing it
  2. What failed? — Fix it or change strategy
  3. What did the opponent do that we did not expect? — Adapt
  4. Do we need to change roles? — Maybe defense is not working
  5. Any mechanical issues to fix? — Quick repairs between matches

docs.title: When to Change Strategy
docs.text: Signal Adaptation Opponent's autonomous beats yours consistently Change starting position or autonomous routine Your intake keeps jamming Switch to defense-focused play Partner's robot is faster than expected Let them play offense, you play defense A specific opponent is dominating Focus defense specifically on that robot You are winning comfortably Play conservative, do not risk breakdowns You are losing and must win Play aggressive, accept higher risk for higher reward

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Scouting is not optional — information is the foundation of good strategy
  2. Pick partners based on data — not friendship or reputation
  3. Define roles before the match — both robots doing the same thing is worse than one doing nothing
  4. Communicate with short, clear callouts — drivers cannot process paragraphs
  5. Adapt between matches — the team that learns fastest wins the tournament

docs.title: Next Up
docs.text: 2v2 Alliance Matches — the Foundation Championship. Time to put everything together.

docs.title: Module 5.1: Robot Integration
docs.text: A pile of mechanisms is not a robot. Integration is the process of combining your drivetrain, intake, slider, wedge, electronics, and wiring into a single machine that works reliably as a unified system. This module covers the practical details that separate a collection of parts from a competition-ready robot.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this module, you will be able to:

  • Attach multiple mechanisms to one drivetrain without interference
  • Plan and execute V5 Brain port assignments
  • Route and manage cables cleanly
  • Manage robot weight and balance
  • Complete a pre-competition robot checklist

docs.title: Attaching Multiple Mechanisms
docs.text:
Your robot from Units 1 and 4 likely has a drivetrain with a wedge, intake, and linear slider. Getting them all on one robot without conflicts is the first challenge.

📷 Photo Needed

Robot with wedge, intake, and slider all mounted, showing coexistence

docs.title: Interference Checklist
docs.text:
Before mounting a new mechanism, check:

Check How to Verify Physical clearance Move every mechanism to its full range of motion. Do any parts collide? Screw head interference Are screw heads on one mechanism catching on parts of another? Cable clearance Will wires get pinched, pulled, or cut by moving mechanisms? Shared mounting points Are two mechanisms trying to use the same holes? Weight distribution Will adding this mechanism make the robot tip or become unbalanced?

docs.title: Mounting Priority
docs.text:
When conflicts arise, prioritize in this order:

  1. Drivetrain — the foundation, never compromise it
  2. Primary scoring mechanism — the one that earns the most points (usually intake)
  3. Secondary mechanism — the one that supplements scoring (slider, arm)
  4. Defensive mechanism — wedge or plow (can be simplified if space is tight)

If It Doesn't Fit, Redesign — Don't Force

Forcing a mechanism into a space where it does not fit leads to bent metal, stripped screws, and unreliable performance. If two mechanisms conflict, go back to CAD and redesign the mounting. Thirty minutes in Onshape is better than two hours of fighting with pliers.

docs.title: V5 Brain Port Assignment
docs.text: The V5 Brain has 21 Smart Ports. Plan your assignments before plugging anything in.

docs.title: Recommended Port Layout
docs.text:
Port Range Assignment Rationale 1-4 Drivetrain motors (left side) Grouped for easy identification 5-8 Drivetrain motors (right side) Grouped for easy identification 9-12 Mechanism motors (intake, slider) Central ports for mechanisms 13-16 Sensors (inertial, rotation, distance) Keep sensor ports together 17-20 Additional mechanisms or pneumatics Reserve for expansion 21 Inertial sensor (convention) Many teams use port 21 for IMU

📷 Photo Needed

V5 Brain with labeled port assignments matching the recommended layout

docs.title: Port Assignment Table
docs.text:
Create a physical label or document that maps every port:

Port 1:  Left front drive motor
Port 2:  Left rear drive motor
Port 3:  (unused)
Port 4:  (unused)
Port 5:  Right front drive motor (reversed)
Port 6:  Right rear drive motor (reversed)
Port 7:  (unused)
Port 8:  (unused)
Port 9:  Intake motor
Port 10: Linear slider motor (if motorized)
Port 11: (unused)
Port 12: (unused)
Port 13: Rotation sensor (intake)
Port 14: (unused)
Port 15: (unused)
Port 16: Distance sensor (front)
Port 17-20: (reserved)
Port 21: Inertial sensor

Label Your Ports

Write the port number on a small piece of tape and wrap it around each cable near the Brain end. When a cable comes unplugged during a match (and it will), you need to know instantly where it goes. Do not rely on memory.

📷 Photo Needed

Tape labels on cables near Brain connector for easy identification

docs.title: Cable Routing Best Practices
docs.text: Bad wiring is the number one cause of robot failures at competitions. A loose cable can disable your entire robot at the worst possible moment.

docs.title: The Rules of Cable Management
docs.text:
1. Every cable must be secured — no free-hanging wires
  2. Cables must not cross moving parts — the surest way to cut a wire
  3. Leave slack at connection points — too tight and the cable pulls out during impacts
  4. Bundle parallel cables — use zip ties to group cables running in the same direction
  5. Route along the frame — cables should follow the structural members, not span open air

📷 Photo Needed

Clean cable routing: zip-tied along frame, bundled, with service loops

📷 Photo Needed

Bad cable routing: loose wires, cables crossing gears, no zip ties

docs.title: Cable Routing Tools
docs.text: Tool Use Zip ties Bundle cables, secure to frame (cut excess tail flush) Adhesive cable clips Stick to flat surfaces, guide cables along specific paths Wire loom / spiral wrap Protect cables in high-abrasion areas Velcro straps Reusable ties for cables you disconnect frequently

docs.title: Danger Zones
docs.text:
These are the areas where cables are most likely to get damaged:

Zone Risk Protection Near spinning gears Cable gets caught and shredded Route behind guards, secure tightly Across pivoting joints Cable stretches and tears Add a service loop (extra slack in a loop) Near sharp metal edges Cable insulation gets cut Add edge protection or reroute At the Brain connector Repeated pulling loosens the plug Secure cable to frame near the port

docs.title: Weight Management
docs.text: VEX robots have a weight limit (check current season rules). Even if you are under the limit, weight distribution affects performance.

docs.title: Weight Distribution Effects
docs.text: Distribution Effect Front-heavy Robot tips forward during stops, rear wheels lose traction Rear-heavy Intake struggles to grip, front wheels lift during acceleration Top-heavy Robot tips during turns, unstable on field elements Balanced (center, low) Best traction, most stable, easiest to drive

docs.title: How to Balance
docs.text:
1. Place the battery in the center-bottom of the robot
  2. Keep the Brain centered (or slightly rear)
  3. If the robot tips in one direction, move heavy components opposite
  4. Add counterweight as a last resort (every gram of counterweight is wasted capacity)

docs.title: Weight Reduction Strategies
docs.text:
If you are over the limit:

  • Use aluminum instead of steel where strength allows
  • Remove unnecessary bracing (keep only what is structurally required)
  • Use fewer, larger screws instead of many small screws
  • Shorten standoffs and C-channels to minimum required lengths
  • Replace heavy components with lighter alternatives (smaller motor cartridges if torque allows)

docs.title: Final Robot Checklist
docs.text: Before any competition match, run through this checklist:

docs.title: Structural
docs.text:
• [ ] All screws tightened (no loose hardware)
  • [ ] No cracks in metal (especially at stress points)
  • [ ] Mechanisms move through full range without interference
  • [ ] Robot fits within size constraints (18" x 18" x 18")

docs.title: Electronics
docs.text:
• [ ] Battery fully charged
  • [ ] All motor cables plugged into correct ports
  • [ ] All sensor cables plugged into correct ports
  • [ ] No loose connections (wiggle each cable to check)
  • [ ] V5 Brain firmware up to date
  • [ ] Radio plugged in and functional

docs.title: Wiring
docs.text:
• [ ] All cables zip-tied or clipped to frame
  • [ ] No cables crossing moving parts
  • [ ] Service loops at pivot points
  • [ ] Port labels on all cables

docs.title: Mechanisms
docs.text:
• [ ] Drivetrain free-spins on all wheels
  • [ ] Intake free-spins, correct compression verified
  • [ ] Linear slider deploys and retracts smoothly
  • [ ] Wedge pivots freely, returns to resting position

docs.title: Software
docs.text:
• [ ] Driver controls mapped correctly (all buttons tested)
  • [ ] Autonomous routine loaded and tested
  • [ ] Motor directions verified (no inverted motors)

Run This Checklist Before Every Match

Not just once — before every match. Things come loose from impacts, cables get pulled during handling, and batteries die between matches. A 2-minute checklist prevents a 0-point match.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
1. Integration is harder than building — plan for things to conflict and budget time to resolve
  2. Port assignments prevent confusion — label everything, document everything
  3. Cable management prevents failures — the best-designed robot fails if a wire comes loose
  4. Weight balance affects driving — center the heavy stuff, keep it low
  5. The checklist is not optional — run it every time, no exceptions

docs.title: Next Up
docs.text: Module 5.2: Autonomous Strategy — program your robot to score on its own.

docs.title: Mechanism Examples
docs.text:
This gallery showcases real-world VEX mechanism designs organized by type. Study these examples to understand how top teams approach common design challenges. Each example includes a description of the design decisions and key features.

Learn by Studying

One of the fastest ways to improve as a designer is to study what others have built. Look at the structural design, power transmission choices, material selection, and packaging. Ask yourself: "Why did they make that choice?"

docs.title: Over-the-Bumper Roller Intake
docs.text:
Common approach for ground game piece acquisition.

Key features typically seen in top designs:

  • Compliant wheels (2-3 inch) on hex shafts for game piece grip
  • Spring-loaded deployment with motor-controlled retraction
  • Polycarbonate or thin aluminum side plates to minimize weight
  • Power transmission through belt or chain from a NEO 550
  • Hard stops at both deployed and retracted positions
  • Surgical tubing or flex wheels for enhanced grip on certain game pieces

docs.title: Funnel/Passive Intake
docs.text:
Used when game pieces can be guided without active mechanisms.

Key features:

  • Carefully shaped polycarbonate funnels that guide game pieces
  • Compliant bumper covers that help center game pieces on contact
  • Geometry designed around the specific game piece dimensions
  • Often combined with an active roller at the throat of the funnel

docs.title: Single-Flywheel Shooter
docs.text:
The most common shooter configuration in VEX.

Key design elements:

  • 4-inch Colson or compliant wheels as the flywheel
  • Curved polycarbonate hood with adjustable exit angle
  • Two NEO motors on the flywheel shaft for fast spin-up and recovery
  • Direct drive or 1:1 belt from motors to flywheel
  • Compression set by the gap between flywheel and hood
  • Side plates thick enough to support the flywheel shaft without deflection

docs.title: Dual-Flywheel Shooter
docs.text:
Used when spin control is critical.

Key design elements:

  • Independent top and bottom flywheels with separate motors
  • Differential speed between top and bottom controls backspin/topspin
  • More consistent shots at varying distances
  • Typically heavier and more complex than single-flywheel

docs.title: Two-Stage Cascade Elevator
docs.text:
The standard vertical lift in VEX.

Key design elements:

  • 1"x2" box tube rails for each stage
  • V-groove bearings riding on tube edges for smooth sliding
  • Cascade rigging with Dyneema or spectra rope
  • Counterbalance using constant-force springs or gas springs
  • Cross bracing between rails for torsional rigidity
  • Belt or cable spool drive from NEO motor with 10:1-20:1 gearbox

docs.title: Three-Stage Cascade Elevator
docs.text:
For extra-long vertical reach.

Key design elements:

  • Same principles as two-stage but with an additional nesting stage
  • Requires careful attention to stage overlap at full extension
  • More counterbalancing needed due to greater height and weight
  • Wider base stage to accommodate nested stages

docs.title: Dead Axle Arm Pivot
docs.text:
The preferred arm pivot design for high-load applications.

Key design elements:

  • 1" round dead axle shaft fixed to the frame
  • Arm rotates on bearings around the dead axle
  • Torque transmitted through a large sprocket (48-60T) bolted to the arm hub
  • #25 chain or belt from a MAXPlanetary gearbox (100:1+ ratio)
  • Both-end shaft support with thick (0.25") tower plates
  • Gas spring counterbalance to reduce motor load

docs.title: Double-Jointed Arm
docs.text:
For maximum reach envelope.

Key design elements:

  • Two arm segments with independent pivot points
  • Careful counterbalancing at each joint
  • Cable/chain routing that doesn't interfere with arm motion
  • Software-controlled coordinated joint movement
  • Significant structural reinforcement at each pivot

docs.title: Swerve Drive
docs.text:
The dominant drivetrain in modern competitive VEX.

Key design elements:

  • Four swerve modules (SDS MK4i, REV MAXSwerve, WCP SwerveX, etc.)
  • 28"x28" or similar square frame
  • Modules inset 2.5-3 inches from frame corners for bumper clearance
  • Bellypan riveted to frame rails for rigidity
  • Battery centered for balance
  • Electronics packaged around the center of the robot

docs.title: West Coast Drive (Tank Drive)
docs.text:
A simpler, proven drivetrain option.

Key design elements:

  • 6 wheel drop-center (center wheel slightly lower for turning)
  • Direct-driven center wheel with outer wheels connected by chain or belt
  • Integrated gearbox in the frame rail
  • Single-speed typically 5:1 to 8:1

docs.title: Hook and Winch Climber
docs.text:
Simple and reliable.

Key design elements:

  • Deployable hook (pneumatic or motorized) that reaches up to a bar
  • Winch drum driven by a high-torque motor (NEO with 50:1+ gearbox)
  • Dyneema or steel cable wound on the drum
  • Ratchet mechanism to prevent back-driving
  • Compact packaging, often mounted on the back of the robot

docs.title: How to Study These Examples
docs.text:
When looking at a mechanism example, ask yourself:

  1. What problem does this solve? — What game task does this mechanism accomplish?
  2. Why this approach? — What alternatives exist, and why was this one chosen?
  3. What makes it work well? — What specific design features contribute to its effectiveness?
  4. What are the tradeoffs? — What did the team give up by choosing this approach?
  5. How would I improve it? — What would you change if you were designing a similar mechanism?

Find More Examples

The best sources for VEX mechanism examples are:

  • VEX Forum — Robot showcase threads
  • RobotEvents — Match videos and event results
  • Team reveals — Teams that share their design process publicly
  • Team CAD releases — Many top teams publish their Onshape documents after the season

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: CTRC Projects are hands-on builds that take the foundation skills you've learned and apply them to real, tangible creations. These aren't worksheets or simulations — you're designing, building, and testing physical machines.

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
The V5 Foundation Program teaches you how to build robots. Projects teach you why it matters. Each project is a complete engineering challenge: you start with requirements, design a solution, fabricate it, wire it up, and put it to the test.

Projects are where you stop following instructions and start making decisions.

docs.title: Available Projects
docs.text:
• BattleBots

    Design, build, and fight a ~500g combat robot with spinning weapons. 3D printed, brushless-powered, radio-controlled carnage.

    Start Building

docs.title: Coming Soon
docs.text:
We're developing more projects to expand the CTRC build experience. Future projects may include:

  • Sumo Bots — autonomous line-following sumo wrestling robots
  • Capture the Flag Bots — multi-robot team strategy challenges
  • Speed Trials — lightweight drag racers optimized for straight-line speed

Suggest a Project

Have an idea for a project? Talk to a mentor or submit a suggestion through the club. The best projects come from students who want to build something cool.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
All projects assume you have completed at least Unit 1 (Drivetrain Mastery) of the V5 Foundation Program. Some projects may require additional units — check each project's overview page for specific prerequisites.

You should be comfortable with:

  • [x] Basic mechanical assembly
  • [x] Using hand tools safely
  • [x] Reading wiring diagrams
  • [x] Basic Onshape CAD skills

docs.title: How Projects Work
docs.text:
Phase What You Do What You Deliver Research Study the rules, understand the constraints Requirements document Design CAD your solution in Onshape Reviewed 3D model Fabricate 3D print, cut, and prepare parts Physical components Assemble Wire, mount, and integrate everything Working machine Test Verify it works, iterate on problems Tested and tuned build Compete Put it to the test against others Battle scars and lessons learned

Document Everything

Take photos at every stage. Keep notes on what worked and what didn't. The best engineers learn as much from failures as from successes — but only if they remember what happened.

docs.title: BattleBots Project
docs.text: Design, build, and fight a ~500g combat robot with spinning weapons. This is the CTRC in-house BattleBots competition — your chance to turn CAD skills, 3D printing knowledge, and electrical wiring into a machine built for destruction.

docs.title: The Goal
docs.text:
Build a radio-controlled combat robot that fits within a 6" x 6" footprint, weighs approximately 500g, and features a spinning weapon (vertical or horizontal). Then fight it against your teammates' bots in the CTRC arena.

This project covers the full engineering process: design, CAD, fabrication, wiring, testing, and competition.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
By completing the BattleBots project, you will:

  • [x] Design a complete robot system under strict weight and size constraints
  • [x] Make engineering tradeoffs between weapon power, armor, and drivetrain
  • [x] CAD a full robot in Onshape with proper mass analysis
  • [x] 3D print structural and functional parts using PLA+, TPU, and PETG
  • [x] Wire and configure a Malenki Nano all-in-one control board
  • [x] Set up failsafes and follow strict safety protocols for spinning weapons
  • [x] Test, iterate, and compete

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting this project, you must have completed:

  • [x] V5 Foundation Unit 1 (Drivetrain Mastery) — minimum requirement
  • [x] Basic Onshape CAD skills (sketching, part modeling, assemblies)
  • [x] Familiarity with 3D printing (or willingness to learn fast)

Recommended

Foundation Unit 3 (Onshape CAD) will make the design phase significantly smoother. If you haven't completed it, expect to spend extra time on the CAD step.

docs.title: Project Phases
docs.text: Phase Module Estimated Time 1 Rules & Overview 30 min 2 Robot Archetypes 45 min 3 Weapon Design 1-2 hours 4 Drivetrain (N20 Motors) 1-2 hours 5 CAD Your Bot 4-8 hours 6 3D Printing Guide 2-4 hours (plus print time) 7 Malenki Nano Setup 1-2 hours 8 Assembly & Wiring 2-3 hours 9 Testing & Safety 1-2 hours Total 13-24 hours

docs.title: Key Components
docs.text: Component Purpose Notes N20 3V 300RPM motors Drive motors Brushed DC with integrated gearbox Brushless motor Weapon motor Powers the spinner Malenki Nano Control board Receiver + 2x brushed ESC + 1x brushless ESC LiPo battery Power source Requires safe handling procedures 3D printed parts Chassis, armor, weapon PLA+, TPU, PETG Radio transmitter Driver control Binds to Malenki Nano receiver

docs.title: Reference Resources
docs.text:
This project draws from the following community resources:

  • "Beetleweights for Dummies" guide — comprehensive beetleweight building reference
  • Duke Combat Robotics wiki — design principles and build guides
  • MakerMuse BattleBot tutorial — Malenki Nano wiring and setup video

Safety First

Combat robots — even small ones — have spinning weapons that can cause serious injury. Every safety rule in this guide exists because someone got hurt when it wasn't followed. Read the Testing & Safety module before you power anything on.

docs.title: Ready?
docs.text:
Start with the Rules & Overview to understand what you're building within, then work through each module in order.

Good luck. Build something terrifying.

docs.title: 3D Printing Guide
docs.text:
Your CAD model is done — now it's time to turn those STL files into real, physical parts. 3D printing is one of the most powerful tools in small combat robotics: you can design a part in the morning, print it in the afternoon, and test it in the arena by evening. When it breaks, you print another one.

This guide covers material selection, print settings, orientation strategy, and common issues for combat robot parts.

docs.title: Materials for Combat Robots
docs.text: Not all 3D printing filaments are created equal. For a combat robot, you need materials that can survive impacts, vibration, and the occasional direct hit from a spinning weapon. Here are the three materials you'll use.

docs.title: PLA+ (Polylactic Acid Plus)
docs.text:
Best for: Chassis, structural frames, motor mounts, weapons

Property Rating Rigidity High Impact resistance Moderate Print difficulty Easy Cost Low Temperature resistance Low (softens at ~60°C)

PLA+ is the workhorse material. It's easy to print, dimensionally accurate, and rigid. The "+" variants (eSUN PLA+, Polymaker PLA Pro, etc.) add toughness compared to standard PLA.

How it fails: PLA+ cracks and shatters on hard impacts rather than deforming. Think of it like glass — strong until it breaks, then it breaks dramatically. This is fine for structural parts because you can print replacements quickly.

docs.title: TPU 95A (Thermoplastic Polyurethane)
docs.text:
Best for: Armor panels, weapon hubs, bumpers, wheel treads

Property Rating Rigidity Low (flexible) Impact resistance Very High Print difficulty Hard Cost Moderate Temperature resistance Moderate

TPU is a flexible filament that absorbs impacts instead of cracking. It bends, stretches, and bounces back. This makes it ideal for parts that take direct hits — armor panels, weapon hubs (to protect the motor), and bumpers.

How it fails: TPU doesn't shatter — it tears or stretches permanently when pushed beyond its limits. It also deforms under sustained load, so it's bad for structural components that need to stay rigid.

TPU is Harder to Print

TPU requires slower print speeds (20-30mm/s), a direct drive extruder works best (Bowden tubes struggle with flexible filament), and retraction settings need careful tuning. If you've never printed TPU before, do a test print first.

docs.title: PETG (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol)
docs.text:
Best for: Parts that need a balance of strength and toughness

Property Rating Rigidity Moderate-High Impact resistance High Print difficulty Moderate Cost Low-Moderate Temperature resistance Moderate (~80°C)

PETG sits between PLA+ and TPU. It's stiffer than TPU but tougher than PLA+. It bends before breaking, which can be an advantage in combat — a PETG part might survive a hit that would shatter PLA+.

How it fails: PETG deforms and bends rather than cracking cleanly. Deformed parts may not fit together properly after a fight.

docs.title: Recommended Material Assignments
docs.text: Part Material Why Chassis / frame PLA+ Rigid, dimensionally accurate Weapon (blade/drum) PLA+ at 80-100% infill Maximum rigidity and mass Weapon hub TPU 95A Absorbs impact shock, protects motor Armor panels TPU 95A or PETG Survives direct hits without shattering Motor mounts PLA+ Needs precise dimensions Wheels PLA+ or TPU PLA+ for rigidity, TPU for grip Wedge / front plow PETG or TPU Takes repeated frontal impacts Battery tray PLA+ Structural, needs precise fit

docs.title: PLA+ Settings (Structural Parts)
docs.text:
Setting Value Notes Layer height 0.2mm Good balance of strength and speed Infill 40-60% For chassis and mounts Infill pattern Gyroid or Grid Gyroid is strongest in all directions Wall perimeters 4+ This is the most important strength setting Top/bottom layers 5+ Prevents puncture from above/below Print speed 50-60mm/s Standard for PLA+ Nozzle temp 210-220°C Check your filament's recommendation Bed temp 60°C Standard for PLA+

Print Settings

Teacher will provide optimized slicer profiles for all materials. Focus on understanding layer orientation principles below.

docs.title: PLA+ Settings (Weapon — Maximum Strength)
docs.text:
Setting Value Notes Layer height 0.2mm Infill 80-100% Maximum mass and rigidity Infill pattern Rectilinear / Grid Dense and strong Wall perimeters 5+ Maximum shell strength Top/bottom layers 6+ Print speed 40-50mm/s Slower for better adhesion

Print Settings

Teacher will provide optimized weapon slicer profiles. Your focus: export STLs and understand orientation.

docs.title: TPU 95A Settings
docs.text:
Setting Value Notes Layer height 0.2mm Infill 40-60% Depends on desired flexibility Wall perimeters 3-4 Print speed 20-30mm/s SLOW — TPU needs time Retraction Minimal or disabled Flexible filament jams with heavy retraction Nozzle temp 220-235°C Higher than PLA Bed temp 50-60°C

Print Settings

Teacher will provide TPU slicer profiles. TPU is challenging to print - focus on correct part orientation.

docs.title: Print Orientation
docs.text: Layer orientation is the single most important decision after material choice. 3D printed parts are weakest in the Z direction (between layers). A part that's strong when hit from the side might snap like a cracker when hit from the top.

docs.title: Understanding Layer Lines and Strength
docs.text:
When 3D printing, each layer bonds to the previous layer. This bond is the weakest point. Look at this comparison:

Key Principle: Orient parts so that impact forces don't try to pull layers apart.

  • Red (BAD): Horizontal holes have weak points where layer lines concentrate stress around the hole
  • Green (GOOD): Vertical holes have solid lines of plastic surrounding them, distributing forces evenly

This is especially critical for motor mounts, weapon axles, and chassis stress points.

docs.title: Rules of Thumb
docs.text:
1. Identify the primary impact direction — which face will get hit?
  2. Orient layers perpendicular to the impact — layers should be stacked in the direction of the force, not across it
  3. Never have layer lines parallel to the impact surface — this is the weakest orientation

docs.title: Orientation Examples
docs.text:
Part Best Orientation Why Weapon bar Flat on bed, widest face down Impact is on the edges, layers stack through the thickness Chassis Open-side up (like a tray) Forces are mostly horizontal, layers stack vertically Armor panel Flat on bed Impact hits the face, layers are perpendicular to the hit Wheel Axle vertical (hub side down) Rotational forces are distributed across layers Motor mounts Holes running vertically through layers See diagram above - vertical holes are strongest

Quick Check Before Printing

Before you slice a part, ask yourself: "If this part gets hit, which direction will the force come from?" Then orient the part so layers are stacked in that direction, not perpendicular to it.

docs.title: Warping
docs.text:
Symptom: Corners of the print lift off the bed, part curves.

Fix:

  • Use a heated bed (60°C for PLA+)
  • Apply glue stick or hairspray to the bed
  • Add a brim (5-10mm) in slicer settings
  • Ensure no drafts near the printer

docs.title: Stringing
docs.text:
Symptom: Thin strings of filament between separate features.

Fix:

  • Increase retraction distance (1-2mm for direct drive, 4-6mm for Bowden)
  • Increase retraction speed
  • Lower nozzle temperature by 5°C
  • Note: Stringing is cosmetic — it doesn't affect strength. Clean it with a heat gun or knife.

docs.title: Poor Layer Adhesion
docs.text:
Symptom: Layers separate easily, part is weak between layers.

Fix:

  • Increase nozzle temperature by 5-10°C
  • Slow down print speed
  • Increase flow rate slightly
  • Ensure the nozzle isn't partially clogged

docs.title: Elephant's Foot
docs.text:
Symptom: First layer is squished wider than the rest of the part.

Fix:

  • Increase Z offset slightly (nozzle is too close to bed)
  • Reduce first layer flow rate
  • Add a small chamfer to the bottom edge in your CAD model

docs.title: Test Before You Fight
docs.text:
Print a Test Piece First

Before you print your entire robot, print a small test piece in the same material and settings. Then try to break it.

  • Bend it — does it flex or snap?
  • Hit it with a hammer — does it crack or absorb the impact?
  • Twist it — do the layers delaminate?

If it breaks too easily, increase wall perimeters, increase infill, or switch materials. It's much cheaper to learn this on a test piece than on your finished robot.

docs.title: Print Time Estimates
docs.text:
Part Approximate Print Time Chassis (unibody) 3-6 hours Weapon (drum/bar) 1-3 hours Wheels (x2) 30-60 min each Armor panels 1-2 hours each Weapon hub (TPU) 30-60 min Total (all parts) 8-16 hours

Plan your print schedule. Start the longest prints (chassis) first, overnight if possible. Print smaller parts during the day when you can monitor them.

docs.title: Post-Processing
docs.text:
After printing:

  1. Remove supports carefully (use flush cutters)
  2. Clean up stringing with a heat gun or knife
  3. Test-fit all parts before assembly — if holes are too tight, drill them out slightly. If too loose, add a layer of tape or print a tighter version
  4. Sand mating surfaces if needed for better fit
  5. Label your parts — write on them with a marker so you know what goes where

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: Your parts are printed — now it's time to set up your electronics. Head to the Malenki Nano Setup guide to wire and configure your control board.

  • SSP CAD files: 15-20 min (GrabCAD account + download)
  • CAD screenshots: 10-15 min
  • Weapon physics diagrams: 15-20 min (Ask Aaron + alternatives)

Total: 70-100 minutes (~1.5 hours)
